U7 / Initiation.why: Good teammates stay close and ready — that way the puck carrier has someone to pass to. You don't want to get too close though!
U7 / Initiation.tip: Keeping possession of the puck is important.
U7 / Initiation.q: You have the puck and the defender is coming to try and get it. What should you do?

spots.msg: Pass it to your teammate-yes

spots.label: Pass
spots.msg: You don't want to pass it to the other team!

U7 / Initiation.sit: What is the goalie's job?
opts: To stop the puck from going in the net.
To make sure their team wins.
To sit on the bench.
To do snow angels when the puck is on the other part of the rink.
U7 / Initiation.why: The goalie's job is to stop the puck from going in the net. That's the most important save!

U7 / Initiation.sit: Your teammate passes you the puck. What should you do first?
opts: Try to stop it with your stick and look up.
Kick it with your foot.
Give it back to the other team.
Sit down with it.
Eat it.
U7 / Initiation.why: Stop the puck first, then look up to see where your teammates are and to read the defense.

U7 / Initiation.sit: If you fall down, you should stay on the ice and wait.
U7 / Initiation.why: Hockey players fall a lot! The best ones get up fast and keep playing.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Fall down, get up, keep skating.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Your coach is talking to the team. What should you do?
opts: Listen and look at the coach.
Skate in circles.
Talk to your friend.
Close your eyes.
U7 / Initiation.why: When you listen to your coach, you learn how to play better. That's how you get good.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You lose the puck to the other team. What should you do?
opts: Try to get it back.
Go to the bench.
Stop skating.
Give up.
U7 / Initiation.why: When you lose the puck, work hard to get it back. Good hockey players don't give up — they work to get it back.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Lost the puck? Go get it back

U7 / Initiation.sit: When you shoot, where should you aim?
opts: At an empty spot in the net.
Right at the goalie's chest.
Over the glass.
At your teammate.
U7 / Initiation.why: Shoot where the goalie isn't. If there's an empty spot, aim there!

U7 / Initiation.sit: A good teammate cheers when their team scores a goal--even when it isn't them.
U7 / Initiation.why: Yes! Good teammates celebrate together, no matter who scored. The whole team did it.

U7 / Initiation.sit: What's the best way to get better at hockey?
opts: Practice lots.
Watch a lot of TV.
Eat candy before games.
Complain a lot.
U7 / Initiation.why: Hockey is a skill — the more you practice, the better you get. Every pro player practiced a lot when they were little.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You should always wear your helmet when you're on the ice.
U7 / Initiation.why: Helmets protect your head. Always wear one — even at practice.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Forwards mostly try to...
opts: Score goals.
Guard the net.
Sit on the bench.
Skate backward.
U7 / Initiation.why: Forwards are the players who try to score. Defense players try to stop the other team from scoring.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Defense players (D-men) mostly try to...
opts: Stop the other team from scoring.
Score lots of goals.
Sit on the bench.
Jump over the boards.
U7 / Initiation.why: D-men protect their own net. They stop the other team from getting the puck close to the goalie. They often don't score as much as forwards, but play a very important part in the success of the team.

U7 / Initiation.sit: If your team is losing, it's okay to give up.
U7 / Initiation.why: Never give up! Try your best every shift. That's what real hockey players do.

U7 / Initiation.sit: When you're skating with the puck, you should...
opts: Keep your head up and look around.
Look at the puck the whole time.
Close your eyes.
Look at the ceiling.
U7 / Initiation.why: Looking up helps you see your teammates and the other team. If you only watch the puck, you might skate into someone!
U7 / Initiation.tip: Head up when you have the puck.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Your teammate falls down during a shift. What should you do?
opts: Keep playing — they'll get up.
Stop and sit down too.
Skate to the bench and hide.
Throw your stick.
U7 / Initiation.why: Teammates fall all the time. You keep playing. They'll get right back up.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You and your teammate are right next to each other chasing the same puck. What should you do?
opts: One chase, the other skate to open ice.
Both chase the puck together.
Both go to the bench.
Bump into each other.
U7 / Initiation.why: Good teams spread out. One chases, one gets open for a pass.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You have the puck. Your teammate is wide open. What's the best choice?
opts: Pass to your open teammate.
Try to skate through three defenders.
Shoot from way too far away.
Stop and look at your skates.
U7 / Initiation.why: Open teammates are gifts. Pass it! Passing is how teams score lots of goals.

U7 / Initiation.sit: When the game ends, you should say 'good game' to the other team — even if you lost.
U7 / Initiation.why: Yep. Every player says 'good game' after hockey. That's being a good sport.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Which one helps you get better at hockey?
opts: Trying new things at practice.
Doing the same easy drill every time.
Skipping practice.
Not listening to coach.
U7 / Initiation.why: You grow when you try hard things. Safe-and-easy feels nice but doesn't make you better.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Try the hard stuff at practice.

U7 / Initiation.sit: It's okay to skate with your skates only kind of tied.
U7 / Initiation.why: No way! Loose skates hurt your ankles and make you slow. Tie them tight every time.

U7 / Initiation.sit: The ref drops the puck for a face-off. Where should you be?
opts: Ready in your spot, stick on the ice.
Skating in a big circle.
Over by your bench.
Talking to the goalie.
U7 / Initiation.why: Face-offs start fast. Be ready — stick down, eyes on the puck.

U7 / Initiation.sit: When you pass the puck, you should pass it to...
opts: Your teammate's stick.
Somewhere random.
At their helmet.
Way behind them.
U7 / Initiation.why: Aim for your friend's stick. That way they can grab it quick and keep playing.

U7 / Initiation.sit: If you don't understand what coach wants, you can ask.
U7 / Initiation.why: Absolutely. Asking a question is smart. Coaches love it when you want to learn.

U7 / Initiation.sit: What does a team captain do?
opts: Helps teammates and works hard.
Yells at everyone.
Does whatever they want.
Skips practice.
U7 / Initiation.why: Captains lead by being a good teammate. They help, they hustle, they listen.

U7 / Initiation.sit: A friend on your team tries their best but misses a pass. What do you say?
opts: 'Good try — next one!'
'That was awful.'
Nothing — ignore them.
'You're bad at hockey.'
U7 / Initiation.why: Teammates pick each other up. Everyone misses. 'Next one' keeps your friend going strong.

U7 / Initiation.sit: The other team just scored. What do you do next shift?
opts: Try even harder.
Give up.
Cry about it.
Tell coach the game isn't fair.
U7 / Initiation.why: Scored on? That's hockey. Best answer is to skate harder and score one right back.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You notice a big crack in your helmet before a game. What do you do?
opts: Tell your coach or parent right away.
Wear it anyway.
Hide it so you can still play.
Glue it with tape.
U7 / Initiation.why: Cracked helmets don't protect you. Tell a grown-up. They'll get you a good one before you skate.

U7 / Initiation.sit: When you're a defender in your own end, you should just stand still in front of the net.
U7 / Initiation.why: Defenders have to move! You follow the puck and the players. Standing still lets the other team score.

U7 / Initiation.sit: At your age, which shot is the best one to practice first?
opts: A wrist shot — stick on the ice.
A huge slap shot like in the NHL.
A shot with your eyes closed.
A shot with no stick.
U7 / Initiation.why: Wrist shots are fast, accurate, and easy to learn. Big slap shots come later when you're bigger.

U7 / Initiation.sit: It's okay to make mistakes at practice — that's how you learn.
U7 / Initiation.why: Yes! Practice is exactly where you should try hard things and mess up. Every mistake teaches you something.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You have the puck. Your teammate is open and yelling your name. What should you do?
opts: Look to pass it to them.
Ignore them.
Shoot even though the net is blocked.
Skate to the bench.
U7 / Initiation.why: Teammates yell when they're open. If you hear your name, look up — and pass if you can.

U7 / Initiation.sit: The game is tied near the end. What should your next shift look like?
opts: Super hard work — skate fast, hustle everywhere.
Easy skating.
Pretend you're tired.
Sit on the ice.
U7 / Initiation.why: Close games are won by the team that works hardest. That's you!

U7 / Initiation.sit: The puck is in the middle of the rink — between the two blue lines. You should...
opts: Skate with speed toward the puck.
Stay by your own net.
Skate in small circles.
Stop and watch.
U7 / Initiation.why: The middle of the rink is open ice — a great place to get moving and join the play.

U7 / Initiation.sit: The ref blows the whistle while you're playing. What do you do?
opts: Stop skating and find the ref.
Keep skating fast.
Shoot the puck into the net anyway.
Yell at the other team.
U7 / Initiation.why: Whistle means stop. Pause, look at the ref, wait for the next face-off.

U7 / Initiation.sit: When you're close to the goalie, it's smart to take a quick shot instead of stickhandling forever.
U7 / Initiation.why: Yes! The longer you stickhandle, the more time the goalie has to get ready. Quick shots catch them off guard.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Your shift is almost over. What's the best way to change lines?
opts: Skate hard to the bench and hop over.
Stay on forever.
Lay down on the ice until someone helps.
Wait for the whistle.
U7 / Initiation.why: Good line changes are fast and on the fly. Hustle to the bench so your teammate can jump on.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Your stick accidentally bumps another player. What do you do?
opts: Say sorry and keep playing.
Laugh about it.
Do it again on purpose.
Pretend you didn't see them.
U7 / Initiation.why: Accidents happen. Good players say sorry and move on. It shows respect.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Shaking hands at the end of the game is an important hockey tradition.
U7 / Initiation.why: It sure is! Every hockey player — from U7 to the NHL — shakes hands after the game.

U7 / Initiation.q: Tap the SLOT — the best spot to try to score.
U7 / Initiation.tip: The slot is right in front of the net — that's where goals happen.

zones.msg: ✓ That's the slot — front of the net, prime scoring area.
That's center ice. Too far from the net to score from.
That's the corner. Tough angle to score from.

U7 / Initiation.sit: You're carrying the puck through the middle of the ice. Where should your eyes be?
opts: Up, looking at the ice ahead and your teammates
Down, watching the puck on your stick blade
On the scoreboard checking the time
Closed so you can focus on stickhandling
U7 / Initiation.why: Eyes up is the foundation of every other hockey skill. When your eyes are up you can see teammates, defenders, and open ice. Looking at the puck means you'll skate straight into trouble.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Eyes up is the foundation of every other hockey skill.
U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: Up, looking at the ice ahead and your teammates

U7 / Initiation.sit: What does an athletic hockey stance look like?
opts: Standing tall with straight legs and stick raised
Sitting flat on the ice waiting for the puck
Knees bent, hips low, weight on the balls of your feet, stick on the ice
Leaning all your weight on one skate
U7 / Initiation.why: Athletic stance is the foundation of every move you make on the ice — stops, starts, crossovers, contact. Knees bent, weight balanced, stick down.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Athletic stance is the foundation of every move you make on the ice — stops, starts, crossovers, contact.

media.alt: Knees are bent, hips are low, weight is centered over the balls of the feet — ready to move in any direction.

U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: Knees bent, hips low, weight on the balls of your feet, stick on the ice

U7 / Initiation.sit: Why do good hockey players bend their knees?
opts: It looks cool
It lets them move quicker, stop faster, and stay strong on the puck
It makes them shorter so the goalie can't see them
It stops the ice from being slippery
U7 / Initiation.why: Bent knees mean you can push off in any direction in a fraction of a second. A tall, stiff player can only go where they're already pointed.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Bent knees mean you can push off in any direction in a fraction of a second.

media.alt: Knees are bent, hips are low, weight is centered over the balls of the feet — ready to move in any direction.

U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: It lets them move quicker, stop faster, and stay strong on the puck

U7 / Initiation.sit: You're open and want a teammate to pass you the puck. Where should your stick be?
opts: Up high above your head
Flat on the ice where you want the pass
Behind your skates
Tucked into your glove
U7 / Initiation.why: Your stick blade on the ice is a target. It tells your teammate exactly where to put the pass — and it's already in the right spot to catch it.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Your stick blade on the ice is a target.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice presenting a clear target where the player wants to receive the puck.

U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: Flat on the ice where you want the pass

U7 / Initiation.sit: The puck is about to hit your stick. What should your hands do?
opts: Push the stick forward to slap the puck
Pull the stick back a little — like you're catching an egg
Squeeze the stick as tight as you can
Let go of the stick
U7 / Initiation.why: Soft hands cushion the pass. Pulling the stick back at the moment of contact takes energy out of the puck so it stays on your blade instead of bouncing off.

media.alt: The puck is meeting the stick blade; the receiver's hands are giving — pulling slightly back with the puck — instead of stabbing at it.

U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: Pull the stick back a little — like you're catching an egg

U7 / Initiation.sit: What does giving with your hands when you receive a pass actually do?
opts: Makes the pass faster
Surprises the goalie
Takes the bounce out of the puck so it stays on your stick
Keeps your gloves clean
U7 / Initiation.why: A stiff blade is like a wall — the puck bounces off. A soft, giving blade is like a glove — the puck sticks. Same idea as a fielder catching a baseball.
U7 / Initiation.tip: A stiff blade is like a wall — the puck bounces off.

media.alt: The puck is meeting the stick blade; the receiver's hands are giving — pulling slightly back with the puck — instead of stabbing at it.

U7 / Initiation._correctAnswerText: Takes the bounce out of the puck so it stays on your stick

🤝 Pass to my friend
💨 Skate fast

U7 / Initiation.tip: Look at the player in black — his stick is on YOUR side.

U7 / Initiation.tip: If you see a wide open net, it might be now or never.

U7 / Initiation.sit: Look at the picture. You have the puck and lots of room. What should you do?
opts: 🥅 Shoot it now
🤝 Pass to my friend now
💨 Skate with the puck
U7 / Initiation.why: Look at all the open ice! The black player is far away. You have lots of time. Skate toward the net with the puck.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Look at all the open ice.

media.alt: The composition itself is the read. The defender is positioned deep (25–30 feet ahead, near the hash marks), creating a wide expanse of open ice between the viewer and the defender. There is visible open ice on BOTH sides of the defender — the cross-ice passing lane AND the lane down the middle toward the goalie are both somewhat available. The defender hasn't committed because he's too far back to commit. The correct read is to attack the open ice, force him to commit, then react.

U7 / Initiation.why: That's the open ice! Skate the puck into all that space. You don't have to pass or shoot right away when you have lots of room.

🤝 Pass to my friend FAST
💨 Slow down

U7 / Initiation.tip: The black player is right in front of you and skating fast.

U7 / Initiation.tip: The black player is RIGHT THERE.
