U7 / Initiation.why: The first reaction after losing the puck is to compete and chase. The longer you wait, the harder it gets to win it back.
U7 / Initiation.tip: First step after a turnover: chase.
U7 / Initiation.sit: Their player just took the puck off your stick. What do you do right now?
opts: Skate hard and try to win it back.
Stand still and yell for help loud.
Glide back to where you were before.
Wait and see what your teammates do.

U7 / Initiation.why: When you stop skating after a turnover, the other team has open lanes to your goalie. Chase back so they don't get a clean look.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Stop skating = you give up a chance.
U7 / Initiation.sit: If you stop skating after losing the puck, what usually happens?
opts: They pass the puck back to your team.
They get an easy chance on your net.
The play gets stopped by a whistle.
They stop skating and wait for you.

U7 / Initiation.why: Puck near your net is the most dangerous spot. The closest player pressures right away — don't wait for someone else.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Puck in your zone? Pressure now.
U7 / Initiation.sit: They steal the puck near your own net. What's the right move?
opts: Skate over and tell your bench coach.
Watch the puck and see what happens.
Backcheck and put pressure on the puck.
Wait at center ice for a pass back.

U7 / Initiation.why: Getting bumped off is part of hockey. Good players get up and keep competing — winning the puck back matters more than the bump.
U7 / Initiation.tip: Get knocked off? Get up. Chase.
U7 / Initiation.sit: A bigger player knocks you off the puck. What's the smart next play?
opts: Tell the ref it was a bad foul.
Skate to the bench because you're tired.
Stand still and watch the next play.
Get up and chase the puck again hard.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just got the puck near center ice. There are two nets. What's the FIRST thing you do?
opts: Skate as fast as you can any direction.
Look up and find which net is yours.
Pass to the nearest player.
Spin around and wait.
U9 / Novice.why: Before anything else — look up. A one-second glance tells you which way you're going. The habit of looking first is the most important skill in hockey.
U9 / Novice.tip: Got the puck? Look up first. Every time.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is loose in the corner and nobody is near it. What do you do?
opts: Wait and see who gets it.
Skate hard toward the puck.
Stay put and call for it.
Go stand in front of the net.
U9 / Novice.why: Loose pucks belong to whoever wants them most. Going hard after every loose puck is one of the most important habits you can build.
U9 / Novice.tip: Loose puck? Go get it. Every time. That's compete.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck and a teammate is wide open beside you with no defender near them. What's the best play?
U9 / Novice.why: Hockey is a team game. Open teammate + blocked path = pass. This is the beginning of understanding that passing can be smarter than carrying.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open teammate + your path blocked = pass. That's real hockey thinking.
opts: Pass the puck to your open teammate.
Try to skate around all the defenders yourself.
Shoot the puck wide to get a faceoff.
Wait for your teammate to come closer.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're close to the net with the puck. Nobody is right on you. What's the best thing to do?
opts: Skate back to center ice.
Shoot at the net.
Pass far across to a teammate.
Wait for a defender to come.
U9 / Novice.why: When you're close to the net and open — shoot! Every shot makes the goalie work. Goals come from shooting, not thinking about shooting.
U9 / Novice.tip: Close to the net and open? Shoot. Always.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team has the puck and is skating toward your net. What do you do?
opts: Skate to center ice and wait for a pass.
Skate back toward your net to help defend on the backcheck.
Stand still and watch the play go past you.
Go talk to your coach about a line change.
U9 / Novice.why: When the other team has the puck and is coming your way, your job is to turn around and get back. This is called backchecking.
U9 / Novice.tip: Other team coming? Turn around and skate back hard.

U9 / Novice.sit: Five teammates are all bunched in one corner chasing the puck. What's a smart thing to do?
opts: Join the pile and chase the puck even harder.
Find empty ice somewhere else and get open.
Skate off the ice and ask for a line change.
Stay exactly where you are and call for a pass.
U9 / Novice.why: Hockey players shouldn't all go to the same spot. Finding open ice gives your team options.
U9 / Novice.tip: Everyone's in the corner? Go somewhere else and get open.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach is explaining a drill and you're not sure what to do. What's the best thing?
opts: Just start skating and hope you guess the drill right.
Listen carefully, then ask what you didn't understand.
Do whatever the kid next to you is doing every time.
Sit down on the ice until someone comes to help you.
U9 / Novice.why: Coaches give instructions to help you succeed. Listening well and asking questions is smart, not weak.
U9 / Novice.tip: Confused? Listen first, then ask. That's how you get better.

U9 / Novice.sit: You fell down on the ice during a play. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Falling down happens to every player. Getting up fast and staying in the play is compete. The game doesn't stop — neither do you.
U9 / Novice.tip: Fell down? Get up fast. Keep going.
opts: Stay down so you don't get in anyone's way.
Get up as quickly as you can and keep playing.
Wait for the whistle before getting up.
Crawl to the bench.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team just shot the puck and the goalie caught it. The other team is going to start a breakout. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: After your team's shot is saved, the puck changes possession. Getting back toward your own net right away gets you in position to defend.
U9 / Novice.tip: Your shot got saved? Start getting back right away.
opts: Stop skating — the play will probably stop anyway.
Skate hard back into your defensive zone to help.
Skate slowly back so you don't waste energy.
Yell at the referee to call a whistle.

U9 / Novice.sit: If your coach shows a new drill you've never done before, it's better to refuse than to try and look bad.
U9 / Novice.why: New drills feel hard at first for everyone. Trying your best — even if you make mistakes — is how you get better. Every great player was a beginner once.
U9 / Novice.tip: New drill? Try it. Mistakes are how you learn.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. Your teammate has the puck in the corner. You're standing far away at center ice. What would be the better place to skate to?
opts: Stay at center ice and wait for the puck to come back out.
Skate to the front of the net — right in front of the goalie.
Skate to the same corner as your teammate to help them out.
Skate all the way to the other end of the rink and wait for it.
U9 / Novice.why: When a teammate has the puck in the corner, the front of the net is where you can help most. A pass to you there is the most dangerous play, and you're right there for any rebound.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate in the corner? You go to the net front. That's your spot.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is skating into the other team's zone. You get there before the puck does. What happens?
opts: Nothing — you got there first, that's good.
The referee calls offsides and play stops.
You score automatically.
The other team gets to shoot.
U9 / Novice.why: If you enter the offensive zone before the puck, your team gets called for offsides and play stops. The puck always has to go in first.
U9 / Novice.tip: Wait for the puck at the blue line. You can't go in before it.

U9 / Novice.sit: You and an opponent both reach a loose puck along the boards at the same time. What do you do?
opts: Stop skating and let them have the whole puck.
Use your body to shield the puck and fight to keep it.
Skate away quickly and hope a teammate gets to it.
Fall down on the ice and hope the ref stops it.
U9 / Novice.why: Puck battles are won by the player who competes harder. Using your body to create a wall between the opponent and the puck is a real skill.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck battle: get your body between the opponent and the puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're on a 3-on-1 rush — three of your players against one defender. How should you attack?
U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out on a rush forces the defender to make an impossible choice. A tight group lets one player cover all three.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush attack: spread out. Make the defender choose.
opts: All three skate side-by-side close together.
All three skate single file straight at the goalie.
Spread out wide so the defender can't cover everyone.
Two go to the net and one wait at the blue line.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team has the puck in the corner of your defensive zone. You're the defender in front of the net. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: When your defender goes to the corner, you cover the front of the net. If you both go to the corner, the most dangerous spot on the ice is completely empty.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defender goes to the corner? You go to the net front. Always.
opts: Leave the front and chase the puck in the corner.
Stay in front of the net and protect that area.
Skate to the bench and yell for a teammate.
Stand by the blue line so you're ready to attack.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is tired and you just want a break. You shoot the puck all the way down the ice from your own end. What happens?
U9 / Novice.why: Shooting the puck from your own side of center all the way down the ice without it being touched is called icing. Play stops and comes back — no line change allowed.
U9 / Novice.tip: Icing = no break. If you need a line change, do it the right way.
opts: Nothing — play continues.
Icing is called and the faceoff comes back to your zone.
The other team gets a penalty.
Your team gets a line change.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in the offensive zone without the puck. Your teammate has it. Three of their teammates are all crowded around them. What should you do?
opts: Go join the crowd and chase the puck with your teammates near the boards.
Find an open space away from the crowd — near the net or at the blue line.
Stand still in your spot and wait for someone to pass you the puck there.
Skate to the bench and take a quick rest while your teammates keep playing.
U9 / Novice.why: Crowding around the puck carrier makes it harder for them, not easier. Finding open ice gives them a passing option and stretches the defense.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate has the puck and everyone's crowding them? Go find open ice. Give them an option.

U9 / Novice.sit: A puck is sliding toward the boards. You and an opponent are racing to it. You're slightly behind them. What's the smart play as you approach?
opts: Give up on the race — they'll probably beat you to it by a mile anyway.
Angle your body to get to the inside position before they reach the puck.
Skate straight at them as fast as you can and try to knock them off it.
Stop skating altogether and call loudly for a teammate to come help you out.
U9 / Novice.why: Getting to the inside position — between your opponent and the puck — before they reach it gives you the advantage even if they arrive first.
U9 / Novice.tip: Race to a puck: focus on getting inside position, not just getting there first.

U9 / Novice.sit: When you're on the bench, you should watch the play and learn from what's happening on the ice.
U9 / Novice.why: Watching the game from the bench isn't just resting learning. Players who pay attention to the game while on the bench come back on the ice knowing what's happening.
U9 / Novice.tip: On the bench? Watch the game. Learn what's happening before you go back on.

U9 / Novice.sit: You shoot the puck at the net. The goalie doesn't quite catch it — the puck bounces off them. What should you do?
opts: Skate away — you already took your one s.
Chase the rebound and try to shoot again.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Just wait for the referee to blow it dead.
U9 / Novice.why: Rebounds from your own shot are one of the easiest scoring chances in hockey. Chasing your shot to the net means you might get a second chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Follow your shot. Rebounds are your friend.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending in your end of the ice. The puck is in the corner far away from you. Should you just watch the puck?
opts: Yes — always watch where the puck is and skate toward it no matter where other players are.
No — find the player near you and keep them in front of you. You can still see the puck with your eyes.
Yes — the puck is the most important thing and nothing else on the ice really matters for you at all.
Yes — skate straight to where the puck is and forget about all the other players on the ice during a play.
U9 / Novice.why: In your defensive zone, every defender needs to cover a player — not just watch the puck. If you lose your check, they could get an open pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defending in your own zone? Find a player to cover. Watch them AND the puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: You've been skating hard for a long time and your legs are getting tired. What's the right move?
U9 / Novice.why: Changing lines keeps your team fresh. When there's a stoppage and you're tired, getting off the ice and letting a fresh teammate on helps your team.
U9 / Novice.tip: Tired during a stoppage? Get to the bench. Let a fresh teammate in.
opts: Keep playing — you don't want to come off.
Skate to the bench and change before you cost your team.
Stand still and wait for the whistle.
Skate slowly so you can stay on the ice longer.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is trying to get the puck but they're getting pushed around by an opponent. You're nearby and don't have the puck. What can you do to help?
opts: Watch — there's nothing you can do without the puck so just wait and let them handle it.
Skate toward your teammate — being close creates support and could help them make a play.
Skate to the other end of the rink and wait there for the puck to come down to you.
Call out to the referee and ask them to blow the whistle and stop the play.
U9 / Novice.why: Being close to a teammate who's in a battle gives them a passing option and moral support. Hockey is about playing together — be there for your teammates.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate in a battle? Get close and be their option. Support them.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're about to shoot the puck at the net. The goalie is standing in the middle. Where's the best place to aim?
opts: Right at the goalie's chest — they'll move out of the way.
Try to shoot around them — aim for a corner of the net.
Aim high for the crossbar every single time you shoot.
Shoot as hard as you can without ever taking time to aim.
U9 / Novice.why: Shooting right at the goalie makes their job easy. Aiming for the corners of the net — where the goalie isn't — gives you a much better chance to score.
U9 / Novice.tip: Aiming for the net? Pick a corner. Shoot where the goalie isn't.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player on your team does something against the rules and gets a penalty. What does that mean for your team?
U9 / Novice.why: When your team takes a penalty, you have to play shorthanded — with fewer players. This is why avoiding penalties is important. The other team has an advantage until the penalty is over.
U9 / Novice.tip: Your team has a penalty? You're playing with fewer players. Everyone has to work harder.
opts: Nothing changes.
Your team plays short-handed and the other team has a power play.
The other team also has to take a penalty.
Your team gets a free shot on net.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player from the other team is about to shoot the puck at your net. You're between them and the net. What could you try to do?
opts: Move out of the way so the puck doesn't hit you or any of your teammates.
Get in the shooting lane and try to block the shot with your body or stick.
Skate to your bench and let your goalie handle the shot all on their own again.
Turn around and look away fast so the puck can't hit you right in your face m.
U9 / Novice.why: Blocking shots is a way every player can help their team. Getting in the lane between the shooter and your net might deflect or block the shot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Player about to shoot? Try to get in the lane and block it.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is rushing toward the other team's net — you have more players than the other team right now. Where should you be on the ice to help most?
opts: Skate as close to the puck carrier as you possibly can every time.
Spread across the ice so the defenders have to cover more space.
Skate behind the puck carrier the whole way and follow them.
Stop skating and watch the puck carrier handle it by themselves.
U9 / Novice.why: Spreading out on a rush forces defenders to cover more space, opening up passing lanes and shooting opportunities.
U9 / Novice.tip: Rush attack? Spread across the ice. Give the defenders more to think about.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're in a great open spot in the offensive zone with no defenders near you. Your teammate has the puck but hasn't seen you. What do you do?
opts: Stay quiet — yelling for the puck is rude to the other team's players.
Call your teammate's name to let them know you're open.
Skate toward them quickly to get the puck yourself and try to score it.
Wait patiently and hope they turn around and spot you all by themselves.
U9 / Novice.why: Letting your teammate know you're open is the right play. A loud 'here!' gives them a target and might lead directly to a scoring chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open and your teammate can't see you? Call for the puck. Let them know.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate is shooting the puck at the net from the blue line. You're the nearest forward. What should you do?
opts: Stay where you are — the shot might just go wide of the net.
Skate hard toward the net to get in position for a rebound shot.
Skate away from the net in case the shot ends up going wide.
Skate to the corner and wait for the puck to come.
U9 / Novice.why: Shots from the blue line often produce rebounds. Getting to the front of the net before the shot arrives puts you in position to score on the rebound.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate shooting from far away? Crash the net. Be ready for the rebound.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck goes over the boards and out of play. What happens?
U9 / Novice.why: When the puck leaves the playing surface, play stops. The game restarts with a faceoff at the nearest faceoff dot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck over the boards? Play stops. Get ready for the faceoff.
opts: Play continues until someone touches the puck.
The referee blows the whistle and there's a faceoff.
The other team gets a penalty.
The clock keeps running but no one can score.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is in the offensive zone corner. Four of your teammates are chasing it in the corner. The entire front of the net and the blue line are empty. What should you do?
opts: Join the crowd — the more teammates fighting for the puck in one spot the better for your team again.
Pick one of the empty spots — net front or blue line — and get there. Give your team an option.
Skate to the bench right away and take a quick rest while your teammates fight for the puck for you.
Watch and wait for one of your teammates to pass the puck to you right there in the corner they are.
U9 / Novice.why: When everyone goes to the same spot, all the other spots open up. Finding the empty space — net front, blue line — gives your team a passing option and makes the defense split.
U9 / Novice.tip: Everyone in the corner? Pick the empty spot. You're more useful there.

U9 / Novice.sit: After a drill, your coach tells you something you did wrong and how to fix it. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Feedback from coaches is how you get better. A coach who corrects you is investing in you. Listening, saying thank you, and trying the correction is exactly what the best players do.
U9 / Novice.tip: Coach gave you feedback? Say thanks and try it their way. That's how you improve.
opts: Tell the coach you already knew that.
Listen and try to do it better next time.
Get upset and skip the next drill.
Ignore it — your coach is wrong.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your team loses, it's a teammate's fault if they made a mistake and you should let them know.
U9 / Novice.why: Losing is part of sports. Being a good teammate means supporting each other even when things go wrong. Encouraging a sad teammate is one of the most important things you can do.
U9 / Novice.tip: Team lost and a teammate is sad? Encourage them. You're in it together.

U9 / Novice.sit: A teammate takes a shot from far away. The puck is coming at the net. You're standing right in front of the goalie. What could you try?
opts: Move out of the way so the puck doesn't hit you and so your goalie can try to save the shot.
Try to tip or deflect the puck with your stick — even a small touch can change where it goes.
Skate away fast toward the blue line so you don't get in the way of your teammate's shot on net.
Watch the shot very carefully to see if it goes right in the net or the goalie makes a big save.
U9 / Novice.why: Deflecting or tipping a shot is one of the hardest things for goalies to stop. Even a small touch from your stick can change the puck's direction enough to score.
U9 / Novice.tip: Shot coming and you're in front? Try to tip it. Even a little touch helps.

U9 / Novice.sit: If an attacker is standing in front of your goalie, leaving them alone is the best plan because the goalie can see around them.
U9 / Novice.why: An attacker screening your goalie is a real problem. Moving them out of the crease area is the defender's job so the goalie can see the puck coming.
U9 / Novice.tip: Attacker in front of your goalie? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U9 / Novice.sit: When there's a faceoff near your net, you should line up exactly where your coach told you and be ready as soon as the puck drops.
U9 / Novice.why: Faceoffs are set plays. Everyone has a position — you're not just watching the center take the draw. Being in position before the puck drops means your team is ready to win the faceoff and execute.
U9 / Novice.tip: About to have a faceoff? Get to your position before the puck drops. Be ready.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team has a 2-on-1 — two of you and only one defender. You don't have the puck. Your teammate does. What should you do?
opts: Stop skating and watch your teammate handle the whole play all by themselves — they've got it from here.
Skate into open space — go to the side where the defender isn't. Give your teammate a pass option.
Skate straight toward your teammate and get right next to them so you can help them handle the puck.
Skate straight to the bench and take a break — your teammate can handle this 2-on-1 rush all by themselves.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the player without the puck needs to get to the open side. If the defender takes your teammate, you're wide open for the pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 without the puck? Get to the open side. Be the option.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck right in front of the net, 5 feet away. The goalie has stepped far out of the net to cut the angle. There's barely any net to shoot at. What do you do?
opts: Shoot anyway — just blast it right at the goalie's chest.
Try to go around the goalie since they've come so far out.
Stop skating and pass it back to a teammate behind you.
Skate backward away from the net and try a better angle.
U9 / Novice.why: When a goalie comes way out to cut the angle, going around them or pulling the puck wide and shooting on an open net is often better than trying to beat them on the shot.
U9 / Novice.tip: Goalie way out cutting the angle? Go around them or pull wide. Don't just blast it at their chest.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending in your zone. The puck is in the corner. You see an opposing player standing in the slot with nobody covering them. What do you do?
opts: Watch the puck in the corner where the action is and where all eyes on the ice should be looking during the play.
Cover the open player in the slot — they're in the most dangerous area and if they get a pass, it'll likely be a goal.
Skate to the corner where the puck is and help your teammates battle for it so your team gets the puck back.
Stand way back at the blue line and wait for the puck to come out of the corner so you can grab it and skate up the ice.
U9 / Novice.why: An uncovered player in the slot is the biggest danger on the ice. Covering them — even when the puck is in the corner — prevents the most dangerous play from happening.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open player in the slot? Cover them immediately. The slot is the most dangerous spot.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your team is attacking the other team's net, both defensemen should skate deep into the offensive zone to help score.
U9 / Novice.why: When both defensemen go too deep, there's nobody to stop the other team from breaking out quickly. At least one defenseman should always be thinking about the space behind the rush.
U9 / Novice.tip: Both D going deep into the attack? Dangerous. Someone needs to be ready for the other team to break out.

U9 / Novice.concept: Don't all go to the puck
U9 / Novice.sit: Three players from the other team are in your defensive zone. Two of your defenders are fighting for the puck in the corner. A third opposing player is wide open near the net. What should you — a forward — do?
opts: Skate into the corner to help your teammates battle for the puck even though they got it.
Cover the open player near the net — they're in a dangerous position and nobody has them.
Watch the whole play happen from center ice until the puck comes out of the corner to you.
Skate all the way back toward the blue line and wait for the puck to come to you there.
U9 / Novice.why: When two teammates are already in the corner, you don't need to be a third player there. The open player near the net is the danger — cover them.
U9 / Novice.tip: Two teammates already in the corner? Cover the open player near the net. Don't all go to the puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're feeling frustrated and you want to hit the other player after the whistle. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Taking a penalty doesn't just punish you — it puts your whole team at a disadvantage. Learning to control your frustration is an important skill that the best players have.
U9 / Novice.tip: Feeling frustrated? Control it. A penalty hurts your whole team.
opts: Hit them anyway — they deserve it.
Skate away. Penalties hurt your team because you have to play short-handed.
Yell at them and the referee.
Throw your stick.

U9 / Novice.sit: You made a mistake — you passed the puck to the other team by accident and they scored. Your teammates are looking at you. What do you do?
opts: Blame someone else on your team for the mistake you just made out there.
Apologize a lot and then feel really terrible about it for the rest of the game.
Put your head up and keep going — everyone makes mistakes. Focus on the next play.
Stop trying to make any more passes to your teammates for the rest of the hockey game.
U9 / Novice.why: Mistakes happen to every player in every game. The best response is to acknowledge it quickly and refocus on the next play. Staying in the moment instead of dwelling on a mistake is a real skill.
U9 / Novice.tip: Made a mistake? Head up. Next play. Everyone does it.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your teammate has the puck and they're trying to skate with it but defenders are around them. You're 10 feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Stand completely still right there — you don't have the puck so there's nothing you can do to help your teammate at this very mome.
Skate to a spot that supports them with your positioning and be the easy pass option.
Skate toward them right away to make the space around the puck carrier a lot tighter and force the defenders closer to everyone.
Skate all the way down to the other end of the ice so you can be ready to grab the puck when it comes back down to you.
U9 / Novice.why: Supporting the puck carrier by positioning yourself behind and to the side gives them an easy outlet if they get pressured. You don't need the puck to help your teammate.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate with the puck? Position yourself behind them to the side. Be the easy option.

U9 / Novice.sit: If you disagree with your coach, the right way to handle it is to ask politely after the drill — not to argue during it.
U9 / Novice.why: Coaches make decisions based on more information than you see. Trying their way first — then asking questions respectfully — is how good players and good people handle disagreement.
U9 / Novice.tip: Disagree with your coach? Try their way first. Ask questions respectfully after. That's how you build trust.

U9 / Novice.sit: The other team takes a penalty. Your team is now on the power play. What does that mean?
U9 / Novice.why: A power play means the other team has fewer players — so someone on your team is always open. Moving the puck to find that open player is how power plays create scoring chances.
U9 / Novice.tip: On the power play? You have the extra skater. Move the puck to find them.
opts: Your team has to play short-handed.
Both teams play with the same number of skaters.
Your team has more skaters on the ice than the other team.
The game is paused for one minute.

U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. You're not sure if you should shoot or pass. What's the most important thing to think about?
opts: Which option looks the coolest to all your friends, teammates, and parents who are watching you from the stands.
Which option is most likely to get the puck on net — either a shot or a pass to a teammate with a better shot.
Whatever your parents and the rest of your family members are probably thinking right now from the stands.
How many people are watching the hockey game right now from both the stands and the benches around the ice.
U9 / Novice.why: The best decision in the offensive zone is the one most likely to put the puck on the net. Sometimes that's shooting, sometimes that's passing to a teammate with a better look. Always think about getting the puck on net.
U9 / Novice.tip: Shoot or pass? Pick whichever one is most likely to get the puck on net.

U9 / Novice.sit: You've been playing hard and you're tired. You have one more shift left. What do you do?
U9 / Novice.why: Your last shift of practice should look like your first. Players who compete hard even when tired build real toughness — and coaches notice every shift, especially the last ones.
U9 / Novice.tip: Tired on your last shift? Go just as hard as your first. That's the standard.
opts: Coast the rest of the shift — you've earned it.
Skate hard the whole shift — every shift counts.
Skate to the bench early.
Stand still in the neutral zone.

U9 / Novice.sit: Warming up before practice is a waste of time because you're going to skate during practice anyway.
U9 / Novice.why: Warm-ups protect your body even when you feel fine. Coaches build warm-ups because they help prevent injuries and prepare your muscles for the work ahead. Following the team routine is part of being coachable.
U9 / Novice.tip: Want to skip the warm-up? Do it anyway. Your coach included it for a reason.

U9 / Novice.concept: Use both sides of your stick
U9 / Novice.sit: You have the puck on your backhand — the weaker side of your stick. A good shot would be on your forehand. There's a small amount of time to switch. What do you do?
opts: Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Take the split second to switch the puck to your forehand if you safely have time — forehand is more accurate.
Throw your hockey stick down on the ice and use your hands and gloves to try and bat the puck into the net.
Pass it instead — you should never ever shoot the puck directly from the backhand side of your hockey stick.
U9 / Novice.why: If you have time, switching to your forehand gives you a more accurate shot. But the backhand is also a real shot — if you don't have time to switch, use the backhand.
U9 / Novice.tip: Puck on your backhand? Switch to forehand if you have time. If not, shoot the backhand a real shot too.

U9 / Novice.sit: On a 2-on-1 rush, both attackers should drive straight at the goalie down the middle.
U9 / Novice.why: On a 2-on-1, the second attacker needs to hold their lane. If you drift to the middle, the pass lane closes. Stay on your side and be ready when the puck comes.
U9 / Novice.tip: 2-on-1 without the puck? Hold your lane. Don't drift. Stay ready for the pass.

U9 / Novice.sit: An attacker has the puck and they're trying to skate around you to get to the net. What's the most important thing you keep between you and the net?
opts: Chase the puck instead of covering your assignment.
Your body — stay between the attacker and your net at all times.
Your skates — keep them pointed toward the attacker at all tim.
Your helmet — keep it facing the attacker at all times.
U9 / Novice.why: The golden rule of defense is to keep your body between the attacker and your net. If you do that, they have to go through you — and that's where you have the advantage.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defending 1-on-1? Keep your body between the attacker and your net. Always.

U9 / Novice.sit: The referee raises their arm during play but hasn't blown the whistle yet. What does that mean?
U9 / Novice.why: A referee with their arm raised is signaling a delayed penalty. Play continues until the team being penalized touches the puck — then the whistle blows. This is actually good for your team because you can pull your goalie for an extra attacker.
U9 / Novice.tip: Ref's arm is up? Delayed penalty. Keep playing — the whistle comes when they touch the puck.
opts: The play is over.
A penalty is being called as soon as the other team touches the puck.
There will be a faceoff.
The goalie was hurt.

U9 / Novice.sit: If your coach asks you to try something a new way, you should give it a real try even if your old way feels easier.
U9 / Novice.why: Almost every skill improvement feels awkward at first. The feeling of 'weird' is your brain and body adjusting to something new. Sticking with the new way through the awkward phase is how you improve.
U9 / Novice.tip: New way feels weird? Keep trying it. Weird is what improvement feels like at first.

U9 / Novice.sit: When a teammate comes off the ice after making a mistake, you should ignore them so they can think.
U9 / Novice.why: A teammate who feels supported after a mistake is more likely to bounce back quickly and play well. One encouraging word from a teammate can make a real difference.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate came off after a mistake? Encourage them. That's what good teammates do.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're skating with the puck toward the net at full speed. A defender is chasing you from behind. Should you slow down to take a better shot?
opts: Yes — slow down and aim really carefully so you can pick a spot in the corner of the net to hit.
No — shoot while moving at full speed, making it harder for the goalie to track.
Stop completely first and then shoot the puck when you are standing still and you can aim it well.
Pass it to one of your teammates who is wide open on the other side of the net instead of shoot.
U9 / Novice.why: Shooting while moving at full speed is harder for a goalie to track than a stationary shot. Slowing down also lets the defender catch up. Shoot while moving.
U9 / Novice.tip: Full speed toward the net? Shoot while moving. Don't slow down.

U9 / Novice.concept: Watch out for the back door
U9 / Novice.sit: The other team has the puck in the corner. You're covering a player at the far post of your net — far away from the puck. They're just standing there. Do you stop covering them?
opts: Yes — they don't have the puck at all and they are far away from where the puck is now so they can't score on your team.
No — a player standing at the far post is waiting for a back-door pass. That's actually one of the most dangerous plays.
Yes — you should move toward the puck in the corner instead of staying to cover the open player at the far post of the net.
Yes — go and stand next to the goalie in the crease instead of covering the open player all the way at the far post.
U9 / Novice.why: A player standing at the back post in the offensive zone is set up for a back-door pass — one of the hardest plays for a goalie to stop. Leaving them means giving up a dangerous scoring chance.
U9 / Novice.tip: Player hanging at the far post? Keep covering them. They're waiting for the back-door pass.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your team is losing by one goal with one minute left. The coach pulls the goalie. Why?
U9 / Novice.why: Pulling the goalie for an extra attacker is a calculated risk. Your net is empty, but the extra skater gives your team more firepower to try to tie the game. It's a real strategy coaches use.
U9 / Novice.tip: Coach pulled the goalie? They want the extra attacker to score. Protect the empty net on defense and attack hard on offense.
opts: To give the goalie a rest.
To put an extra skater on the ice and try to score.
To start a fight.
To save energy for next game.

U9 / Novice.sit: When you're the first one off the ice for a line change, you should hold the bench door open for your teammates.
U9 / Novice.why: A clean line change means the incoming player is ready before the outgoing player leaves. A messy change can result in too many players on the ice. Coordinating the exchange is both players' responsibility.
U9 / Novice.tip: Line change: make sure the exchange is clean. Incoming player ready, then you leave. No gaps, no overlaps.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're shooting the puck at the net. Should you always try to shoot as hard as you possibly can?
opts: Yes — the hardest shot you can take always wins and beats the goalie every single time you take one of them.
Not always — an accurate shot that goes where the goalie isn't is better than a hard shot right at them.
Yes — goalies simply can't stop a shot that is hard enough to get past their pads and into the back of the net.
Hard shots are rude — always just shoot very softly and gently at the goalie so they don't get hurt at all.
U9 / Novice.why: Accuracy beats power when it comes to shooting. A perfect hard shot right at the goalie is an easy save. A well-placed shot — even if it's softer goes where the goalie isn't is more likely to score.
U9 / Novice.tip: Hard shot vs. accurate shot? Pick the corner first. Power helps, but location wins.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're watching the play develop. You can see a pass is about to be made by the other team across the ice. You're in a spot where you could skate to intercept it. What do you do?
opts: Wait to see where the puck goes first before you decide whether you should skate over to intercept.
Start skating to intercept it now — before the pass is made.
Stay right where you are on the ice and wait for the pass to come to one of the other players.
Skate right at the player who is about to receive it and try to check them before they get the p.
U9 / Novice.why: Reading a pass before it's made — and moving early — is how you intercept pucks. Moving after the pass is made means you're always too late.
U9 / Novice.tip: See a pass about to happen? Start moving to intercept now — before the pass is made.

U9 / Novice.sit: Talking to your linemates before you go on the ice helps you play better together.
U9 / Novice.why: A quick word with your linemates before a shift helps everyone get on the same page. Even one sentence about what you're going to do builds communication and team chemistry.
U9 / Novice.tip: About to go on for a shift? Say something to your linemates. Even one word. Communicate.

U9 / Novice.sit: A pass is coming to you and the goalie is not ready for a quick shot. If you stop the puck and aim, the goalie will have time to set. What should you try instead?
opts: Always stop the puck first and then aim carefully at the net before you take your shot every time.
One-time it — shoot it right when it arrives. A quick one-touch shot catches the goalie off guard.
Pass it to someone else instead — you should never ever shoot the puck without stopping it first.
Wait for a better time to shoot — hold the puck a few more seconds until the goalie is out of position.
U9 / Novice.why: A one-timer — shooting the puck on the first touch without stopping it — can catch a goalie before they're set. Even at a young age, learning to take the puck in stride is a skill worth practicing.
U9 / Novice.tip: Pass coming and goalie not ready? One-time it. Quick release before they can set.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your coach shows the team video and points out a mistake you made, you should get defensive and explain why it wasn't your fault.
U9 / Novice.why: Video feedback is a coaching tool used at every level of hockey. Being pointed out in video is a chance to learn — the coach is trusting you to handle it and improve. That trust is earned by the players who respond well.
U9 / Novice.tip: Coach showed video of your mistake? Listen and learn. Video feedback is how pros get better too.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're skating toward the net with the puck. A defender is focusing only on you. Your teammate is completely open on the other side of the net. What do you do?
opts: Focus on your own play and let others figure out theirs.
Pass to your open teammate — a player who sets up a teammate for a goal is just as important as the scorer.
Try to do a fancy trick move with the puck to try and get around the defender and score a goal by yourself.
Skate away from the net and pass the puck back to one of your defensemen so they can set up a brand new play.
U9 / Novice.why: Setting up a teammate who's open for a goal is one of the most important plays in hockey. A pass to an open teammate is often the highest-percentage play — and assists matter as much as goals.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open teammate? Pass. Setting up a goal is just as important as scoring one.

U9 / Novice.sit: Every time a puck is loose, you and your opponents are racing for it. What's the most important thing about who wins?
U9 / Novice.why: Making it a habit to race to every loose puck — not just the ones you think you'll win — is what separates compete players from passive ones. Even if you don't always win the race, the habit itself makes you better.
U9 / Novice.tip: Loose puck anywhere on the ice? Race to it. Make it a habit. Every single time.
opts: Who is the bigger player.
Who is the closest to start.
Who works harder and skates faster.
Who has the better stick.

U9 / Novice.sit: There's a faceoff in your defensive zone right beside your net. The referee is about to drop the puck. Where should all the players except the centers be?
opts: Wherever they feel like on the ice — it doesn't really matter where the non-center players stand before the puck drops for the face.
In their assigned positions — covering specific areas and players based on what the coach set up. Be there before the puck drops.
Chase the puck instead of covering your assignment.
Standing all the way back at center ice so they can be ready to attack if the other team wins the faceoff draw in your defensive zone.
U9 / Novice.why: Defensive zone faceoffs are critical moments. Every player except the centers should be in their assigned position before the puck drops — covering a player or an area. The setup before the drop is part of the play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defensive zone faceoff? Get to your position before the puck drops. Every player has a job.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're the goalie. The other team has the puck and is skating toward your net from the middle. Where should you be standing?
opts: In one of the two far back corners of your own goal net.
Right in the middle of the net, between the two posts.
Skating out from your crease to meet them at center.
Sitting down on the ice right in front of your net.
U9 / Novice.why: The goalie's job is to protect the net. Standing in the middle between the two posts gives you the best chance to stop the puck no matter which side it comes from.
U9 / Novice.tip: Always come back to the middle. That's your home.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player is skating toward you with the puck. You're nervous and want to fall down to block the shot. What should you do instead?
opts: Fall down on the ice right away so you can cover the whole bottom of the net.
Stay on your feet as long as possible.
Skate all the way out to the blue line and try to poke the puck off of the player.
Turn around so you can face the net and get ready to dig the puck out if they score.
U9 / Novice.why: Staying on your feet keeps you in the game. The moment you fall down, you lose the ability to move left or right. Stay up and track the puck.
U9 / Novice.tip: Stay up as long as you can. Watch the puck. Drop when you have to.

U9 / Novice.sit: Even when there's a lot of action in front of your net, the goalie should keep their eyes on the puck the whole time.
U9 / Novice.why: The puck is what scores goals. No matter what else is happening, the goalie always tracks the puck. Players move — but the puck tells you where the danger is.
U9 / Novice.tip: Eyes on the puck. Always. That's the number one goalie rule.

U9 / Novice.sit: You stop a shot but the puck bounces out in front of your net. What should you do?
opts: Stand still and just wait for the other team to take another shot on goal.
Try to cover the puck or push it to the corner so your team can get it.
Skate out of the crease and try to pass the puck up to one of your teammates.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U9 / Novice.why: A rebound right in front of your net is dangerous — the other team can shoot again immediately. Try to cover it with your glove or paddle it to the corner where your team can get it.
U9 / Novice.tip: Stop it once, then smother it or push it away. Don't let the rebound sit in front.

U9 / Novice.sit: If the puck is loose behind your net and you can't see it, you should yell to your teammate to tell you which side it's on.
U9 / Novice.why: Goalies can see things from their angle that teammates can't — and teammates can see things goalies can't. Communicating out loud helps your whole team.
U9 / Novice.tip: Talk out there! Goalies who communicate help their whole team.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player from the other team is standing right in front of your net blocking your view. What do you do?
opts: Ignore them completely and just trust that your teammates will come push them out of the zone.
Try to see around them — move side to side a little to get a better view of the puck again.
Skate into them and try to push them out of the front of the crease all by yourself.
Sit down on the ice so you can try to see the puck underneath the player's legs and skates.
U9 / Novice.why: Goalies can move slightly side to side to peek around a screener. Your teammates should also be pushing that player out, but you can help yourself by moving to find the puck.
U9 / Novice.tip: Screener in front? Move to find the puck. Don't just stand still.

U9 / Novice.sit: A player is coming at you from the right side of the net. They have a very bad angle — almost behind the net. What do you do?
opts: Leave the net and chase them behind it to try and poke the puck off them before they get any chance to take a shot.
Move toward them and take away the near post — give them the long side shot which is much harder to score on.
Stay in the exact middle of the net no matter where they are skating — let them shoot from whatever angle they want.
Fall down on the ice and try to cover the bottom of the net with your legs so they can't score there at all.
U9 / Novice.why: When a player has a bad angle, the goalie moves toward them to take away the short side — the close post. This forces them to shoot across the whole crease which is much harder.
U9 / Novice.tip: Bad angle? Take away the short side. Hug the near post.

U9 / Novice.sit: After going down to make a save, the goalie should pop back up onto their skates as fast as possible.
U9 / Novice.why: Getting up quickly after going down is one of the most important goalie skills. The puck can come back any second — you need to be back on your feet and in position.
U9 / Novice.tip: Down? Get up fast. Every second on the ice is a second you can't make a save.

U9 / Novice.sit: You make a save and have your glove on the puck. Your team is tired and needs a rest. What can you do?
opts: Throw the puck back out into play with your glove so the game keeps going without any whistle.
Cover it completely with your glove — the referee will blow the whistle and your team gets a rest.
Kick the puck to one of your defensemen so they can skate out of your zone and give the team a r.
Stand up and skate with the puck out to the blue line before passing it to one of your teammates.
U9 / Novice.why: When a goalie covers the puck completely, the referee stops play. This lets both teams rest and reset — very useful when your team is tired or under heavy pressure.
U9 / Novice.tip: Need a rest? Cover the puck completely. The whistle will come.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your defenseman is behind the net and can't see where the other team's players are. You can see everything from your angle. What should you do?
opts: Stay quiet their job to figure out pressure and cover players by themselves.
Tell them where the pressure is coming from: 'Left side!' or 'Pressure right!'.
Skate out of your crease to help your defenseman battle for the puck behind the net.
Go back to the crease and ignore the whole play happening behind the net with your.
U9 / Novice.why: The goalie has the best view of the whole ice. Telling your defenseman where pressure is coming from is one of the most valuable things a goalie can do — it helps your whole team.
U9 / Novice.tip: You see it all from back there. Talk to your D. They need your eyes.

U9 / Novice.concept: What to do when they score
U9 / Novice.sit: The other team just scored a goal. Put what happens next in the right order.
items: Skate back to center ice for the faceoff
Line up in your position
Get ready for the puck to drop
Play hard on the next shift — one goal doesn't matter
U9 / Novice.why: After a goal against, you reset. Go to center, line up, get ready, and compete. Getting upset wastes time — the next shift is what matters.
U9 / Novice.tip: They scored? Reset. Line up. Next shift. That's how tough players respond.

U9 / Novice.sit: You want to pass the puck to your teammate. Put these steps in order.
items: Look up and find your teammate
Make sure they're open and ready
Put the puck on their stick — aim your pass
Call out to let them know it's coming
U9 / Novice.why: A great pass starts with looking up, letting your teammate know, making sure they're ready, and then putting it right on their stick. Calling out first helps them get ready.
U9 / Novice.tip: Look, call, check they're ready, pass it on their stick. That's teamwork.

U9 / Novice.sit: The puck is loose in the corner. Put these steps in order.
items: See the loose puck
Skate hard toward it — don't wait
Get your stick on the puck first
Look up and make a play with it
U9 / Novice.why: Loose pucks go to whoever gets there first. See it, go hard, get it, then look up. Standing and watching lets the other team grab it.
U9 / Novice.tip: See it, go get it, win it, use it. Every loose puck.

U9 / Novice.sit: The referee just blew the whistle. Put what you should do in order.
items: Stop playing — the whistle means stop
Look at the referee
Listen to where the faceoff will be
Skate to your position for the faceoff
U9 / Novice.why: When the whistle blows, everything resets. Stop, look at the ref, listen, then get to your spot. Players who keep playing after the whistle get penalties.
U9 / Novice.tip: Whistle: stop, look, listen, get to your spot. Every time.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach is teaching a new drill. Put these steps in order.
items: Stop skating and look at the coach
Listen to the instructions carefully
Watch if the coach demonstrates it
Try the drill and do your best
U9 / Novice.why: Learning works best when you stop and focus first. Listen to the words, watch the demo, then try it. Players who skip the listening part make more mistakes.
U9 / Novice.tip: Stop, listen, watch, try. That's how you learn new things fast.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach says your line is up next. Put these steps in order.
items: Get to the door of the bench
Watch the play so you know what's happening
Jump on when your teammate comes off
Skate hard to where you need to be
U9 / Novice.why: A quick, smart line change starts before you leave the bench. Be ready at the door, watch what's happening, jump on at the right time, and get into the play fast.
U9 / Novice.tip: Be at the door, watch the play, jump on, skate hard. Good changes start on the bench.

U9 / Novice.sit: It's okay to stop trying if the other team is winning by a lot.
U9 / Novice.why: You always play hard no matter the score. Games can change fast, and the habits you build when it's tough are the habits that make you better. Never stop competing.
U9 / Novice.tip: Down by a lot? Doesn't matter. Keep going. Hard work is always worth it.

U9 / Novice.sit: When your teammate scores a goal, you should celebrate with them even if you didn't help on the play.
U9 / Novice.why: Hockey is a team sport. Celebrating together builds team spirit. Your teammate's goal is your team's goal — every player matters, even when you're not the one who scored.
U9 / Novice.tip: Teammate scored? Celebrate with them. That's what teams do.

U9 / Novice.sit: When the referee blows the whistle, you should stop playing immediately.
U9 / Novice.why: The whistle means play is dead. Anything you do after the whistle doesn't count and could get you a penalty. Stop right away, every time.
U9 / Novice.tip: Whistle = stop. Immediately. No exceptions.

U9 / Novice.sit: Only the best player on the team should shoot the puck.
U9 / Novice.why: Every player should shoot when they have a chance. The more shots your team takes, the more chances you have to score. You don't have to be the best player to put the puck on net.
U9 / Novice.tip: Got a chance to shoot? Shoot. Every player. Every shot counts.

U9 / Novice.sit: Everyone on the team needs to help on defense — not just the defensemen.
U9 / Novice.why: Defense is everyone's job. Forwards backcheck. Centers cover the middle. When the whole team defends, the other team has a much harder time scoring.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defense isn't just for D. Everyone helps. The whole team defends together.

U9 / Novice.sit: Every player on the team should always skate directly to wherever the puck is.
U9 / Novice.why: If everyone goes to the puck, the rest of the ice is empty. Smart players spread out and find open ice. Only one or two players need to be near the puck — everyone else should be open for a pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Not everyone goes to the puck. Spread out. Find open ice. Be ready for a pass.

U9 / Novice.sit: Making mistakes during practice and games is part of getting better at hockey.
U9 / Novice.why: Every player makes mistakes — even the pros. Mistakes are how you learn what works and what doesn't. The important thing is to keep trying and learn from them.
U9 / Novice.tip: Made a mistake? Good. Now you know. Try again. That's how you get better.

U9 / Novice.q: The coach says 'protect the slot.' Which area is the slot?
choices: The highlighted area in front of the net
The area near the blue line
U9 / Novice.tip: The slot is prime scoring real estate — from the faceoff dots to the top of the crease. Most goals come from here.

U9 / Novice.sit: The whistle blew. Where do you line up for a face-off in your own zone?
opts: At the face-off dot closest to your net.
At center ice.
In front of the other team's net.
On the bench.
U9 / Novice.why: In your own zone, every face-off has a spot. You line up at the dot closest to your goalie.
U9 / Novice.tip: Own zone = own dot.

U9 / Novice.sit: Your coach yells 'back!' What does that usually mean?
opts: Skate back toward your own goalie.
Skate backward in circles.
Back up to the bench and sit.
Lean backward on your skates.
U9 / Novice.why: 'Back!' means get back to your own end. Your team is defending and they need you home.
U9 / Novice.tip: 'Back!' = skate to your goalie.

U9 / Novice.sit: Which zone is YOUR offensive zone?
opts: The zone where the other team's net is.
The zone where your goalie stands.
The middle zone.
The penalty box.
U9 / Novice.why: Your offensive zone is the one you're trying to score in — where the OTHER team's net is.
U9 / Novice.tip: You score = offensive zone.

U9 / Novice.sit: The neutral zone is the area between the two blue lines.
U9 / Novice.why: Yes. Neutral zone = middle of the rink, between the blue lines. Not offensive, not defensive.
U9 / Novice.tip: Between blue lines = neutral.

U9 / Novice.sit: You just crossed your own blue line into the middle of the rink. Which zone are you in now?
opts: Neutral zone.
Offensive zone.
Defensive zone.
Penalty zone.
U9 / Novice.why: Crossing out of your own zone puts you in the neutral zone. Good awareness!
U9 / Novice.tip: Between the blues = neutral.

U9 / Novice.q: You have the puck. Tap every teammate who's open for a pass.
U9 / Novice.tip: Open means no defender in the passing lane.

U9 / Novice.sit: Why is it important to look up while you're carrying the puck through the neutral zone?
opts: So coaches can see your face on the bench
So you don't trip over the blue line
So you can see who's open and where pressure is coming from
So the goalie can see your eyes
U9 / Novice.why: Eyes-up = information. Looking up tells you which teammate is open, where the defender is closing from, and whether you have time to skate or need to make a play. Eyes down = none of that.
U9 / Novice.tip: Eyes-up = information.

media.alt: Player's eyes are level with the rink, looking up the ice at teammates and defenders — not down at the puck on the stick blade.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: So you can see who's open and where pressure is coming from

U9 / Novice.sit: When should you look around to see what's going on around you?
opts: Before the pass arrives on your stick
Only after you've received the puck
Never — focus on the puck
Only when the coach yells
U9 / Novice.why: Scanning before the puck arrives is what lets you make a one-touch play. If you only look up after you receive, the defender is already on you.
U9 / Novice.tip: Scanning before the puck arrives is what lets you make a one-touch play.

media.alt: Before the pass arrives, the player's head is turned to scan the ice — they already know where the puck and pressure are coming from.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Before the pass arrives on your stick

U9 / Novice.sit: You're defending the slot in your own end. What should your body and stick be doing?
opts: Standing straight up so you look bigger
Knees bent, stick flat on the ice between you and the attacker
Lunging at the attacker to take the puck
Skating away from the slot to find help
U9 / Novice.why: Defending in the slot is patient work. Bent knees keep you ready to move. Stick on the ice covers the lane between you and the attacker without committing.
U9 / Novice.tip: Defending in the slot is patient work.

media.alt: Knees bent, hips back, stick on the ice between the player and the attacker — ready to defend without lunging.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Knees bent, stick flat on the ice between you and the attacker

U9 / Novice.sit: Where should your stick be when you're defending in your own end?
opts: Up high so you can swing it
Flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck and an open opponent
Tucked under your arm
Held behind your back
U9 / Novice.why: Stick on ice in the passing lane is the cheapest, highest-value defensive habit. It blocks passes without you having to skate or hit anyone.
U9 / Novice.tip: Stick on ice in the passing lane is the cheapest, highest-value defensive habit.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck carrier and the open teammate — taking away the pass without leaving the feet.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck and an open opponent

U9 / Novice.sit: What helps your teammate make a great pass to you?
opts: Yelling really loudly
Skating back and forth so they see you
Standing perfectly still
Putting your stick blade flat on the ice exactly where you want the puck
U9 / Novice.why: A passer needs a target. Stick blade flat on the ice = obvious target. Yelling or moving without a stick target makes the passer guess.
U9 / Novice.tip: A passer needs a target.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice presenting a clear target where the player wants to receive the puck.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Putting your stick blade flat on the ice exactly where you want the puck

U9 / Novice.sit: A defender is pressuring you on the wall in the offensive zone. Where should your body be?
opts: Squared up to the defender so you can fight for the puck
On the ice for the referee to see
Between the defender and the puck — body shielding the puck
Skating away from the defender as fast as you can
U9 / Novice.why: Body between the defender and the puck = puck protection. Squaring up means the defender can reach the puck. Shielding buys you time for support.
U9 / Novice.tip: Body between the defender and the puck = puck protection.

media.alt: Player's body is between the defender and the puck — back to the defender, puck on the far side of the body along the boards.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Between the defender and the puck — body shielding the puck

U9 / Novice.sit: How do you generate speed when you're skating around a curve or turn?
opts: Glide and let momentum carry you
Stop and start again
Cross your outside skate over your inside skate to push
Skate straight across the curve
U9 / Novice.why: Crossovers are how you ADD speed in a turn. Gliding bleeds speed. Every turn is a chance to push — and the kid who crosses over arrives first.
U9 / Novice.tip: Crossovers are how you ADD speed in a turn.

media.alt: Outside skate is crossing over the inside skate as the player rounds a curve — generating speed through the turn instead of gliding.

U9 / Novice._correctAnswerText: Cross your outside skate over your inside skate to push

U9 / Novice.sit: You're on a 2-on-1 with your teammate. What's your best play?
opts: Shoot at the goalie
Pass to your teammate
Skate around the defender
Stop and wait
U9 / Novice.why: The defender is playing you, not your teammate. Look at his stick — it's on your side. Your teammate is wide open. Pass!
U9 / Novice.tip: The defender is playing you, not your teammate.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: True or False: The best play here is to shoot.
opts: True
False
U9 / Novice.why: The defender's stick is taking away your shot. But your teammate has lots of open ice. The pass is the smarter play.
U9 / Novice.tip: The defender's stick is taking away your shot.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: Tap the area where you should pass the puck.
opts: Hotspot target: the open ice between the defender and the teammate on the right side
U9 / Novice.why: That's the open lane. Your teammate is heading there with his stick down ready to receive. Pass into that space.
U9 / Novice.tip: That's the open lane.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: Fill in the blank: When the defender plays you on a 2-on-1, the right play is to ___.
opts: Accepted answers: pass / pass to my teammate / pass it across
U9 / Novice.why: Yes! When the defender plays you, your teammate is open. The pass is the right play almost every time.
U9 / Novice.tip: Yes.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: Where is the defender's stick?
opts: On your teammate's side
On your side (the puck side)
In the air
Behind him
U9 / Novice.why: The defender's stick is on YOUR side, taking away your shot. That's why the pass is open.
U9 / Novice.tip: The defender's stick is on YOUR side, taking away your shot.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: Why is passing better than shooting in this picture?
opts: Shooting is too hard for kids
The defender is taking away your shot but not the pass
Passing is always the right answer
The goalie is too good
U9 / Novice.why: Good defenders take something away. This one is taking away your shot. So the pass — what he's GIVING you — is the right play.
U9 / Novice.tip: Good defenders take something away.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U9 / Novice.sit: You're on a 2-on-1 with your teammate. What's your best play?
opts: Shoot at the goalie
Pass to your teammate
Skate around the defender
Stop and wait

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team has the puck in your defensive zone. What's the most important thing every player on your team should be doing?
opts: Watching the puck.
Finding a player to cover.
Skating to the blue.
Waiting for a turnover.
U11 / Atom.why: In your defensive zone, every player needs to find a check. Watching the puck without picking up a check is how goals happen.
U11 / Atom.tip: Own zone: find your check first. Then watch the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has the puck in the offensive zone. It's sliding toward the blue line. You're the defenseman. What do you do?
opts: Let the puck slide out of the zone.
Skate to the puck and keep it in the zone.
Back up toward the blue line to give yourself more room to react.
Skate to the front of the net.
U11 / Atom.why: Keeping the puck in the offensive zone is the defenseman's first job.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck going to the line? Keep it in. That's your job as D.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is forechecking. The opposing D just picked up the puck behind their net. You're the first forward in. What's your goal?
opts: Crash the net hard right away and look for a rebound chance in tight.
Put pressure on the D so they can't make an easy breakout pass.
Wait at the blue line for your linemates to catch up before forechecking.
Skate back to the red line.
U11 / Atom.why: The first forward on the forecheck pressures the puck carrier to take away easy options and force a mistake.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forechecking: pressure the D. Take away their easy out.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just lost the puck in the offensive zone. The other team is breaking out. What should every forward on your team do immediately?
opts: Stay high in the offensive zone and wait for a long stretch pass.
Turn around and skate back toward your own zone.
Stop and call for a pass.
Stand still and watch.
U11 / Atom.why: The instant your team loses possession, every forward transitions back. Not eventually — immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lost the puck? Turn around. Right now.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck along the boards and a defender is coming at you from behind. What's the right thing to do with your body?
opts: Turn around to face the defender.
Put your body between the defender and the puck.
Spin off the boards and try to skate through the defender.
Pass the puck away right before the defender arrives.
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection is about body position. Getting your body between the puck and the defender gives you time to make a play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defender behind you? Get your body between them and the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck in your own zone with a forechecker right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to skate out of the zone yourself.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
Shoot the puck the length of the ice.
Wait for a better option to open.
U11 / Atom.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. The two defenders take your two wingers. You're the trailing center. Where do you position?
opts: Race hard to the net for a tip-in chance.
Stay high and be ready for a drop pass or rebound shot.
Skate to the corner to cover the weak-side winger.
Stop at the red line to backcheck early.
U11 / Atom.why: The trailer on a 3-on-2 stays high — ready for a drop pass if the play breaks down or to get a rebound shot from the top.
U11 / Atom.tip: Trailer on a rush: stay high. You're the safety and the extra option.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team rushes 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Pass across to the right winger for a quick one-timer chance.
Pass across to the left winger for a clean shot at the net.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Pull up at the blue line and circle back for a full regroup.
U11 / Atom.why: When both defenders go to the wings, they've given you the middle. Driving the lane and shooting catches the goalie off-guard.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. The puck goes to the corner. Your nearest winger is there but about to get pressured. No coverage behind you. Should you pinch?
opts: Yes — go help your winger immediately — crash down fast and support the battle because two players on the puck wins more than one every time.
No — hold the blue line. If your winger loses the puck you'll be caught deep.
Go to the net front — crash the crease for a rebound while your winger battles along the boards and let your partner cover the point area.
Skate behind the net to support — work down low and be ready to receive a D-to-D pass along the goal line from your winning teammate.
U11 / Atom.why: Pinching when there's no coverage behind you and your winger is under pressure is high-risk. If the puck turns over, you're caught deep with a 2-on-1 against you.
U11 / Atom.tip: No coverage behind you? Don't pinch. Hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the point on the power play. The slot is full of traffic. You have a shooting lane to the net. What do you do?
opts: Hold the puck and wait for the traffic in the slot to clear away.
Shoot immediately — traffic in front means screens and rebounds.
Pass cross-ice to the other point while reading the situation.
Skate into the slot with it yourself.
U11 / Atom.why: Traffic in front of the net is an asset, not a problem. Shoot through it — the screen blocks the goalie's view and the traffic creates rebounds.
U11 / Atom.tip: Traffic in front? Shoot. That's exactly what you want.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in a 2-1-2 forecheck. The puck is in the corner. As the high forward, where do you position?
opts: Go into the corner and chase the puck hard — win the battle with numbers by joining your teammate so you can pin the puck along the boards.
Stay high between the two defensemen — cut off passes and take away the middle.
Go straight to the net front — be a screen and look for rebounds while the low forwards battle for the puck along the boards in the corner.
Back up to the blue line and wait — let your low forwards work the corner and be ready to support if the puck comes to you up top there.
U11 / Atom.why: In a 2-1-2 forecheck, the high forward cuts off outlet passes through the middle.
U11 / Atom.tip: High forward in a 2-1-2: sit between the D. Cut off the middle outlet.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're behind your own net with the puck. A forechecker is coming around the left side. Your left D is covered. Your right D is open. What do you do?
opts: Try to carry the puck out to the left side fast.
Spin to the right and find your open defenseman.
Stop and wait for the forechecker to commit.
Shoot it down the ice.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading which side the pressure is coming from and reversing to the open side is basic puck protection under pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forechecker coming from the left? Spin right. Go where they're not.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards. You're the defenseman. You want to angle them into the corner. Which direction should you push them?
opts: Toward the middle of the ice.
Into the corner toward.
Straight backward toward your net.
It doesn't matter immediately.
U11 / Atom.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier away from the dangerous middle and into the corner.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angling: push them to the boards, away from the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. A shot comes from the point. You're standing in front of the net. What's your job on that shot?
opts: Get out of the goalie's line of sight so they can track the puck.
Screen the goalie and be ready to tip or deflect the shot.
Skate to the corner to grab the rebound.
Drop to the half-wall to be an outlet for a pass out of traffic.
U11 / Atom.why: The net-front player on the power play screens the goalie and tips shots. Getting out of the way defeats the purpose of being there.
U11 / Atom.tip: Net front on the PP: screen the goalie and get ready to tip.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive the puck at the half-wall. The goalie is slightly out of position and you have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Pass to the winger breaking to the net.
Shoot at the exposed net immediately.
Hold the puck and look for an option.
Pass back to the defense at the point.
U11 / Atom.why: An out-of-position goalie with a shooting lane is an opportunity. Shoot before they recover.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie out of position with a lane? Shoot. Right now.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're low in the corner with the puck. Your winger is at the half-wall. A defender is pressuring you hard from behind. What do you do?
opts: Try to power through and hold the puck against the boards.
Bank a quick pass off the boards to your half-wall winger.
Spin and skate into the middle ice.
Shoot the puck on net from down low in the corner.
U11 / Atom.why: A quick board pass to the half-wall winger is the reliable escape when you're being pressured in the corner. The boards are your extra teammate.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressured in the corner? Bank it off the boards to the half-wall. Use the boards.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team turns the puck over. The other team has a 2-on-1 and you're the only forward close enough to get back. You can't catch the puck carrier. What do you do?
opts: Skate full speed straight at the puck carrier and try to poke it away from behind.
Skate back hard on the weak side — get between the second attacker and the net.
Go straight to the net to support your goalie.
Peel off to the bench for a quick change.
U11 / Atom.why: You can't catch the puck carrier, but you can take away the pass option on the weak side.
U11 / Atom.tip: Can't catch the puck carrier? Get to the weak side. Take away the pass.

U11 / Atom.sit: Three attackers enter your zone. They overload the left side — two attackers on the left and one on the right. Both your D are back. How should they adjust?
opts: Both D go to the left to cover the two attackers — when the other team overloads one side, your defensemen should both shift there to match numbers.
One D takes the two on the left, the other holds the middle and takes the weak-side attacker — both D communicate.
Both D shift to the right weak side — defend the back door because that's where the pass is coming to create a tap-in goal for the third attacker.
One D goes behind the net to cover — pre-rotate to cover a pass behind the goal line to take away wraparound plays before they develop at all.
U11 / Atom.why: An overload means the D have to communicate and adjust. One D takes the strong side with two attackers, while the partner takes the weak-side player and the slot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Overload coverage: communicate. One takes the strong side, the other holds the middle and weak side.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the offensive zone D. The puck went to the corner. Your winger got there first and has possession, but a defender is closing. No coverage behind you. Your other winger is at the half-wall. Should you pinch?
opts: Yes — pinch down to the corner and help your winger win the battle for the puck.
No — your winger has possession and your half-wall winger can support. Hold the blue line.
Yes — any time a teammate is battling for the puck you should pinch down.
Skate to the net-front instead.
U11 / Atom.why: Your winger has possession and the half-wall winger can support. You don't need to go. Pinching here with no coverage behind you risks a breakout against you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Winger has it and half-wall can help? Hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
opts: Chase the puck into the corner.
Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
Skate back hard to your defensive zone right away.
Go to the bench.
U11 / Atom.why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase.
U11 / Atom.tip: They won the corner battle? High forward reads the breakout. Don't chase — intercept.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in the corner. Two defenders are collapsing on you from different angles. Your only open teammate is at the blue line — a long pass blocked by two defenders. What do you do?
opts: Attempt the long stretch pass to the blue line and hope it sneaks through the two defenders.
Protect the puck tight to the boards with your body, wait for the angle to open, then make a short chip pass.
Try to spin through both defenders with quick footwork and create your own lane out.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and trust a teammate is there on the other side.
