U11 / Atom.why: Under dual pressure with a long pass blocked, protecting tight and waiting for an opening is the disciplined play. Two defenders can't both have perfect angles — one will leave a gap.
U11 / Atom.tip: Two defenders collapsing? Protect tight and wait. The gap will open.

U11 / Atom.sit: A skilled attacker gets a step on you at the blue line and is now skating toward your net with speed. You can't catch them. What's the right approach?
opts: Give up the chase and skate calmly back so you're set for the next rush after the scoring chance.
Get your stick into their lane — angle them toward the boards and get your body in front of the shooting lane.
Try to grab them.
Dive forward and slide on your belly to block the shot — your body can take away the whole lane.
U11 / Atom.why: When you can't catch an attacker, get in their lane. Angling them toward the boards takes away the shooting lane.
U11 / Atom.tip: Can't catch them? Get your body in the lane. Take away the shooting angle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your penalty kill gets the puck out of your zone. It's a 2-on-3 as you exit. Do you try to score shorthanded or just clear?
opts: Go for the shorthanded goal — attack the net.
Clear the zone and get back — a clean clear is a win on the PK.
Pass back to your goalie.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
U11 / Atom.why: A clean clear on the PK is a win — it kills clock and resets the power play.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK clear: clean exit = success. Don't gamble on a shorthanded goal.

U11 / Atom.sit: You skate into the offensive zone with speed. You planned to shoot from the right side, but as you enter you see the goalie has that side covered and a teammate is breaking to the net on the left. What do you do?
opts: Shoot to the right side as planned — the coach drew it up this way so stick to the plan.
Read and react — pass to your breaking teammate. The plan changes when the situation changes.
Slow down, circle back to the blue line, and think about what the best option might be.
Dump it into the corner and send your linemates in to regroup and start the cycle.
U11 / Atom.why: Plans are starting points, not locks. If the situation changes, read it and react.
U11 / Atom.tip: Read what's actually there. Your plan is a starting point, not a commitment.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're at the half-wall with the puck. The point defender is cheating toward the net. The slot defender is watching the corner. Nobody is tight on you. What's the play?
opts: Pass the puck down to the corner — find a teammate low behind the goal line to start a cycle with the defender cheating up toward the net instead.
Shoot from the half-wall — you have time and the slot defender is distracted.
Pass up to the point defender — move the puck to the blue line for a one-timer since you have time and the slot defender isn't watching you.
Drop the puck behind you immediately — leave it for a trailing teammate to pick up since nobody is pressuring you on the half-wall right now.
U11 / Atom.why: When the slot defender is watching the corner and nobody's tight on you at the half-wall, you have a shooting opportunity. Take it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Nobody on you at the half-wall and slot D is distracted? Shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two forecheckers are deep in your zone. Your winger is up the wall but covered. Your center in the middle is also covered. Your D has the puck behind the net. What's the best option?
opts: Force a pass to a covered teammate — a quick hard pass can break through the coverage if you put it on the tape before the forechecker reacts.
Wheel and carry it out — both forecheckers are committed to your forwards. The D carrying it out is the best option.
Shoot the puck around the boards — rim it hard along the wall past the forecheckers and let your winger chase it up to the neutral zone.
Wait behind the net with the puck — hold it until the forecheckers commit to one side and then make your decision based on what they do.
U11 / Atom.why: When both forecheckers have committed to your forwards and left the D unguarded, the D carrying it out is the right play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both forecheckers on your forwards? D carry it out. The lane is open.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You win the puck along the boards in your own zone. What's the priority?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Get the puck out of your zone as fast as possible — any clean clear is a win.
Hold the puck in your corner to kill time off the penalty.
Try to score a shorthanded goal.
U11 / Atom.why: Clearing the zone on the PK is priority one. Every second you hold the puck in your zone is a second the power play can set up.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK with the puck? Get it out. First clean option. Don't hold.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Chase both attackers hard and try to attack and score on the rush the other way.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
All five players retreat to the front of your own net.
Ignore the rush and stay in the offensive zone ready for the next chance.
U11 / Atom.why: A power play counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures the puck, everyone else recovers position. Panicking creates a turnover.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't panic.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is breaking out. The strong-side winger is completely covered. The D picks up the puck on the right side. The center is in the middle and the left winger is open on the short side. What's the right read?
opts: Force the pass to the covered strong-side winger — the puck will sort itself out.
Pass to the open left winger on the short side — shortest distance, cleanest.
Carry it out yourself.
Rim it around the boards.
U11 / Atom.why: The short-side winger being open is the clean, low-risk breakout pass. The shortest distance pass under pressure is usually the right one.
U11 / Atom.tip: Short-side winger open? Take the short pass. Shortest distance = cleanest option.

U11 / Atom.sit: The other team is on the power play. Their point player has the puck. One of their forwards is screening your goalie in front of the crease. What's your job as the nearest PK forward?
opts: Chase after the point player to pressure the shot from outside.
Push the screener out of the crease area — your goalie needs to see.
Stay in the passing lane.
Back up toward your own goal line.
U11 / Atom.why: The screener in front of the crease is the most dangerous player on the power play. Your job is to move them out — not chase the puck.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screener in the crease? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the defenseman at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Hold at the blue line and let the formation develop deeper.
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready if the puck turns over.
Ignore them completely and focus on keeping the puck in deep.
Call for the puck yourself so you can take the shot from the point.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter-attack lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.
U11 / Atom.tip: Center positioning for counter? Cheat back slightly. Ready for the turnover.

U11 / Atom.sit: Late in a shift, you beat a tired defenseman at center ice and you're a step ahead but they're right behind you. What's the priority?
opts: Try a complex deke to beat the goalie since the defenseman is still behind you.
Shoot as quickly as possible once you're in range — don't let the D recover.
Pull up and wait for a teammate.
Skate slowly to catch your breath.
U11 / Atom.why: With a tired D right behind you, the clock is against you. Get in range and shoot before they recover. Complex moves give them time to catch up.
U11 / Atom.tip: D right behind you on a partial break? Get in range and shoot fast. Don't deke.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your goalie stops a shot and has the puck. One winger is coming back and available. The forecheckers are still deep. What's the goalie's best move?
opts: Freeze the puck and take the faceoff in your own zone.
Pass to the winger coming back — beat the forecheck.
Rim it hard around the boards past the forecheckers.
Shoot it the length of the ice toward the far end.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie handling the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Goalie starts the breakout. Beat the forecheck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
opts: Goalie holds the puck and stops play for a faceoff — three forecheckers means breakout is too dangerous, so a faceoff is the safer play for your team.
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
Goalie shoots the puck hard down the ice — a long clear past their blue line gets the puck out of danger and forces them to reset fully.
Goalie rims the puck around the boards behind the net — the winger on the far side can pick it up as it comes around the far corner quick.
U11 / Atom.why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie made the save under pressure? Find the breaking player. Read the breakout.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They both have speed. The puck carrier is on your left. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on your left immediately — step up hard and take away the shot because the puck carrier is the biggest threat on the rush.
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter.
Race forward toward the net — get back to your crease to help your goalie on the shot coming from the puck carrier on the left side of the ice.
Skate to the weak side and cover the off-wing attacker — take away the back door pass and let your goalie handle the shot from the puck carrier on left.
U11 / Atom.why: Speed on a 2-on-1 is the attacker's advantage. Backing up fast enough while staying in the middle to take away the pass is the correct positioning.
U11 / Atom.tip: Fast 2-on-1? Back up fast. Stay in the middle. Force the shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Always wait for the perfect shot after a catch — take an extra second to settle the puck and pick your spot before releasing the shot on the goalie.
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Passed to an open teammate in the slot — don't shoot unless you have a perfect look, and moving the puck one more time opens a better chance.
Skated in closer to the net first — get to the top of the crease where the goalie can't cover as much net before you release the shot up high.
U11 / Atom.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone for a long shift. The defenders are getting tired. What should your forwards be doing to take advantage?
opts: Slow the pace down so your own forwards can save their legs.
Keep cycling and creating traffic — tired defensemen make mistakes under sustained.
Shoot from every spot on the ice no matter the angle or lane.
Change lines right away before the tired defenders can recover their wind.
U11 / Atom.why: Sustained offensive zone pressure tires defensemen. Tired defenders make bad reads, miss sticks, and give up inside position.
U11 / Atom.tip: D getting tired in your zone? Keep the pressure going. Fatigue creates mistakes.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is cycling and the slot is clogged. All four opponents are below the dots. What's the play?
opts: Keep cycling low and wait for the slot to finally clear out for a scoring chance.
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested.
Shoot from the corner no matter the angle and hope for a bounce off the goalie.
Dump it back in and change lines.
U11 / Atom.why: When all four defenders collapse below the dots, the point is wide open. A point shot from there is uncontested and generates traffic and rebounds.
U11 / Atom.tip: Four D collapsed below the dots? The point is open. Give it up. Free shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the last defender back as two attackers come in on you. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit to the puck carrier on the left — step up hard and take away the shot because stopping the puck carrier removes the biggest threat on the rush.
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter to the right — take away the pass and the tip since the cutter is the biggest threat and your goalie can stop the shot from the puck carrier on the left.
Back up all the way to your crease — give your goalie the best sight line and let them handle the two-on-one from the top of the crease area.
U11 / Atom.why: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie.
U11 / Atom.tip: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass. Let the goalie have the shot.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just had a power play chance and the goalie covered the puck. Faceoff in the offensive zone. You win the draw. Two of the other team's forwards are already breaking out. What's the immediate play?
opts: Set up the power play again and let your teammates handle everything — your PP is already working so you reset in the offensive zone and run it again.
Get the puck to a breaking teammate immediately — the 2-on-2 counter is better than resetting the PP.
Hold the puck along the boards and regroup — let your power play catch up and set up the attack after you've re-entered the offensive zone clean.
Dump the puck in deep and chase — get it behind the defense and forecheck hard since you've got fresh legs on your power play unit already.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading a counter-opportunity after a dead puck is advanced hockey sense. If two forwards are breaking and you win the draw, the counter is a better chance than resetting the power play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Win the draw and teammates are breaking? Go. Counter-attack beats a reset every time.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck right in front of the opponent's net. A defender has grabbed your stick and a second is pushing you from behind. The goalie is scrambling. What do you do?
opts: Stop and call loudly for a penalty — the refs will see the stick hold and the push from behind, so let them make the call on the obvious infraction.
Keep your feet moving and get your stick free — any shot or swipe on net is worth taking.
Fall down hard to draw the penalty — going to the ice with two defenders on you is the easiest way to get the refs to blow the whistle right away.
Pass the puck out of the slot area — find a teammate at the point or half-wall to reset the play since you're tied up by two defenders.
U11 / Atom.why: In front of the net under pressure, stopping your feet is the worst thing you can do. Keep moving, get your stick free, and put anything on net.
U11 / Atom.tip: Front of net with defenders on you? Keep your feet moving. Get the stick free. Shoot anything.

U11 / Atom.concept: Read the ice not the noise
U11 / Atom.sit: It's late in the third period. Your team is down by one. You get the puck in the offensive zone with one minute left. Your coach is yelling to shoot. You see a teammate in a clearly better position. What do you do?
opts: Shoot right away because your coach is yelling at you to shoot.
Pass to the better position — the right play is still the right play.
Hold the puck and take a second to think about options.
Skate toward the bench for a line change.
U11 / Atom.why: Coaches yell shoot to create urgency — but the right play is still the right play. A teammate in a clearly better position is worth the pass even late in the game.
U11 / Atom.tip: Coach says shoot but a teammate has a better look? Pass. Read the ice, not just the noise.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has a power play with only 10 seconds left. You're in the offensive zone at the half-wall. No clear shot lane. What's the play?
opts: Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase it with the clock winding down.
Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than nothing with 10 seconds left.
Skate the puck back out, regroup, and try to set up a clean zone entry again.
Hold the puck and protect it.
U11 / Atom.why: With 10 seconds left on a PP, the time to set up is gone. Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than the power play expiring with no attempt.
U11 / Atom.tip: 10 seconds left on the PP? Shoot from anywhere. Time's up.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team forechecks 2-1-2. Both D and one forward are along the boards below the dots. You're the second high forward. The D has rimmed the puck to the far winger position. Who gets it?
opts: You go — you're the highest forward so the rimmed puck is your responsibility.
The near winger goes — they're in better position. You stay high and be the next pass option.
Both forwards chase it hard together so one of you wins the race to the boards.
The D jumps up from below the dots to retrieve the rimmed puck.
U11 / Atom.why: The near winger retrieves the rim their assignment. You stay high as the next option. If both forwards go deep, you lose your high forward safety.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rim going to the far winger? Let them get it. You stay high. Don't both go deep.

U11 / Atom.sit: The point player is winding up for a shot and there's a screener standing right in your sightline. You can't see the puck. What do you do?
opts: Give up on the shot — you can't see it — if there's no way to track the puck just cover the low corners and hope it hits you for a lucky save.
Move laterally to find a gap around the screener, call to your D to move them, and be ready for a tip.
Drop into butterfly early to cover the bottom half — most screened shots go low because the shooter can't see either, so seal the ice completely.
Stay completely still and trust positioning — any movement takes you out of the angle, so hold your spot and let the puck hit you on the save.
U11 / Atom.why: Screeners require active problem-solving. Moving to find the puck, calling for your D to move the screener, and being ready for a tip is the full response. Passive goalies get scored on by screens.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screened shot: find the puck, call for help, expect a tip. Don't be passive.

U11 / Atom.sit: A forechecker is coming hard around the left side. The puck is sitting behind your net. You get there first. What do you do with it?
opts: Try to carry the puck out yourself.
Stop it for your left defenseman who is coming to get it — get the puck to their forehand.
Shoot it the length of the ice and let your forwards chase it.
Freeze it.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie who handles the puck well under pressure is a huge asset. Stopping it cleanly for your D's forehand — not their backhand — sets them up for a clean breakout pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: Play the puck to your D's forehand. Clean stop, clean pickup, clean exit.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your two defensemen are both going to the corner to battle for a puck. An attacker is cutting to the front of the net with nobody covering them. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing — it's a D problem.
Call out loudly to your nearest forward — 'Front! Front!' — so they know to pick up the open player.
Skate out of your crease to cover the open attacker in front of the net yourself.
Drop into your butterfly early and wait for the pass to come out of the corner.
U11 / Atom.why: When your D are both going to the corner, the front of the net is exposed. The goalie sees this happening and must call for a forward to cover it. That verbal communication can prevent a goal.
U11 / Atom.tip: D both going to the corner? Call 'Front!' immediately. Get someone on that player.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're down and out of position. The puck is sliding toward an open corner of the net and you can't get up in time. What do you try?
opts: Give up the goal and reset — once you're down you can't recover, so accept it and get back in position for the next shift after the puck drop.
Throw your stick or extend whatever body part you can reach into the path of the puck — any contact can change its direction.
Call for a penalty on the shooter — wave your glove at the ref because you were knocked down and ask for a delayed call on the play.
Stay still and trust the post — the puck usually hits the pipe from that angle, so freezing in place is the best chance to get lucky.
U11 / Atom.why: Out-of-position desperation is a real skill. Throwing your blocker hand, extending your toe, or reaching with your stick can deflect a puck that would have been a sure goal. Never give up on a play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Out of position? Reach with anything. A blocker or a toe can make a save.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You make a save and the puck is loose at your feet. Your team has it covered nearby. Should you freeze it?
opts: Freeze the puck immediately to kill the clock — any stoppage you can get late in a one-goal game is good because it burns time off the clock.
Leave it for your team — freezing it gives the other team a faceoff in your zone with 90 seconds left, which is dangerous.
Kick the puck away from the front of your net — send it anywhere away from the danger area and let your team sort out possession after.
Throw the puck to the corner with your glove — get it out of the slot area and let your defensemen go retrieve it behind the goal line.
U11 / Atom.why: Late game freeze decisions matter. Freezing the puck in your own zone gives the other team a faceoff set play — potentially with their best players — in your zone. If your team has control, leaving it lets them clear and kill more clock.
U11 / Atom.tip: Leading late? Don't automatically freeze. If your team has it, let them clear. Faceoffs in your zone are dangerous.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker carries the puck behind your net at speed, going from your left to your right. They're about to try a wraparound. What do you do?
opts: Stay in the middle of your crease and wait for the puck to appear on the other side.
Move with them — push to the right post as they come around and seal it. Don't let them get the post.
Come out past the crease to stop them.
Drop into the butterfly right away and slide across behind you.
U11 / Atom.why: On a wraparound, the goalie moves with the puck carrier and seals the near post as they come around. If you get to the post before they do, there's no open net to stuff the puck into.
U11 / Atom.tip: Wraparound coming? Move with them. Seal the post before they get there.

U11 / Atom.sit: Put the steps of a basic breakout in the correct order.
items: D retrieves the puck behind the net
Wingers get up the boards and create outlet options
D makes a pass to the open winger
Center supports through the middle of the ice
U11 / Atom.why: A clean breakout starts with the D retrieving the puck, wingers getting open along the boards, then the D makes a pass, and the center fills through the middle for support.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breakout: D gets it, wingers get open, pass, center supports. In that order.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. Put these steps in the correct order.
items: Puck carrier enters the zone with speed and draws the first defender
Wide players drive the net and get open
Puck carrier reads the D and passes to the open player
Receiver shoots or makes the next play
U11 / Atom.why: A 3-on-2 works when the puck carrier draws attention first, wide lanes get filled, the read is made, and then the shot or next play happens.
U11 / Atom.tip: 3-on-2: draw, fill lanes, read, finish. Speed and sequence.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just got a power play. Put these steps in the right order to get set up in the offensive zone.
items: Win the faceoff or gain possession
Enter the zone with control — carry or pass it in
Get to your assigned PP positions
Move the puck to find the open lane or shot
U11 / Atom.why: A power play starts with possession, then a clean zone entry, getting into formation, and then executing the puck movement to create a chance.
U11 / Atom.tip: PP: win it, enter with control, get set, move the puck. Don't rush the setup.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is rushing up ice toward you. Put the defensive steps in order.
items: Skate backward and establish your gap
Read the attacker's speed and angle
Close the gap as they approach the blue line
Steer them to the outside with your body position
U11 / Atom.why: Good gap control is a sequence: establish position, read the attacker, close as they approach, then steer them wide. Skipping steps gets you beat.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defend the rush in steps: gap, read, close, steer. Don't jump ahead.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is about to take a defensive zone faceoff. Put these steps in order.
items: Know your assignment before the puck drops
Get to your position beside or behind the center
Win the draw back or tie up their center
Get to the puck first and make a quick play out of the zone
U11 / Atom.why: Faceoffs are structured. Know your job, get into position, compete on the draw, then execute. Players who don't know their assignment before the drop are already behind.
U11 / Atom.tip: Faceoff: know your job, get set, compete, execute. Preparation wins draws.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. Put the recovery steps in order.
items: Recognize the turnover immediately
Nearest player pressures the puck carrier
Other forwards sprint back toward the defensive zone
Defensemen hold the blue line or retreat to proper gap
U11 / Atom.why: Transition defense is a chain reaction: recognize, pressure, backcheck, hold structure. The team that recovers fastest gives up the fewest odd-man rushes.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turnover: recognize, pressure, backcheck, structure. Speed of reaction matters most.

U11 / Atom.sit: You need to make a line change during play. Put these steps in the right order.
items: Make sure your team has possession or the puck is in a safe area
Skate toward the bench — don't coast
Get close to the bench before your replacement jumps on
Fresh player jumps on and gets into the play immediately
U11 / Atom.why: A good line change requires timing. Changing at the wrong time or too slowly creates odd-man situations. Possession first, skate hard to the bench, short change, fresh player engages.
U11 / Atom.tip: Line change: safe puck, skate hard to bench, short swap, fresh legs engage.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is starting a cycle in the offensive zone corner. Put the cycle steps in order.
items: Win the puck battle along the boards
Protect the puck with your body and look for support
Pass to a teammate who's in motion along the wall or down low
Supporting players rotate to create the next passing option
U11 / Atom.why: The cycle is a continuous loop: win the battle, protect, pass, rotate. If any step breaks down — especially the rotation — the cycle dies.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cycle: win the battle, protect, pass, rotate. Keep the loop going.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The other team has the puck. One of your teammates skates to the puck instead of covering their player. What's the mistake?
opts: They should have stayed on the bench longer — too many players out there at once causes confusion, so the fix is a quicker line change on the shift.
They left their check open — in the D-zone, everyone needs to have a player covered before chasing the puck.
They should have gone straight to the net front — the slot is the most dangerous area in your zone so that's where everyone needs to be.
They skated too slow on that play — if they had chased harder they would have won the puck battle and taken possession back for the team.
U11 / Atom.why: Chasing the puck in the defensive zone without covering your check first is one of the most common mistakes at U11. It leaves someone wide open for a pass and easy goal.
U11 / Atom.tip: D-zone: cover your check first, then worry about the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate carries the puck into the offensive zone. You're skating hard to join the rush but you cross the blue line before the puck does. What went wrong?
opts: You were skating too slow to keep up with the play — if you had more speed through the neutral zone you would have caught up to the puck.
You entered the zone before the puck offside. You need to time your entry so the puck crosses the line first.
You should have stopped at center ice and waited — a trailing forward should stay back at the red line until the puck is clearly in the zone.
The puck carrier was too slow getting over the line — if they had pushed the pace harder you wouldn't have gotten ahead of the puck at all.
U11 / Atom.why: Offside kills momentum. The player without the puck must make sure the puck enters the zone before they do. Timing your entry is a basic but critical skill.
U11 / Atom.tip: Blue line: puck goes first, then you. Time your entry.

U11 / Atom.sit: On the forecheck, all three forwards go deep into the corner chasing the puck. The opposing D makes a quick pass up the middle and the other team breaks out with speed. What was the mistake?
opts: Back off the forecheck and let the D-zone defense handle it.
All three forwards went deep — at least one forward needs to stay high to prevent the breakout pass through the middle.
The defensemen were too far back at the blue line — they should have stepped up to pinch and kept the puck in the offensive zone right away.
The goalie didn't stop the breakout pass across the ice — goalies should play the puck when their team is forechecking hard in the offensive zone.
U11 / Atom.why: Three forwards deep in the corner means nobody is covering the middle of the ice. The second or third forward needs to stay high to cut off the easy breakout pass.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheck: never all three deep. Someone stays high. Always.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the D at the offensive blue line. The puck is loose along the boards. You pinch down to get it, but the opposing forward gets there first and goes around you for a breakaway. What was the mistake?
opts: You should have pinched harder and finished the check — if you commit to a pinch you have to follow through, so a half pinch is what got beat on the play.
You pinched when the puck wasn't clearly yours to win — if you can't get there first, hold the line instead of gambling.
Your partner should have covered for you — when one D pinches, the other D rotates to the middle, and your partner didn't read the play in time there.
The forward got lucky on the bounce — the puck took a weird bounce off the boards that nobody could have predicted during that battle.
U11 / Atom.why: Pinching is a gamble. If you can't win the race to the puck, you're giving up your position and creating a breakaway. Only pinch when you're confident you'll get there first.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pinch only when you'll win the race. If it's 50/50, hold the line.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team loses the puck in the offensive zone. Two forwards stand and watch for a moment before skating back. The other team gets a 3-on-2 rush. What's the mistake?
opts: The defensemen were out of position when the turnover happened — they were too deep in the offensive zone and couldn't get back in time at all.
The forwards didn't transition immediately — when you lose the puck, you turn and skate back right away, no watching.
The goalie came out too far from the net — they were too aggressive playing the puck and couldn't recover in time to stop the three-on-two rush.
The coach called the wrong play on that shift — the system your team was running left too many forwards deep in the offensive zone area.
U11 / Atom.why: Standing and watching after a turnover is the number one transition mistake. The other team converts turnovers into rushes in seconds. Every moment of standing still is a step they gain on you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lose the puck? Turn and go. Immediately. No watching.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. One of your players keeps chasing the puck carrier around the zone instead of staying in the box formation. What's the mistake?
opts: They should chase even harder — when you're down a skater you need to pressure the puck all over the zone to force a bad pass from their power play.
They're breaking the PK structure — on the penalty kill, stay in your box and force them to make plays around you instead of chasing.
They should go to the bench to change — a tired PK player chasing around is worse than no player, so they need to get off the ice right away.
They need to ice the puck more often to kill time — any chance to dump it down the ice is a good one when you're killing a penalty off.
U11 / Atom.why: The penalty kill box works because it covers passing lanes and takes away the middle of the ice. Chasing the puck opens up holes the power play will exploit.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK: stay in the box. Let them move the puck around you. Don't chase.

U11 / Atom.sit: During a line change, the fresh player jumps on the ice while the tired player is still at center ice skating slowly to the bench. The ref blows the whistle. What happened?
opts: The ref made a bad call on the change — the refs often miss line changes at this level and this one clearly shouldn't have been blown down at all.
Too many players on the ice — the fresh player jumped on before the tired player was close enough to the bench. The outgoing player needs to be within arm's reach of the bench.
Offside at center — the fresh player jumped onto the ice past the red line while the puck was still in the defensive zone, so the play was ruled offside.
Icing on the tired player — the tired player skated past center without playing the puck so the ref blew it down for icing against the team that was changing.
U11 / Atom.why: Too many players is an avoidable penalty. The outgoing player must be close to the bench before the new player jumps on. Communication and awareness prevent this.
U11 / Atom.tip: Line change: don't jump on until the other player is at the bench. Communicate.

U11 / Atom.sit: You pick up the puck along the boards. A defender is coming from your right side. You turn to your right — directly into the defender — and lose the puck. What should you have done?
opts: Turned faster on the turn — if you had whipped your body around quicker the defender wouldn't have caught up and you'd have kept possession.
Turned away from the pressure — to your left — protecting the puck with your body between you and the defender.
Shot the puck immediately off the boards — a quick wrist shot toward the net would have been better than trying to turn into a defender.
Called for help louder from your teammates — a quick yell brings a winger low to support you along the boards before you get hit.
U11 / Atom.why: Turning into pressure is a guaranteed turnover. Always turn away from the check, putting your body between the puck and the defender. Read where the pressure is coming from before you turn.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pressure coming? Turn away from it, not into it. Protect with your body.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a 2-on-1, you should always shoot no matter what the defender does.
U11 / Atom.why: On a 2-on-1, the right play depends on the defender. If they take you, pass. If they take the pass, shoot. Reading the defender is the whole point of a 2-on-1.
U11 / Atom.tip: 2-on-1 is about reading, not always shooting. Defender takes you? Pass. Takes the pass? Shoot.

U11 / Atom.concept: Everyone covers a player in D-zone
U11 / Atom.sit: In the defensive zone, every skater on your team should have an opposing player to cover.
U11 / Atom.why: Defensive zone coverage means every player picks up a check. If someone is left uncovered, the other team has an easy passing option for a scoring chance.
U11 / Atom.tip: D-zone: five skaters, five assignments. Nobody floats.

U11 / Atom.sit: When your team loses the puck, it's okay to wait a second to see what happens before skating back.
U11 / Atom.why: There is no waiting after a turnover. Every second of standing still is a step the other team gains on their rush. Transition back is immediate — always.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turnover means instant backcheck. No waiting. No watching. Go.

U11 / Atom.sit: On the penalty kill, your team should form a box shape to protect the middle of the ice and the slot.
U11 / Atom.why: The penalty kill box takes away the most dangerous area — the slot and middle of the ice. It forces the power play to shoot from the outside where chances are lower.
U11 / Atom.tip: PK box protects the slot. That's the whole idea. Stay in shape.

U11 / Atom.concept: Give lots of room on D
U11 / Atom.sit: As a defenseman, you should give the attacker as much room as possible so they can't get around you.
U11 / Atom.why: Too much gap gives the attacker time and space to make plays. Good gap control means closing the distance so they have less time to decide, while still being able to match their speed.
U11 / Atom.tip: Big gap = free time for the attacker. Close it down. Take away their time.

U11 / Atom.sit: Talking to your teammates on the ice — calling for the puck, calling out open players, warning about pressure — makes your team better.
U11 / Atom.why: Communication is one of the biggest advantages a team can have. Calling 'man on,' 'time,' 'wheel,' or 'open' gives your teammates information they can't see. Quiet teams get surprised.
U11 / Atom.tip: Talk on the ice. Every call helps. Quiet teams lose pucks they didn't need to lose.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a forecheck, all three forwards should go deep into the zone to chase the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: If all three forwards go deep, there's nobody high to cut off the breakout pass or recover if the other team escapes the pressure. At least one forward stays high.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheck: one or two deep, one stays high. Never all three in the corner.

U11 / Atom.sit: When making a line change, it's fine to glide slowly to the bench since your replacement is already coming on.
U11 / Atom.why: Gliding to the bench means you're on the ice longer without contributing and your team is effectively short a player in the play. Skate hard to the bench so the change is fast and clean.
U11 / Atom.tip: Skate hard to the bench. Every second of coasting is a second your team plays short.

U11 / Atom.sit: When protecting the puck, you should keep your body between the puck and the opposing player at all times.
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection is about body position. Your body is a shield — keep it between the defender and the puck, and they can't get it without going through you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck protection = body position. Shield the puck with your body. Simple and effective.

U11 / Atom.sit: As a defenseman, you should never join the rush into the offensive zone.
U11 / Atom.why: Defensemen can and should join the rush when the situation is right — when there's a clear odd-man advantage or your partner is back to cover. Good D join the play; they just do it smartly.
U11 / Atom.tip: D can join the rush — just make sure your partner has you covered. Smart D jump in at the right time.

U11 / Atom.sit: You enter the offensive zone with speed. A defender is square in front of you, but your linemate is wide open on the left wing. What do you do?
opts: Try to deke around the defender one-on-one.
Pass to your open linemate immediately.
Dump the puck in deep and chase after it.
Slow down and wait for more teammate help.
U11 / Atom.why: The defender is committed to you — which means your teammate is open. Defender takes you = give it up.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defender takes you + teammate is open = pass. Almost always.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. The other team has it and is skating the other way. You're the nearest forward. What's your first move?
opts: Stay in the offensive zone in case.
Immediately turn and skate back.
Skate slowly back and watch.
Go to the bench as fast as you can.
U11 / Atom.why: The instant the puck changes hands, your job changes. You go from attacker to backchecker immediately.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turnover = turn around. Don't wait. Go now.

U11 / Atom.sit: You lose a faceoff in your own zone. The other team's center has the puck. What's your immediate priority?
opts: Argue with the referee.
Go pressure the puck carrier immediately.
Skate back to the bench.
Stand still and watch the play unfold.
U11 / Atom.why: Lost faceoffs happen — what matters is the reaction. Going immediately to pressure the puck forces a mistake or delays the play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lost the draw? Go hard immediately. The puck is right there.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your defenseman picks up the puck in your own corner. As a winger, where should you be going?
opts: Stand in the corner with the defenseman and help.
Get up the wall to give the D an outlet option.
Skate straight to center ice and call for a pass.
Stay close to the net in case of a loose rebound.
U11 / Atom.why: Breakout positioning means getting to spots that give your defenseman real options. As a winger, that's typically up the wall.
U11 / Atom.tip: D has the puck in the corner? Get up the wall. Give them an out.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're a defender and an opposing player is skating toward you with the puck, 20 feet away. What's right?
opts: Skate straight at them as fast as you can and try to knock the puck loose.
Back up and maintain a steady gap while angling them toward the boards.
Turn around and skate backward immediately all the way back to your crease.
Stand still and hold your ground.
U11 / Atom.why: Gap control means not giving up too much — but also not closing too fast. Skating straight at a puck carrier lets them pull the puck past you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Don't rush a puck carrier. Control your gap. Make them go where you want.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in your own zone. A forechecker is right on you. Your D partner is five feet away and completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to skate out yourself past the forechecker.
Pass immediately to your open D partner.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice.
Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
U11 / Atom.why: The open D partner five feet away is the easy, correct play. Under pressure the simple play is almost always right.
U11 / Atom.tip: Open partner right beside you? Give it to them. Simple.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your goalie makes a save and has the puck. The other team has forecheckers coming in. Your winger is breaking back toward the goalie. What should the goalie do?
opts: Hold the puck under the glove and freeze it for a faceoff play.
Pass to the winger breaking back — beat the forecheck.
Shoot the puck all the way down the ice to the far end.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far side winger.
U11 / Atom.why: A goalie with the puck can start the breakout before the forecheck resets. A crisp pass to the returning winger beats the pressure.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie has it and a winger is coming back? Start the breakout fast.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is attacking 3-on-2. Where should the three forwards position themselves?
opts: All three forwards skate together in a tight group up the middle.
Spread out across the ice so the two defenders can't cover everyone.
Two forwards go left wing and the third goes right wing.
One attacker goes in alone while the other two hang back at center ice.
U11 / Atom.why: Spreading out on a rush forces the defenders to make an impossible choice. A tight group lets two players cover all three.
U11 / Atom.tip: Rush attack: spread out. Make the defenders choose.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your defenseman has the puck behind the net. Both wingers are covered. The other defenseman is open on the far side. What's the best option?
opts: Force a pass to a covered winger anyway.
Walk out from behind the net alone and skate.
Pass across to the open defenseman to reset the breakout.
Shoot the puck hard around the boards and change.
U11 / Atom.why: When your outlets are covered, the D-to-D pass behind the net resets the play. It switches the point of attack and gives the other side time to get open.
U11 / Atom.tip: Outlets covered? Go D-to-D. Reset and rebuild.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. The puck is in the left corner. You're the right winger. Where's your best spot?
opts: Go into the left corner to help your teammate win the puck.
Stay on the right side near the net — the weak side.
Skate back to the blue line and be ready for a point shot.
Skate back to center ice so you're ready for a fast break.
U11 / Atom.why: As the weak-side winger, your job is to stay available on the opposite side. Pucks go cross-ice constantly — you need to be ready.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck in the far corner? Stay on your side near the net. That's where the cross-ice pass goes.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just got the puck in your own zone. The other team's forecheckers are still in your end. What do you do with the puck?
opts: Shoot it down the ice to the far end as fast as you can.
Skate it out calmly — the forecheckers are out of position and you have time.
Wait behind your own net for the forecheckers to get tired and regroup.
Pass it back to your goalie and let them freeze it for a faceoff.
U11 / Atom.why: When the other team's forecheckers are caught deep in your zone, they're out of position. That's your window to carry the puck out with speed.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheckers caught deep? Carry it out. You have time.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're in the offensive zone with the puck. You have a shooting lane but your teammate is streaking to the net completely unmarked. The goalie hasn't seen them yet. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you already have the lane open.
Pass to your unmarked teammate cutting to the net.
Dump the puck into the corner.
Hold the puck and stickhandle to wait for a better play.
U11 / Atom.why: An unmarked teammate cutting to the net with the goalie unaware is a higher-percentage chance than your open shot. The selfless play is also the smart play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate cutting to the net unmarked? Give it up. That's the better chance.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1 against you. The puck carrier has a teammate to their right. What's the most important thing you do?
opts: Rush hard at the puck carrier to try to poke the puck away before the pass can be made.
Take away the pass — stay in the middle, force the shooter, and let the goalie handle the shot.
Chase the trailing player and leave.
Back up all the way to your own crease and let the goalie handle the two attackers alone.
U11 / Atom.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender's job is to take away the pass. If you rush the puck carrier, the pass kills you. Force the shot — your goalie is ready for that.
U11 / Atom.tip: 2-on-1 as D: take away the pass. Make them shoot. That's your job.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is forechecking. Two forwards are already deep in the offensive zone. You're the third forward. Where do you set up?
opts: Skate deep into the zone with the other two forwards and forecheck hard.
Stay at the top of the zone near the blue line as the safety.
Head to the bench for a quick change.
Skate down to the half-wall spot.
U11 / Atom.why: The third forward on a forecheck is the safety valve. If you all go deep, you leave yourself exposed to a quick breakout and a 3-on-2 against you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Two forwards deep on the forecheck? You're the safety. Hold the blue line.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just won a puck battle in the corner in your own zone. The other team has four players still deep in your end. You have speed. What's the call?
opts: Reset and regroup behind your own net first.
Go — skate it out fast and attack while they're out of position.
Pass to your D and let them carry it up the ice and decide.
Shoot it down the ice and chase.
U11 / Atom.why: When you win a puck battle and the other team is caught deep, that's a transition opportunity. Going immediately puts them on their heels.
U11 / Atom.tip: Other team caught deep? Go now. Transition kills before they reset.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck behind your own net. A forechecker is coming at you hard. You have a defenseman open to your left. What do you do?
opts: Try to outskate the forechecker and carry it yourself up the wall fast.
Quickly pass to the open defenseman before the forechecker arrives.
Stop behind the net and wait it out.
Shoot the puck down the ice yourself.
U11 / Atom.why: Decision timing is everything. The forechecker closing on you is information — it tells you to move the puck before you get pressured.
U11 / Atom.tip: See pressure coming? Move the puck before it gets there.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck near the goal line, way out to the side. The goalie has the near post covered completely. A teammate is at the far post. What do you do?
opts: Shoot from where you are — any shot on net is a good shot from this angle for sure.
Pass across to your teammate at the far post — the goalie can't get there in time.
Skate to center ice and take the shot.
Dump the puck behind the net and go fight for it in the corner with your teammate.
U11 / Atom.why: Shooting from the goal line with the near post completely covered gives the goalie an easy save. A far-post pass forces the goalie to move across the entire crease.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie has your side? Pass across. Make them move all the way.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck along the right boards toward your net. You're the defender. What's the goal of your positioning?
opts: Stop them cold by skating straight at them and poking the puck off their stick quickly.
Angle your body to steer them along the boards and away from the middle of the ice.
Back up all the way to your crease and let the goalie handle it.
Wait for them to shoot from a bad angle and then block the shot with your body fast.
U11 / Atom.why: Angling means steering the puck carrier where you want them — along the boards and away from the dangerous middle of the ice.
U11 / Atom.tip: Angling: push them to the boards. Keep them out of the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D picks up the puck. The strong-side winger is covered. The weak-side winger is open on the far side. What's the breakout call?
opts: Force the pass to the covered strong-side winger and hope they win it.
Pass to the open weak-side winger — they're the clean option.
Skate it out yourself.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far-side corner area.
U11 / Atom.why: A covered winger is not an option. The clean outlet is the open player — even if they're on the far side. Read the ice, not just the default option.
U11 / Atom.tip: Strong side is covered? Go weak side. Find the open player.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate is about to take a shot from the point. Nobody is near the net. What should you do right now?
opts: Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide of the net.
Skate hard to the front of the net and get ready for a rebound.
Back up to the blue line and be ready to defend a fast break.
Call for a pass instead of letting them take a long point shot.
U11 / Atom.why: Getting to the net before the shot arrives means you're in position for a rebound before the goalie can cover it. Net-front presence wins games.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate about to shoot? Crash the net. Get there before the puck does.

U11 / Atom.sit: You have the puck in your own zone. A defender is pressuring you. Your teammate is breaking into the neutral zone. They're 40 feet away but wide open. What's the read?
opts: Dump the puck out along the boards and chase it yourself.
Attempt the stretch pass to the breaking teammate.
Skate it out yourself through the pressure.
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for a better option.
U11 / Atom.why: A stretch pass to a breaking forward turns a defensive zone puck into an immediate odd-man rush. The risk is the pass — but if the lane is open, it's the highest-reward play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breaking teammate wide open? Thread the stretch pass. Big reward if you make it.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're defending in your own zone. Your D partner goes behind the net to get a puck. A forward is crashing to the net and nobody is covering them. What do you do?
opts: Wait for your D partner to come back out from behind the net.
Call out the open player to your teammates and cover them yourself.
Leave them alone — the goalie has a good view and will make the save.
Stand in front of your own net and let the forward battle your goalie.
U11 / Atom.why: When your D partner leaves to get the puck, you need to cover the open attacker. Calling it out also helps your team organize.
U11 / Atom.tip: D partner left? You cover the open player. Call it out loud.

U11 / Atom.sit: An attacker is rushing the puck at you at full speed. You're the only defender. The attacker is fast. What do you do?
opts: Charge at them at full speed to meet them until someone tells you otherwise.
Back up at a controlled pace.
Turn around and race them to your net.
Stand completely.
U11 / Atom.why: Charging a fast attacker lets them blow past you. Backing up at a controlled pace while angling forces them to slow down or go where you want.
U11 / Atom.tip: Fast attacker coming? Back up controlled. Don't chase. Angle them.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D picks up the puck. Both wingers are tied up by forecheckers. But your center is open in the middle of the ice. What's the breakout call?
opts: Force the pass to one of the covered wingers along the wall.
Pass to the open center in the middle — they're clean.
Carry the puck out yourself through the pressure.
Rim the puck around the boards to the far corner.
U11 / Atom.why: The center in the middle is often the hidden option on a breakout. If both wingers are covered, the center up the middle is the smart outlet.
U11 / Atom.tip: Both wingers covered? The center in the middle might be the cleanest option.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck turns over in the neutral zone and now the other team has a 2-on-2 going toward your net. You're one of the two defenders. What's your priority?
opts: Both defenders chase the puck carrier hard and try to strip the puck before they get a shot off.
Take your side — one defender takes the carrier, the other takes the second attacker — and protect the middle.
Both defenders collapse down into the crease and pack the net to block every shot attempt.
Skate straight back to the goal line and let the goalie handle the 2-on-2 rush on their own.
U11 / Atom.why: A 2-on-2 defense requires each defender to take a side. The most dangerous play is a middle pass — stay in the lanes and make them go wide.
U11 / Atom.tip: 2-on-2 against you: each take a side. Protect the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team is entering the offensive zone. The defender is set at their blue line — they're not going to let you carry it in. What's the best entry option?
opts: Carry the puck in at full speed and try to blow right past the defender with pure speed.
Chip the puck into the zone behind the defender and skate hard to get it first.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your teammates.
Pass it back to the D and regroup.
U11 / Atom.why: A set defender at the blue line means you probably can't carry it in cleanly. Chipping the puck into space behind them and racing to get it first beats the defender's positioning.
U11 / Atom.tip: Defender set at the line? Chip it in and go get it. Don't force the carry.

U11 / Atom.sit: You don't have the puck in the offensive zone and your teammate is being pressured. The defender covering you is watching the puck. What do you do?
opts: Stand still in your spot and call loudly for the puck so your teammate knows you are open.
Skate to a new open spot — cut to the net or find a seam. Your defender will have to follow.
Skate over toward your covered teammate to give them a close support pass option quickly.
Wait right where you are until the pressure clears so you don't crowd your teammate out.
U11 / Atom.why: Moving without the puck forces your defender to follow you — and every step they take to follow you is a step away from the puck carrier. Create problems by moving.
U11 / Atom.tip: No puck and defender watching the play? Move. Make them follow you. Create space.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate just won a puck battle in the corner in the defensive zone. They have the puck but defenders are still around them. You're nearby without a defender on you. What do you do?
opts: Skate away to center ice.
Get close enough to be an easy pass — support behind or beside them.
Call for a long stretch pass up to the blue line and break out fast.
Stand still and watch the battle play out for yourself.
U11 / Atom.why: When a teammate wins a puck battle in a tough spot, they need immediate support. Getting close gives them a safe, quick outlet before the next pressure arrives.
U11 / Atom.tip: Teammate won the puck battle? Get close and give them a quick option.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your D picks up the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are coming but they haven't arrived yet. You have 2 seconds. What's the key thing your D should do?
opts: Wait until both forecheckers have arrived before deciding which outlet pass to try to make.
Look up immediately and read which outlet is open before the pressure arrives — decide early.
Rim the puck around the boards no matter what the forecheckers are doing on that play.
Skate out of the zone with the puck no matter what pressure is coming from the forecheckers.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading the breakout before the pressure arrives — deciding early — is the difference between a clean exit and getting trapped. The D needs to see the ice before the forechecker takes the options away.
U11 / Atom.tip: D with the puck and pressure coming? Look up and decide early. Don't wait.

U11 / Atom.sit: A forward is coming at you on a breakaway. You're standing on your goal line. What should you do with your position?
opts: Stay flat on your goal line.
Skate out toward the shooter to cut the angle — give them less net to shoot at.
Skate all the way out past your blue line to challenge the shooter far from the net.
Lie flat down on the ice right away and hope the shooter misses the net entirely.
U11 / Atom.why: Coming out toward the shooter cuts down the angle — the shooter sees less net. A goalie who stays on the goal line gives the shooter a huge target. Move out and take space away.
U11 / Atom.tip: Come out toward the shooter. Take away the angle. Give them less net.

U11 / Atom.sit: Two attackers are coming at your lone defenseman on a 2-on-1. The puck carrier has the puck. What's the goalie's job?
opts: Skate out of the crease to challenge the puck carrier and cut down their shooting angle before they can fire a shot at you.
Stay in your crease, be ready for a shot, and watch for the pass — the D takes away the pass, you take the shot.
Yell at your defenseman to do something.
Come out past the crease to the top.
U11 / Atom.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender takes away the pass and the goalie takes the shot. The goalie stays set in the crease and is ready for either — but primarily prepared for the shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: 2-on-1: your D takes the pass, you take the shot. Stay set and read it.

U11 / Atom.sit: A shot is coming low along the ice. What's the most effective way to stop it?
opts: Try to kick the puck away with one foot while standing up on your skates the whole time.
Drop into butterfly position — both knees down, pads flared out to cover the bottom.
Fall sideways quickly.
Stand straight up and hope that the shot hits you somewhere on your leg pads or chest.
U11 / Atom.why: The butterfly position drops both pads to the ice and covers the bottom of the net effectively. Low shots along the ice are best stopped this way.
U11 / Atom.tip: Low shot coming? Butterfly. Pads down, cover the bottom of the net.

U11 / Atom.sit: You make a save but you can direct where the rebound goes. Where should you try to send it?
opts: Straight back into the slot where a teammate might be.
Into the corner where your team can retrieve it.
Over the boards and out of play to stop the attack.
Anywhere far from the net and away from trouble.
U11 / Atom.why: Directing rebounds to the corner keeps the puck away from the most dangerous area — the slot. Your team can retrieve it in the corner and start a breakout.
U11 / Atom.tip: Control your rebounds. Aim for the corners, not the middle.

U11 / Atom.sit: A player from the other team is standing right in front of you, blocking your view of a point shot. The puck is about to be shot. What do you do?
opts: Give up on seeing the puck and just hope the shot hits some part of your equipment cleanly.
Move laterally to try to find a sightline, and be ready to react to tips and deflections.
Skate hard into the screener to knock them out of the way before the shot gets released.
Call for your teammates to clear the screener and wait for them to move the player away.
U11 / Atom.why: Screeners are a real problem but not impossible. Moving slightly helps you find the puck, and being ready for a deflection means you're not just frozen behind the screen.
U11 / Atom.tip: Screener in front? Move to find the puck. Expect a tip. React.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck comes behind your net. Your winger is coming back to get it but they're a second away. You can get there first. Should you play it?
opts: Never touch the puck because it's too risky and leaving the crease always gives the other team a chance to score on the empty net.
Yes — if you can get there first and your winger is a step away, stop it for them so they have a clean pickup.
Only if the other team definitely isn't coming for the puck too and you have a completely clear path behind your net to play it.
Kick it with your skate to the corner.
U11 / Atom.why: Goalies who can handle the puck are a huge asset. If you can get to it first and stop it for your winger, you've started the breakout and taken pressure off your defense.
U11 / Atom.tip: Comfortable behind the net? Play it. Stop it for your winger. Be an extra D.

U11 / Atom.sit: A player is on a breakaway coming straight at you. They're moving fast. When should you commit to going down?
opts: Right away as soon as they cross your blue line — drop into the butterfly immediately and wait.
Wait until they've made their move — commit when you see what they're going to do, not before.
Never go down at all — always stay up on your skates no matter what the shooter chooses to do.
As soon as they cross the blue line so you are ready for the shot before it gets released.
U11 / Atom.why: The goalie who commits too early loses. A patient goalie who waits for the shooter's move before committing forces the shooter to beat them rather than just beat the commit.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breakaway: be patient. Make them show you something first. Then commit.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck is in the corner to your left. An attacker has it but hasn't shot. Where should you be in your net?
opts: In the middle of the net.
Touching the left post — take away the near side.
All the way across touching the far right post.
Out of the crease to cut the angle.
U11 / Atom.why: When the puck is in the corner, the goalie moves to the near post. This takes away the short side — the most dangerous option for that angle. The long side is much harder to score from.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck in the corner? Move to the near post. Take away the short side.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck is bouncing in the crease area and both you and your defenseman are going for it. What should you say?
opts: Say nothing and grab it.
Yell 'Mine!' loud and clear so your defenseman knows to get out of the way.
Tap them on the shoulder pad.
Wait until after the play is over to talk about who should have gone for the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: 'Mine!' is one of the most important calls in hockey. It tells your defender to clear out and lets you take control of the puck without a collision or confusion.
U11 / Atom.tip: 'Mine!' — say it loud and say it early. Own your crease.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team's goalie stops the puck. Put the basic breakout steps in order.
items: Goalie leaves the puck for the defenseman or makes a short pass
Wings skate to their lanes along the boards
D picks up the puck and looks up for an option
D passes to the open winger and the team moves up ice
U11 / Atom.why: A breakout starts with the goalie, then wings get open, D retrieves and reads, then makes the pass. Everybody has a job and the order matters.
U11 / Atom.tip: Breakout: goalie starts it, wings get open, D reads, pass and go.

U11 / Atom.sit: You receive a pass in the offensive zone. Put the steps for taking a good shot in order.
items: Receive the pass and control the puck
Get your head up and pick your target
Get the puck into shooting position on your stick
Release the shot at the target
U11 / Atom.why: A good shot starts with a clean reception. Then you look where to shoot, get the puck ready, and release. Rushing past the target-picking step means you're just throwing it at the net.
U11 / Atom.tip: Catch it clean, pick your spot, set it up, let it go. Good shots follow a sequence.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just intercepted a pass in your own zone. Put the transition to offense in order.
items: Recognize you have the puck — transition starts
Puck carrier looks up for options
Forwards sprint up ice to create passing lanes
Pass ahead to a forward with speed through the neutral zone
U11 / Atom.why: Fast transitions catch the other team off guard. The moment you get the puck, everyone's job changes — recognize it, read options, create lanes, and move the puck quickly.
U11 / Atom.tip: Got the puck? Transition now. Forwards go. Move it fast while they're caught flat.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team enters the offensive zone with the puck. Put the positioning steps in order.
items: Puck carrier drives wide or deep
One forward goes to the net for screens and rebounds
Third forward supports high — near the top of the circles
Defensemen hold the blue line and keep the puck in
U11 / Atom.why: Good offensive zone structure means everyone has a role. Puck goes deep, someone covers the net, someone supports high, and D hold the line. Structure creates chances.
U11 / Atom.tip: In the O-zone: puck deep, player at the net, support high, D on the line. Structure.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're carrying the puck through the neutral zone toward the offensive blue line. Put your decision-making steps in order.
items: Read the defender's position at the blue line
Check if teammates are available for a pass
Decide: carry it in, pass it in, or dump and chase
Execute your choice with speed and commitment
U11 / Atom.why: Zone entry is a read, not a guess. Read the D, check your options, pick the best one, and commit. Players who don't read first make bad entries.
U11 / Atom.tip: At the blue line: read, check options, decide, commit. Don't guess — read.

U11 / Atom.sit: You should always dump the puck into the offensive zone instead of carrying it in.
U11 / Atom.why: Dumping is one option, but if you have speed and space, carrying it in keeps possession and creates better chances. Read the situation — dump when you have to, carry when you can.
U11 / Atom.tip: Dump or carry? Read the defense. Carry when you can, dump when you must.

U11 / Atom.sit: Forwards should skate back hard to help on defense when the other team has the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: Backchecking is every forward's responsibility. A forward who doesn't backcheck leaves the team short in the defensive zone and creates odd-man rushes against.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forwards backcheck. Period. It's not optional. Your team needs you back there.

U11 / Atom.sit: You should always aim for the top corner of the net when you shoot.
U11 / Atom.why: Top corner is a great target, but low shots create rebounds, five-hole shots catch goalies off guard, and sometimes a quick release matters more than perfect placement. Read the situation.
U11 / Atom.tip: Top corner isn't the only option. Low shots, far side, five-hole — read the goalie and pick the best target.

U11 / Atom.sit: In the offensive zone, your team should spread out and use the whole ice instead of bunching up near the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: Spreading out creates passing lanes, opens up space, and makes it harder for the defense to cover everyone. Bunching up makes the defense's job easy — they can cover everyone at once.
U11 / Atom.tip: Use the whole ice on offense. Spread out. Bunching up helps the other team, not yours.

U11 / Atom.sit: When breaking out of your own zone, passing up the middle of the ice is always the safest play.
U11 / Atom.why: The middle of the ice in your own zone is the most dangerous area for a turnover. A picked-off pass in the middle goes straight to the slot. The boards are the safe breakout route.
U11 / Atom.tip: Middle ice in your own zone is danger. Use the boards to break out safely.

U11 / Atom.sit: When your team loses the puck, you should immediately start skating back toward your own net.
U11 / Atom.why: Transition defense is instant. Every second you wait is a step the other team gains. The fastest backchecking teams give up the fewest goals on the rush.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lose the puck? Instant backcheck. No thinking. No watching. Go.

U11 / Atom.q: You've got the puck in the right corner. Tap every teammate who's in a dangerous scoring spot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Scoring spots are the slot and the top of the crease. Wingers below the goal line and D at the blue line aren't dangerous right now.

U11 / Atom.q: You've got the puck at the blue line. Which passing lane to your winger is open?
U11 / Atom.tip: Read the defender's stick. The lane that doesn't cross a stick is the pass.

lanes.msg: Defender's stick is right across this lane. It'll get picked off.
Clean lane — pass curls below the defender's stick.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate has the puck along the wall in the offensive zone. You're nearby. What should you do?
opts: Find open ice within one pass.
Skate behind the net, out of sight.
Yell at them to shoot.
Stand still and watch.
U11 / Atom.why: Puck support means giving the carrier an easy outlet. Open yourself up one pass away so they don't have to force it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Be an option, not a spectator.

U11 / Atom.sit: Good puck support means skating AWAY from your teammate who has the puck so you don't crowd them.
U11 / Atom.why: Too far away and you're not a pass option. Stay close enough to receive — usually about one stick-length to a half-rink away, depending on the play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Close enough to help, not so close you crowd.

U11 / Atom.sit: A teammate is battling for the puck in the corner. Where's the best spot to support them?
opts: A few feet above them, ready for a pass out.
Right on top of them in the corner.
Behind your goalie.
At the other team's blue line.
U11 / Atom.why: Corner battles need an outlet up high. Sit a few feet above, ready for the chip-up. Two people in the corner = wasted bodies.
U11 / Atom.tip: Corner battle? Sit above it.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The defender drifts toward YOU. What's the right play?
opts: Pass to your teammate.
Shoot high.
Dump and chase.
Stop and wait.
U11 / Atom.why: If the D takes you, they're not taking the pass. Move the puck across and your teammate has a look.
U11 / Atom.tip: D on you → pass.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The defender stays in the middle, taking away the pass. What's the play?
opts: Shoot — go to the net yourself.
Force the pass anyway.
Pass back to your D.
Pull up and wait.
U11 / Atom.why: If the defender commits to the passing lane, they're giving you the shot. Take it.
U11 / Atom.tip: D on the pass → shoot.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a 2-on-1 you should always pass no matter what the defender does.
U11 / Atom.why: Reading the defender is the whole point. Pass OR shoot depending on who they take — not a default.
U11 / Atom.tip: Read first, then decide.

U11 / Atom.sit: The puck drops at center ice and bounces to your stick. First move?
opts: Look up and find a teammate.
Skate to the bench.
Shoot it down the ice.
Freeze the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: Every breakout starts with head up. Look first, then make the read.
U11 / Atom.tip: Head up before you move the puck.

U11 / Atom.sit: The best skaters take 2–3 hard strides right away — they don't glide.
U11 / Atom.why: Short hard strides out of the blocks win races for the puck and give you speed to protect or attack.
U11 / Atom.tip: Hard strides first. Glide later.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're in a tight corner with the puck. You want to escape. How do you start?
opts: Low, explosive first steps.
Long smooth glide.
Slow backward crossovers.
Stop and wait.
U11 / Atom.why: In tight spaces you need quick separation — stay low and explode off your edges.
U11 / Atom.tip: Low + explosive = escape.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate is winding up from the point. You're the net-front forward. What do you do?
opts: Plant in front of the goalie for a screen or tip.
Skate to the far boards.
Go to the bench.
Raise your stick above your head.
U11 / Atom.why: Net-front is a job. Screen the goalie, be ready to tip, and hunt the rebound. That's where ugly goals are born.
U11 / Atom.tip: Point shot = plant at the net.

U11 / Atom.sit: A good net-front forward keeps their stick on the ice even after the shot is taken.
U11 / Atom.why: Rebounds pop out fast. Stick on the ice = you can tip, redirect, or jam — all with no extra motion.
U11 / Atom.tip: Stick down, always.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're at the net-front and the defender crosschecks you out of position. What's the smart play?
opts: Re-establish position fast, keep the stick down.
Fall to the ice and stay there.
Skate away and pout.
Drop your stick and yell.
U11 / Atom.why: Good net-front guys get moved, then get back. Don't waste the shift. Reset, stick down, hunt the rebound.
U11 / Atom.tip: Moved? Get back.

U11 / Atom.sit: Where should you aim a pass to a skating teammate?
opts: A stick-length ahead of where they are now.
Right at their current skates.
Behind them, at their heels.
At the boards.
U11 / Atom.why: Lead your teammate. A pass goes to where they'll be, not where they are.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pass to space, not the player.

U11 / Atom.sit: A hard, flat pass is usually better than a soft, floating pass.
U11 / Atom.why: Hard flat passes are easier to handle, harder to intercept, and faster to the target. Soft floaters are picked off.
U11 / Atom.tip: Flat and firm. Not soft.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're about to pass and you notice a defender in the passing lane. What do you do?
opts: Delay or find a different teammate.
Fire it through anyway.
Pass it into the defender.
Throw your stick.
U11 / Atom.why: Passes into defenders become their rush the other way. If the lane isn't open, reset and find a new option.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lane blocked? Reset.

U11 / Atom.sit: A teammate makes a mistake that leads to a goal against. First move on the bench?
opts: Tap their pads and say 'next shift.'
Yell at them.
Roll your eyes.
Turn your back.
U11 / Atom.why: Teams that pick each other up score more the rest of the game. Teams that tear each other down lose fast.
U11 / Atom.tip: Mistake? Pick them up.

U11 / Atom.sit: It's a teammate's job to set you up — you don't have to pass back or look for them after.
U11 / Atom.why: Good teams move the puck both ways. If a teammate sets you up, you look for them next time. That's trust.
U11 / Atom.tip: Give it to get it.

U11 / Atom.sit: Coach benches you unexpectedly. What's the team-first response?
opts: Cheer for the players on the ice.
Slam the gate.
Refuse to answer questions.
Skate to the bathroom.
U11 / Atom.why: Bench energy matters. Cheering teammates on — especially when you're frustrated — is captain behavior.
U11 / Atom.tip: Benched? Cheer louder.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're on defense and the play is moving laterally along the blue line. What stride helps you stay with it?
opts: Crossovers — stay square with power to either side.
Straight-line skating.
Snowplow stops.
One-foot glide.
U11 / Atom.why: Crossovers let a defender mirror lateral puck movement without losing power or losing balance.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lateral play = crossovers.

U11 / Atom.sit: Backwards skating is just as important as forward skating for defensemen.
U11 / Atom.why: D-men spend most of their shift skating backwards or in transition. Backward skating, pivots, and crossovers are the core.
U11 / Atom.tip: D = backward machine.

U11 / Atom.sit: What's the most efficient way to change directions quickly?
opts: Edge work — dig in and push off.
Slow down, then turn.
Stop completely.
Jump.
U11 / Atom.why: Inside and outside edges are how fast players change directions without losing speed.
U11 / Atom.tip: Edges win races.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate passes to you on the half-wall in the offensive zone. What do you usually do next?
opts: Protect the puck and cycle it low or find the next option.
Shoot from the wall.
Skate to the point.
Dump it out of the zone.
U11 / Atom.why: The cycle is about maintaining possession. Protect, look, and move the puck low or back — wear defenders out.
U11 / Atom.tip: Protect → look → move.

U11 / Atom.sit: In a cycle, the most important job is to keep moving your feet so you're always a pass option.
U11 / Atom.why: Stationary cyclers die. Keep feet moving, support the carrier, give them an outlet with speed.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cycle = feet always moving.

U11 / Atom.sit: The defender pins your teammate on the boards. What's the cycle read?
opts: Sit above and be the outlet.
Crash the net.
Go back to the point.
Head to the bench.
U11 / Atom.why: Pinned teammate needs a release valve. Sit a stick's length above the battle — not in it — and take the chip-up.
U11 / Atom.tip: Pinned teammate → outlet above.

U11 / Atom.sit: Your team just scored to tie the game. What's the best mindset for your next shift?
opts: Stay smart — don't chase the go-ahead goal with risky plays.
Force everything into the net.
Celebrate longer at the bench.
Play dump-and-chase only.
U11 / Atom.why: After a big goal, the team that stays disciplined usually wins. Don't chase the next one and gift the other team a rush.
U11 / Atom.tip: Tie game = stay disciplined.

U11 / Atom.sit: A player with high hockey IQ thinks about where the puck is going, not just where it is.
U11 / Atom.why: Anticipation is the difference between chasing and controlling. See the play a half-second ahead.
U11 / Atom.tip: See the next play, not the current one.

U11 / Atom.sit: It's the end of the game and you're up 1-0. What's a smart play in your offensive zone?
opts: Keep the puck low and cycle — eat clock.
Take a flashy shot from the point.
Try a saucer pass through the slot.
Dump it out of the zone.
