U11 / Atom.why: Up a goal late = game management. Cycle low, eat clock, make them come get it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Up a goal late = cycle low.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're at the side of the net with the puck. The goalie is sprawled. Best shot?
opts: Tuck it into the empty top corner.
Slap shot at the goalie.
Pass it back to the point.
Skate behind the net.
U11 / Atom.why: Empty net = calm, accurate, top corner. Don't overthink it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie down = top corner, calm.

U11 / Atom.sit: A quick release matters more than shot power for most goals.
U11 / Atom.why: Goalies mostly track shots they see cleanly. Quick release on and off your stick = they don't have time to react.
U11 / Atom.tip: Quick release > raw power.

U11 / Atom.sit: You're in the slot. Your shot is blocked. What's the next play?
opts: Get the rebound or fake and shoot again.
Skate back to the bench.
Blame your stick.
Freeze the puck.
U11 / Atom.why: Shots get blocked. Good shooters stay hunting — track the rebound or reset the shot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Blocked? Hunt it again.

U11 / Atom.q: Your D has the puck behind the net on a breakout. Which winger is the best outlet?
U11 / Atom.tip: The winger who's unchecked AND parallel to the goal line is the safest pass.

lanes.msg: ✓ Wide open — unchecked LW on the wall.
Covered — the forechecker is right there.

U11 / Atom.q: The puck carrier is cutting to the slot. You're the weak-side D. Where should you be?
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side D protects the net front. Stand between the puck and the backdoor, stick in the passing lane.

targets.feedback: ✓ Net-front seal. Stick in the backdoor lane, body between puck and net.
You just doubled up on the puck carrier — nobody's at the net. Goalie's alone.

U11 / Atom.q: You're the D, ringing the puck around the boards. Which winger is the open outlet?
U11 / Atom.tip: The outlet without a forechecker on top is the safe pass. Don't force the strong-side wall.

lanes.msg: Clean rim — RW is alone with the wall as a passing line.
Forechecker's already on the LW. That's a turnover at your own blue line.

U11 / Atom.q: You're walking the puck at the point. The strong-side feed is shadowed. What's open?
U11 / Atom.tip: Read both defenders. The lane through the seam — between two coverage triangles — is the chance.

lanes.msg: C is shadowed. The defender's stick eats this pass on the way.
Clean seam to the LW low in the circle — that's a one-timer angle.

U11 / Atom.q: Regroup in the neutral zone. Forecheck is pressing the strong side. Where's the safe pass?
U11 / Atom.tip: When the strong side gets pressured, a quick D-to-D resets the attack on the weak side.

lanes.msg: C is locked up. This pass goes through three sticks.
Clean D-to-D reset. Now D2 attacks the weak side with time.

U11 / Atom.q: Puck is in the strong-side corner. Your partner's pinned in the battle. You're weak-side D. Where do you go?
U11 / Atom.tip: Weak-side D owns the net front when the strong side is locked up. Stick in the backdoor lane, eyes both ways.

spots.msg: ✓ Net-front, stick in the backdoor lane. You'll kill any cross-ice feed to X2.
You just chased X2 to the wall — nobody's at the net and a centring pass is a goal.
Too high. You're not helping the corner battle and the slot is wide open.

U11 / Atom.q: Carrier is coming up the wall on a 1-on-1. You're the lone D. Where do you set your gap?
U11 / Atom.tip: Mid-ice angle, stick on puck. Force them outside, don't backpedal to the net.

targets.feedback: ✓ Tight angle, stick on puck, body between carrier and middle ice. They have to dump it or get walled off.
You backed in to the net. Carrier walks across the slot with all day to shoot.

U11 / Atom.q: Your D is winding up for a point shot. You're the net-front F. Where do you set up?
U11 / Atom.tip: Goalie's eyes, not their pads. Off the post, slightly to the strong side, stick on the ice for the tip.

targets.feedback: ✓ In the goalie's eyes, off the post, stick down. You either screen the shot or tip it.
You're behind the net's plane and on the wrong side. No screen, no tip — D might as well shoot at a wall.

U11 / Atom.q: You're F1 forechecking. Their D is retrieving the puck behind the net. What's your angle?
U11 / Atom.tip: Take away one side. Force them up the strong-side wall where F2 is waiting — never give them the middle.

targets.feedback: ✓ Strong-side angle. You force the D up the wall right into F2's pressure. Classic 1–2 forecheck.
You opened the strong-side wall. They reverse the puck weak-side and breakout clean.

U11 / Atom.q: Carrier is wide, picking up speed at centre ice. You're the lone D — where do you set your gap at the blue line?
U11 / Atom.tip: Tight gap, mid-lane. Step up before they cross the line so they can't carry it in clean.

targets.feedback: ✓ Tight gap at the line. They have to chip and chase or take it wide and lose speed.
Backed in 30 feet. They walk in clean with the puck on a string.

U11 / Atom.q: Cycle in your D-zone. Their F1 has the puck below the goal line. You're the strong-side D — where?
U11 / Atom.tip: Stay above the puck, sealed against the boards. Don't get pulled below the goal line.

spots.msg: ✓ Above the puck, between F1 and the slot. You take away the centring lane.
Below the goal line. You can't defend the slot from here — pass to the front is wide open.
Way too high — that's the slot. Nobody's pinning F1 to the wall.

U11 / Atom.q: Their D pinches in. Loose puck on the half-wall, your wing fights it free. You're the weak-side D. Where do you fill?
U11 / Atom.tip: When their D pinches and a forward gets caught up, the weak-side D rotates UP to cover. Don't stay locked at the post.

targets.feedback: ✓ Up to high-slot / point area. You pick up their pinching D before it becomes a chance against.
Stuck at the net — you abandoned the high man. Goalie reads it but the high shot is wide open.

U11 / Atom.q: Point shot incoming. Their F is parked in front of your goalie. Where do you box them out?
U11 / Atom.tip: Body between attacker and the puck. Stick down in their lane. Don't let them turn and tip.

targets.feedback: ✓ Sealed between attacker and goalie. Goalie sees the shot, no tip available.
Behind your own attacker. They turn into the slot and tip the shot in.

U11 / Atom.q: 2-on-1 against, you're the lone D. Their carrier is wide, winger inside. What's your spot?
U11 / Atom.tip: Take away the pass. Goalie has the shooter. Stick in the seam, body in the lane.

targets.feedback: ✓ Centred, stick in the passing lane. Carrier has to shoot — that's the goalie's job.
You jumped the carrier — pass goes cross-ice and X2 has a tap-in.

U11 / Atom.q: Carrier driving the wall in your zone. You're stick-side. Stick on puck or body on body?
U11 / Atom.tip: Stick first. If you commit your body and they cut back, you're skating to the wrong place.

targets.feedback: ✓ Stick on puck, body angled to the wall. They have to dump or get walled off.
All-in body check. They cut back inside and you're chasing. Slot opens up.

U11 / Atom.q: Defensive-zone faceoff loss to your right. You're the right-side D. Where do you go first?
U11 / Atom.tip: Lost faceoff = beat your man to the slot. Don't watch the puck, find your check.

spots.msg: ✓ Box out the high X-D before they can shoot. That's your immediate threat off a lost draw.
C is already on the wall battle. You're doubling up and leaving the high shot wide open.
Goalie covers net. You're looking for the next threat coming in, not standing on the goal line.

U11 / Atom.q: Last man back on a rush. Carrier's flying down the middle. Where do you cut them off?
U11 / Atom.tip: Last forward back is a defender. Take the inside lane — never let them through the middle.

targets.feedback: ✓ Inside, mid-lane. They have to go wide and lose speed — gives the D time to set.
You drifted to the wing. Carrier walks down the middle with all the time in the world.

U11 / Atom.q: Loose puck behind your net. You're retrieving. Your partner is below the dot on the strong side. Where do they need you?
U11 / Atom.tip: If you're retrieving, partner needs to be your safety valve — net front, ready to reverse if forecheck closes.

spots.msg: ✓ Net-front strong-side support. You can reverse to them if the forecheck pins you.
Way too high. You can't reverse to a partner at the point — too far, too risky.
Same side as the forechecker. You'll just hit them with a reverse.

U11 / Atom.q: Forecheck pinning the strong side. Your partner is open across the ice. Best pass?
U11 / Atom.tip: When the strong side gets sealed, D-to-D resets the breakout to the weak side every time.

lanes.msg: Up the wall — F2 is already there. Turnover at the goal line.
✓ D-to-D behind your net. Resets the play, partner has time and space.

U11 / Atom.q: F1 is on top of you. Two outlets — strong-side wall and reverse to behind the net. Which?
U11 / Atom.tip: If the wall is bottled up AND you have a partner behind, REVERSE — quick changes of direction beat brute force.

lanes.msg: Wing is blanketed. Pass dies in coverage.
✓ Reverse to D2 behind the net. Resets pressure, partner has new angle.

U11 / Atom.q: Forechecker between you and your wing on the wall. Wing or middle pass?
U11 / Atom.tip: When the wall is blocked, look middle. Centre is usually open if the forecheck is committed to the wall.

lanes.msg: F1 is right in this lane. Pick-off, breakaway against.
✓ Centre is open in middle ice. Quick pass, then C transitions up.

U11 / Atom.q: You've cycled the puck low and want to feed the slot. Two passing lanes — across or up to the point.
U11 / Atom.tip: If a teammate is unchecked in the slot, that's the chance. The point is the safe reset, not the scoring play.

lanes.msg: ✓ Cross-ice to the slot — C is unchecked. One-timer chance.
Point is the safe play but the slot was wide open. You bailed on the chance.

U11 / Atom.q: Teammate winding up for a point shot. You're crashing the net. Where do you finish your route?
U11 / Atom.tip: Off the post, in the goalie's eyes, stick on the ice. Goalie can't see, you can tip — that's a chance.

targets.feedback: ✓ Off the post, in the eyes. Screen + tip — every D's worst nightmare.
Wrong side, too high. No screen, no tip. Goalie sees it the whole way.

U11 / Atom.q: Point shot's coming. You're a slot fill-in (not the screen). Where do you go?
U11 / Atom.tip: Slot fill-in finds the rebound. Stick down, ready to whack at anything that pops out.

targets.feedback: ✓ Mid-slot, stick down. Rebound territory — most goals come from here.
Behind the net. No rebound goes here — you're invisible to the play.

U11 / Atom.q: Teammate's about to dump the puck deep. You're F1 chasing. What angle do you take?
U11 / Atom.tip: Dump-and-chase, F1 takes the inside lane to cut off the strong-side wall. Force them to reverse or get hit.

targets.feedback: ✓ Inside angle. You arrive with their D, cut off the wall, force a reverse.
Outside angle. Their D walks the puck up the wall untouched.

U11 / Atom.q: You're carrying the cycle low. Their forwards have collapsed. Bump it back up to the point or back into the wall?
U11 / Atom.tip: When their forwards collapse low, the point is wide open. Reset to the D for a clean walk-and-shoot.

lanes.msg: ✓ Up to the D — clean reset. They walk into a quiet point with a shot lane.
Wall pass into a wall of bodies. Turnover at the goal line.

U11 / Atom.q: Walking the line for a shot. The defender's stick is high blocking the slot lane — what's open?
U11 / Atom.tip: If the centre is taken away, walk into the seam between the D and the boards. That's the wide-side shot lane.

lanes.msg: Stick's right in this lane — blocked.
✓ Walk into the wider seam. Shot gets through clean to the goalie's stick side.

U11 / Atom.q: F1 forechecking a retrieval. The carrier already has their head up. Pressure or back off?
U11 / Atom.tip: Head up = they've seen the pass. Don't run at them; take the lane away instead. F1 plays angle, not speed.

targets.feedback: ✓ Cut off the strong-side wall, force them up the boards into F2.
Ran straight at them. They saw it coming, made the easy pass past you.

U11 / Atom.q: F1 is engaging the carrier on the wall. You're F2. What angle do you take in?
U11 / Atom.tip: F2 angles in stick-on-stick — eliminates the pass option even if the carrier escapes F1.

targets.feedback: ✓ Stick-on-stick angle. If carrier gets it past F1, you eat the puck.
You went weak side — there's nobody supporting F1 and any pass back is uncovered.

U11 / Atom.q: 3-on-2 rush. You're the wide winger on the strong side. Where's your route?
U11 / Atom.tip: Wide winger drives the wall, hard. Pulls a D wide and opens up the slot for the centre.

targets.feedback: ✓ Drive the wall hard. Pulls D1 wide, opens the slot for the centre's shot.
You crashed the slot and now there's two attackers fighting for the same lane.

U11 / Atom.q: Rush against. F3 backchecking. Where do you cut into the play to support the D?
U11 / Atom.tip: F3 takes the highest open attacker. Cut through the middle of the rush, not around it.

targets.feedback: ✓ Cut through and pick up X2 — the highest unchecked attacker.
Doubled up on the carrier with D1. X2 and X3 are both unmarked for the cross.

U11 / Atom.q: PK in your zone. They're cycling on the strong side. You're the strong-side PK forward. Where?
U11 / Atom.tip: PK box: strong-side F stays high, takes the strong-side point, doesn't chase below the dot.

targets.feedback: ✓ Strong-side high. You take away the point shot AND the seam pass to the slot.
Chased into the corner — you're now below the puck and out of the box.

U11 / Atom.q: PP one-timer setup. The puck's about to come back to you at the point for the shot. Where do you settle for the release?
U11 / Atom.tip: Off the strong-side hash. Open up to the puck so the one-timer is in stride, not stretching across your body.

targets.feedback: ✓ Strong-side hash, hips open. One-timer is in stride, full velocity.
Weak side. You'd have to reach across your body — the shot's a lob, not a one-timer.

U11 / Atom.q: Offensive zone faceoff WIN clean back. You're the strong-side winger. Where do you go?
U11 / Atom.tip: Off a clean win, strong-side winger crashes the net for screens / tips / rebounds.

targets.feedback: ✓ Crash the net. D is winding up, you're the screen / tip / rebound man.
Drifted to the weak-side point. You're nowhere near the action.

U11 / Atom.q: Up by one with 30 seconds left. They've pulled their goalie. You've got the puck behind your net. Where do you go?
U11 / Atom.tip: Don't ice it — that's a faceoff in your zone. Carry the puck up the wall, eat seconds, only chip if pressured.

targets.feedback: ✓ Up the strong-side wall, controlled. Burns clock without the icing risk.
You iced it. Faceoff in your zone, no goalie for them — recipe for the tying goal.

U11 / Atom.q: Carrier coming at you 1-on-1 up the right wall. Where do you set your gap?
U11 / Atom.tip: Your gap is the distance between you and the carrier. Too tight, they go around you. Too loose, they walk in. Where do you stand to make them choose?

spots.msg: ✓ Tight gap on the wall, stick on the puck, body angled toward the middle. Now the carrier has to chip-and-chase, get walled off, or try a low-percentage cut. You forced the choice.
Backed in toward the net. The carrier walks down the wall with all the time in the world to read the slot, dish to a trailer, or take a shot through traffic.
Lunging in for the poke at the boards. If the carrier pulls the puck back or cuts inside, you are skating where they used to be — now they are behind you with the goalie alone.
You drifted to the middle and let them come up the wall untouched. Without you closing the gap on the wall side, they keep full speed into the slot.

U11 / Atom._source: Hockey Canada LTPD Stage 4 (U11) Defenseman Module: gap control · USA Hockey ADM 11U Defensemen: tight gap, force outside, stick first · Pat Quinn, Coaching Hockey for Dummies — Defending the Rush

U11 / Atom.q: F1 closing on you behind the net. F2 has your strong-side winger sealed. Draw the puck's path to your LW.
U11 / Atom.tip: Forecheckers tell you what is blocked. The fastest play under pressure is rarely a direct pass — sometimes the boards are your best teammate.
U11 / Atom._source: Hockey Canada LTPD Stage 4 (U11) Breakouts module · USA Hockey ADM 11U Defenseman Module: read pressure, rim weak-side · Pat Quinn, Coaching Hockey for Dummies — Breakout Reads

U11 / Atom.sit: You see a defender closing from the right and two teammates ahead. What does "eyes up" let you do here that "eyes down" wouldn't?
opts: Skate faster through the neutral zone
Choose the right pass before the defender takes it away
Make a tighter turn
Stickhandle through traffic
U11 / Atom.why: Eyes-up turns information into a decision. You can see the defender's angle and your two options at once — pick the better pass before he closes the lane. Eyes-down players make the play after the defender forces it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Eyes-up turns information into a decision.

media.alt: Player's eyes are level with the rink, looking up the ice at teammates and defenders — not down at the puck on the stick blade.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Choose the right pass before the defender takes it away

U11 / Atom.sit: Your teammate on the wall is winding up to pass to you. What's the best thing to do RIGHT NOW, before the puck arrives?
opts: Stare at his stick blade so you don't miss the pass
Yell for the puck louder
Check over your shoulder for defenders and open teammates
Skate harder toward the net
U11 / Atom.why: The window before the pass arrives is when you can scan without losing the puck. A shoulder check tells you who's behind you, who's open, and whether you'll have time to shoot or need to move it again.
U11 / Atom.tip: The window before the pass arrives is when you can scan without losing the puck.

media.alt: Before the pass arrives, the player's head is turned to scan the ice — they already know where the puck and pressure are coming from.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Check over your shoulder for defenders and open teammates

U11 / Atom.sit: You scanned and saw a defender closing on your blind side. The pass is coming. What's your best play?
opts: Catch the pass and try to spin off the defender
Catch and move the puck quickly to a teammate or to space — don't hold it
Let the pass go and skate away
Catch the pass and stickhandle
U11 / Atom.why: Knowing pressure is coming changes your play. Move the puck on the first touch instead of trying to beat the defender. The shoulder check is what made this read possible.
U11 / Atom.tip: Knowing pressure is coming changes your play.

U11 / Atom.sit: On a faceoff, what's the problem with standing tall instead of being in athletic stance?
opts: You can't see the puck drop
The ref will throw you out
Your stick is too long
You'll lose the puck — you can't pull it back fast enough or hold position against the other player
U11 / Atom.why: Faceoffs are won on the first move and on body leverage. A tall player has nothing to push against, can't move quickly, and gets pushed off the dot.
U11 / Atom.tip: Faceoffs are won on the first move and on body leverage.

media.alt: Knees are bent, hips are low, weight is centered over the balls of the feet — ready to move in any direction.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: You'll lose the puck — you can't pull it back fast enough or hold position against the other player

U11 / Atom.sit: Why is lunging at the attacker a bad idea when you're defending the slot?
opts: You might trip
The ref will call a penalty
If you miss, you're out of position and the attacker has the lane to the net
It uses too much energy
U11 / Atom.why: Lunging is a one-shot move — if it doesn't work, you're done. Patient stance with stick on ice forces the attacker to make a play around you, which buys time for help.
U11 / Atom.tip: Lunging is a one-shot move — if it doesn't work, you're done.

media.alt: Knees bent, hips back, stick on the ice between the player and the attacker — ready to defend without lunging.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: If you miss, you're out of position and the attacker has the lane to the net

U11 / Atom.sit: Your stick is on the ice in front of you while you defend the slot. What's the stick actually doing?
opts: Taking away the pass to the slot attacker — making the carrier choose another option
Holding you up so you don't fall
Showing the ref you're playing fair
Nothing — it's just a habit
U11 / Atom.why: A defensive stick on the ice is your fifth skater. It blocks the cross-ice pass without you having to move. The carrier is forced to take a worse play.
U11 / Atom.tip: A defensive stick on the ice is your fifth skater.

media.alt: Knees bent, hips back, stick on the ice between the player and the attacker — ready to defend without lunging.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Taking away the pass to the slot attacker — making the carrier choose another option

U11 / Atom.sit: What does keeping your stick flat on the ice take away from the other team?
opts: Their goalie
Their faceoff
Their pass — the stick fills the line between two of their players
Their offside
U11 / Atom.why: Your stick is a literal shadow on the ice. It physically blocks the passing line between the carrier and an open teammate. The carrier sees the lane closed and has to pick a worse play.
U11 / Atom.tip: Your stick is a literal shadow on the ice.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck carrier and the open teammate — taking away the pass without leaving the feet.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Their pass — the stick fills the line between two of their players

U11 / Atom.sit: Of these, which is the best defensive habit?
opts: Stick flat on the ice in the lane, ready to deflect a pass
Stick raised in the air, ready to swing
Stick on your hip while you watch the puck
Stick blade vertical pointed at the carrier
U11 / Atom.why: A flat blade on the ice covers the lane all by itself. Raised, hipped, or vertical sticks give the carrier a free pass through space.
U11 / Atom.tip: A flat blade on the ice covers the lane all by itself.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice in the passing lane between the puck carrier and the open teammate — taking away the pass without leaving the feet.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Stick flat on the ice in the lane, ready to deflect a pass

U11 / Atom.sit: Your stick is flat on the ice as a target — why is that better than holding it up off the ice?
opts: A flat blade catches a pass cleanly; a lifted blade lets the puck slide under and roll away
It's against the rules to lift your stick
It saves energy
It tricks the goalie
U11 / Atom.why: A blade on the ice is both a target AND a catching surface. A blade lifted even an inch lets the puck pass under it — pass missed, possession lost.
U11 / Atom.tip: A blade on the ice is both a target AND a catching surface.

media.alt: Stick blade is flat on the ice presenting a clear target where the player wants to receive the puck.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: A flat blade catches a pass cleanly; a lifted blade lets the puck slide under and roll away

U11 / Atom.sit: You've shielded the puck from a defender on the wall. What's your job NEXT?
opts: Try to score from the wall
Look up and find a teammate cutting to the slot or open space
Hold the puck on the wall as long as possible
Pass the puck back to your goalie
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection isn't the goal — it's the bridge. Once the puck's safe, your eyes go up and find the teammate who's now open because the defender is on you.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck protection isn't the goal — it's the bridge.

media.alt: Player's body is between the defender and the puck — back to the defender, puck on the far side of the body along the boards.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Look up and find a teammate cutting to the slot or open space

U11 / Atom.sit: The defender is reaching around your body trying to poke the puck. Where exactly should the puck be?
opts: On the FAR side of your body from the defender — your torso between him and the puck
On the SAME side as the defender's stick
Right at your skates
Behind your back
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection is geometric. Put your body in the line between the defender's stick and the puck. If the puck is on the same side as the defender, no body in the way = easy poke.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck protection is geometric.

media.alt: Player's body is between the defender and the puck — back to the defender, puck on the far side of the body along the boards.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: On the FAR side of your body from the defender — your torso between him and the puck

U11 / Atom.sit: A forechecker is closing on you fast in the neutral zone. What's your best move?
opts: Skate straight at him to dump and chase
Stop dead and wait
Turn your back to him and shield the puck while you find support
Try to deke around him at full speed
U11 / Atom.why: Turning away buys you two seconds for your support to arrive. Skating into him means a check; deking at speed often means a turnover. Turn, protect, wait for help.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turning away buys you two seconds for your support to arrive.

media.alt: Player has turned their back to the on-coming defender, shielding the puck with the body while looking up ice for support.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Turn your back to him and shield the puck while you find support

U11 / Atom.sit: Why is turning away from a forechecker often better than skating forward through him?
opts: It's the rule in youth hockey
Turning makes him commit to one side, so you can release the puck to support OR move the other way once he passes
It looks fancier
It makes you faster
U11 / Atom.why: Turning is a delay tactic that creates options. The forechecker has to commit to one side; once he does, you have the other side AND the support pass available.
U11 / Atom.tip: Turning is a delay tactic that creates options.

media.alt: Player has turned their back to the on-coming defender, shielding the puck with the body while looking up ice for support.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Turning makes him commit to one side, so you can release the puck to support OR move the other way once he passes

U11 / Atom.sit: You've turned your back and shielded the puck from the forechecker. What should you be looking for next?
opts: A teammate curling for support — pass to him while the forechecker is committed
A way to skate past the forechecker
The bench so you can get a line change
Your goalie
U11 / Atom.why: Puck protection without support is just a stall. The whole point of buying time is so a teammate can curl into a passing lane. Find him, hit him with the pass, keep the play moving.
U11 / Atom.tip: Puck protection without support is just a stall.

media.alt: Player has turned their back to the on-coming defender, shielding the puck with the body while looking up ice for support.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: A teammate curling for support — pass to him while the forechecker is committed

U11 / Atom.sit: What's the best stick blade angle for receiving a pass cleanly?
opts: Flat on the ice and slightly closed (cupped) toward you
Lifted off the ice with the blade open
Vertical pointed at the passer
Held above your shoulder
U11 / Atom.why: Cupped + on the ice = the puck stays under the blade. Open blade lets it bounce up and over. Vertical or lifted = the puck slides under the blade and is gone.
U11 / Atom.tip: Cupped + on the ice = the puck stays under the blade.

media.alt: The puck is meeting the stick blade; the receiver's hands are giving — pulling slightly back with the puck — instead of stabbing at it.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Flat on the ice and slightly closed (cupped) toward you

U11 / Atom.sit: Two players are racing around a curve to get to a loose puck. One glides through the turn; the other does crossovers. Who gets there first?
opts: The glider — less effort
The crossover skater — they're adding speed in the turn
Whoever started closer
It's always a tie
U11 / Atom.why: Crossovers convert each curve into an acceleration zone. Gliding is free coasting — no speed added. In a tied race the crossover wins by a stride or two every time.
U11 / Atom.tip: Crossovers convert each curve into an acceleration zone.

media.alt: Outside skate is crossing over the inside skate as the player rounds a curve — generating speed through the turn instead of gliding.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: The crossover skater — they're adding speed in the turn

U11 / Atom.sit: When you do a crossover around a curve, where should your weight be?
opts: Loaded on the inside edge of your inside skate, with your body leaned slightly into the turn
Centered on both skates, body upright
Leaning AWAY from the turn so you don't fall
All on the outside skate
U11 / Atom.why: Loaded inside edge + lean into the turn = the foundation of a powerful crossover. The inside edge is what you push against; the lean is what lets you accelerate through the curve instead of fighting it.
U11 / Atom.tip: Loaded inside edge + lean into the turn = the foundation of a powerful crossover.

media.alt: Outside skate is crossing over the inside skate as the player rounds a curve — generating speed through the turn instead of gliding.

U11 / Atom._correctAnswerText: Loaded on the inside edge of your inside skate, with your body leaned slightly into the turn

U11 / Atom.sit: You're carrying the puck on a 2-on-1 rush into the offensive zone. The defender is between you and your teammate. What's your best play right now?
opts: Shoot — the goalie is at the top of the crease so a quick wrister has space.
Pass to your teammate on the right — the defender has committed to you and the lane is open.
Deke around the defender to your left and try to go in alone.
Chip the puck behind the defender and skate around him.
U11 / Atom.why: This is a classic pass-vs-shoot read on a 2-on-1. The defender has committed to the puck carrier — body weight is leaning left, stick is extended toward your side, and his momentum is going with you. That means the cross-ice passing lane is wide open. Shooting against a goalie who is square to you when a teammate is alone on the back door is the lower-percentage play. The textbook read on a 2-on-1 is: if the defender plays the puck, pass; if the defender plays the pass, shoot. Here, the defender is playing you. Move the puck. Concept: cross-ice read, lane support, pass vs shoot.
U11 / Atom.tip: This is a classic pass-vs-shoot read on a 2-on-1.

opts: When their lead pad hits the post (or they reach the second post)
They never stop
After 5 seconds
When you yell stop
U11 / Atom.why: A butterfly slide ends at the post. Your shooting window is BEFORE that — once they're set against the post, the angle is back.
U11 / Atom.tip: A butterfly slide ends at the post.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: Should you shoot at where the goalie WAS or where they ARE GOING?
opts: Where they were — they're already moving away from that spot
Where they're going — get ahead of them
Right at their chest
Doesn't matter
U11 / Atom.why: Shoot at the gap. Their motion left a hole BEHIND them — that's where the puck goes.
U11 / Atom.tip: Shoot at the gap.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: Goalie out of position + you have time — best play?
opts: Snap a quick shot at the open side, no delay
Stop and try a deke
Pass to no one
Skate behind the net
U11 / Atom.why: Don't deke an out-of-position goalie. They're already beat. Snap it home before they recover.
U11 / Atom.tip: Don't deke an out-of-position goalie.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: How can you tell the goalie's slide is committed (not faked)?
opts: Their weight is on the lead pad with stick down — committed
Their head is up
They're standing tall
They're in an even-weight butterfly
U11 / Atom.why: Committed slide = lead pad heavy, low stick. Reading that = open lane is real, not bait.
U11 / Atom.tip: Committed slide = lead pad heavy, low stick.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: Goalie has been pulled out of position by a previous play — what's the carry-over read?
opts: Don't deke — they're already beat. Take a high-percentage open-net shot
Always deke them again
Pass to a non-existent teammate
Skate to the goalie and check them
U11 / Atom.why: When the goalie's already lost the angle, the shot is the play. Don't out-think it.
U11 / Atom.tip: When the goalie's already lost the angle, the shot is the play.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: What if both the goalie AND the puck are moving the same direction at the same speed?
opts: The angle is locked — you need to change yours (cut wide or back)
Just shoot anyway
Stop and start over
Always deke
U11 / Atom.why: Same-direction same-speed = no angle change. Force the issue with a cut to break the tracking.
U11 / Atom.tip: Same-direction same-speed = no angle change.

media.alt: Goalie sliding laterally, off-balance — the read is exploit the open side. Cut to the open net, shoot the open lane.

U11 / Atom.sit: What net are you trying to score in ?

U11 / Atom.tip: Anticipate the next play, not the current one.
U11 / Atom.sit: Given what you see in this freeze-frame, what happens NEXT?

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier entering the offensive zone. One defender is set at the blue line right in front of you. What's the right decision?
opts: Force the carry through the defender instead of using your linemate or chip option.
Read the defender — if they're set, use your linemate or chip it behind them.
Dump it in rather than trying to force a risky play here before they score.
Dump it in since the other team has committed to the other side right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A set defender at the blue line is telling you something. Read them — if they're set and you try to carry, you risk a turnover in the worst spot. Use your linemate or chip the puck in.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender set at the line? Read them. Chip, use your linemate, or carry — but read first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers enter your zone. You're the center on defense. Your two wingers have taken the two opposing wingers. Who do you cover?
opts: Hold the puck.
The opposing center.
Carry it wide.
Skate hard and recover.
U13 / Peewee.why: In man-to-man defensive zone coverage, the center covers the opposing center. Covering your assignment — not the puck — is the foundation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Three attackers in your zone? As the center, you've got their center. Find them and cover them.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the center on a 3-on-2. Both defenders take your two wingers. The middle lane is completely open. What do you do?
opts: Try to slow the rush down and give your support time to get back.
Shoot low since that creates the best look and look for support.
Drive the middle lane and shoot — the defenders opened it for you.
Shoot low before the play develops further and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: When both defenders go wide to cover the wings, they've given you the middle. Drive it and shoot — the goalie is unscreened and the lane is open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D go wide on a 3-on-2? Drive the middle. Shoot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team just turned the puck over in the offensive zone. The other team has it and is breaking out. What should every forward do immediately?
opts: Shoot low a better chance.
Turn around and backcheck hard — immediately.
Shoot low since that creates the best look.
Shoot low while your teammates recover.
U13 / Peewee.why: The instant the puck changes hands, every forward transitions back. The sooner you go, the less damage the other team can do.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover? Turn around. Right now. Every forward.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate is about to shoot from the point. You're the nearest forward. Where should you be?
opts: Carry it wide since that creates the best look and look for support.
Crash the net — be there for the rebound before the shot arrives.
Hold the puck from your current spot while staying in position.
Hold the puck before the defense recovers and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Crashing the net before the shot arrives puts you in position for a rebound before the goalie can cover. Net-front presence on shots wins games.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Teammate shooting from the point? Crash the net. Get there before the puck does.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. One forechecker is closing from the left. Your right winger is up the wall on the right — open. Your left winger is covered. What's the breakout call?
opts: Drive to the net front rather than trying to force a risky play here instead.
Pass to the open right winger — the forechecker has committed to the left side.
Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
Challenge at the line a better chance while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: The forechecker committing to one side creates an opening on the other side. Read the forechecker and use the open outlet.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forechecker committed to one side? The outlet on that same side may be open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. They have speed. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
Back up fast enough to not get beaten by their speed, stay in the middle, and force the shooter.
Skate to the corner and dig it out since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Skate to the corner and dig it out before the play develops further while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Speed on a 2-on-1 is the attacker's advantage. Backing up fast enough while staying in the middle takes away the pass and forces the shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Fast 2-on-1? Back up fast. Stay in the middle. Force the shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier in the neutral zone at full speed. Two defenders are set at the blue line. Your linemate is trailing. Best entry?
opts: Shoot from wherever you are now since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Chip the puck past the inside defender and go get it, or use a give-and-go with your trailer.
Shoot from wherever you are now before the play develops further while staying in position.
Force the carry through the defender instead of using your linemate or chip option.
U13 / Peewee.why: Carrying against two set defenders at the blue line is low-percentage. The chip-and-chase or give-and-go with the trailer are the smart options.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two set defenders? Chip + go or use your trailer. Don't carry into a wall.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the right D at the point on the power play. A PK forward is closing hard on you. Your left D partner is wide open. What do you do?
opts: Charge through the forechecker and try to break the pressure yourself.
Make a quick D-to-D pass to your partner — they're open and you're not.
Chip it past them rather than trying to force a risky play.
Skate the puck back behind your own net and start over.
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure at the point, the D-to-D pass resets the play. Your partner has time and a better look.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pressure coming at the point? D-to-D. Let your partner reset.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is cycling below the dots in your zone. As the weak-side defenseman, when do you challenge the puck carrier?
opts: Chip it past them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
When a player with the puck is in a dangerous spot — near the slot or about to pass to the slot.
Change lines because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Back up and defend because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: Defending the cycle means picking your moments. You don't challenge every pass — protect your position and challenge when the puck is in a truly dangerous spot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defending the cycle: stay in position. Challenge when dangerous, not just moving.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The lone defender is backing up in the middle, not committing to either side. What do you do?
opts: Back up and defend while staying in position and look for support.
Drive wide to force the defender to fully commit first, then decide.
Drive to the net front since that creates the best look from this spot.
Back up and defend and then look for support and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: If the defender isn't committing, you haven't created the advantage yet. Drive wide to force their decision.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender not committing? Make them. Drive wide first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're on a breakaway. The goalie is skating aggressively out to cut your angle. What's the most effective play?
opts: Pass it to your D at the far point before the play develops further and look for support.
Pull the puck wide, make the goalie commit to one side, then shoot or go to the other side.
Challenge at the line because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
U13 / Peewee.why: An aggressive goalie is looking for an early shot or for you to go straight. Make them commit — take the puck wide, let them follow, then shoot or go opposite.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Aggressive goalie on a breakaway: make them commit first. Then go where they're not.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. Both wingers are covered. Your center breaks free through the neutral zone — 40 feet away but completely open. What's the call?
opts: Hit the near winger with a short pass — safer than threading the needle.
Thread the stretch pass to the center — they're breaking and open.
Chip it off the boards to center ice and race for it yourself.
Hold the puck and wait for a trailer to come back for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stretch pass to a breaking center turns a defensive zone puck into an immediate rush chance. The reward — an odd-man rush — is worth the risk when the lane is clean.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Breaking center open on the stretch? Thread it. Risky but the reward is a rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1. The puck is at the top. A penalty killer closes hard on the top player. What should the top player do?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Move the puck quickly before the pressure arrives — pass to the half-wall or the other side.
Pass it to your D at the far point and then get back into your defensive position.
Swing wide along the boards since the other team has committed to the other side at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: On the power play, moving the puck before pressure arrives is the key. One extra skater means someone is always open — find them before the PK player gets there.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP with pressure coming? Move the puck early. Someone is open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Force a play and risk losing the puck to maintain possession.
Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
Dump it in since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
Thread a pass to the slot rather than trying to force a risky play here and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots burns clock, forces the other team to chase, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late: the clock is your ally. Protect the puck. Make them chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Swing wide along the boards rather than trying to force a risky play.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
Shoot from wherever you are now and wait for the play to reset and look for support.
Swing wide along the boards and then get back into your defensive position.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PP counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures, everyone else recovers. Panicking and all chasing creates a turnover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't panic.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is on the power play. A forward is screening your goalie right in front of the crease. The puck is at the point. What's your job as the nearest PK player?
opts: Challenge at the line before the defense recovers right away.
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie needs to see.
Challenge at the line a better chance.
Pressure the carrier hard while staying in position at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: A screener blocking your goalie's sightline is the most dangerous player on the ice. Moving them out is the priority — before the shot is taken.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Screener in the crease? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shot comes from the blue line right at the middle of the net. You're standing in front of the goalie. What's your job?
opts: Get out of the way so the goalie can see the shot clearly.
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's tracking.
Screen the goalie with your back but don't touch the puck.
Move off to the side and look for a rebound instead.
U13 / Peewee.why: Tipping a shot on net is one of the most effective plays in hockey. Even a small deflection changes the trajectory enough to beat a goalie who is tracking the original shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot coming from the point right at the goalie? Tip it. Any deflection beats their tracking.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the point on the power play. You have the puck and a shooting lane — but two PK forwards are closing on you hard. You have one second. What's the best play?
opts: Pressure the carrier hard because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice from this spot.
Fake the shot to freeze them, then pass to the half-wall player who opened up when the PKers committed to you.
Drop back and play defense because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Get your stick in the lane because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
U13 / Peewee.why: When two PK players commit to you, they've left someone open. A shot fake freezes them for a split second — the half-wall opens up the instant they both lean at you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two PKers closing? Fake the shot. Someone opened up when they committed.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the D at the point. You see the opposing center beginning to break toward your blue line for a potential counter. Your team still has the puck. What do you do?
opts: Chip it past them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Hold the blue line but cheat back slightly — be ready to pursue if the puck turns over.
Stay high at the blue line — don't venture into the offensive zone.
Take the outside lane since forcing it would be risky here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading a player beginning to position for a counter lets you cheat back slightly. You stay at the line but you're ready. If the puck turns, you're in position.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Center positioning for a counter? Cheat back slightly. Ready for the turnover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone trap. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Pull the puck back to your D, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
Try to stickhandle through them because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice before they score.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement — go backward to go forward.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Two attackers entered your zone and both D picked them up. A third attacker trails in 5 seconds later — nobody picked them up. You're the center on defense. What do you do?
opts: Pass back because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Pick up the late attacker — your winger on that side needs to find their assignment.
Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Skate hard since the other team has committed to the other side before they score.
U13 / Peewee.why: A late attacker entering the zone creates a coverage breakdown. The center needs to pick them up or communicate a switch immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Late attacker uncovered? Call it and pick them up. Coverage gaps get exploited.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The defender commits fully to your teammate. The goalie slides across expecting your pass. What's the right call now?
opts: Drop back and play defense while staying in position first.
Shoot — the goalie has moved and there's a lane on your side.
Pull up at the hash marks along the boards and look for support.
Drop back and play defense while staying in position again.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the defender commits to your teammate AND the goalie slides to take away the pass, they've left your side open. Shoot — the goalie has moved.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1: if the goalie chases the pass, shoot. Read the goalie.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but barely any time. What do you do?
opts: Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway.
Skate to the corner and dig it out a better chance on the play.
Skate to the corner and dig it out while your teammates recover on the play.
U13 / Peewee.why: In tight to the net with time running out, a quick backhand release is often better than taking the time to switch to your forehand. The goalie is already set for the forehand.
U13 / Peewee.tip: In tight with time running out? Shoot what you've got. Don't switch hands.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team breaks out. As you cross the red line you see both opposing defensemen are pinching up — they're at the red line, not their blue line. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
Skate hard behind them — you have a breakaway if you beat them to their blue line.
Pull up at the hash marks since the other team has committed to the other side first.
Change lines rather than trying to force a risky play here and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Defensemen pinching up means they've given up the space behind them. If you can beat them to the puck, you have a breakaway or a clean zone entry.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D pinching up? Burn them. Get behind them before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling and the slot is clogged. All four opponents are below the dots. What's the play?
opts: Take the outside lane and try to drive the net yourself.
Pass back to the open point — four defenders below the dots means the point shot is uncontested.
Force a centering pass through all four defenders to a teammate in the slot.
Skate into the slot yourself to draw coverage away from your teammates.
U13 / Peewee.why: When all four defenders collapse below the dots, the point is wide open. A point shot from there is uncontested and generates traffic and rebounds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Four D collapsed below the dots? The point is open. Give it up. Free shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play with only 10 seconds left. You're in the offensive zone at the half-wall. No clear shot lane. What's the play?
opts: Challenge at the line since that creates the best look while staying in position.
Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than nothing with 10 seconds left.
Challenge at the line before the play develops further while staying in position.
Challenge at the line and then get back into your defensive position on the play.
U13 / Peewee.why: With 10 seconds left on a PP, the time to set up is gone. Shoot from wherever you are — any shot is better than the power play expiring with no attempt.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 10 seconds left on the PP? Shoot from anywhere. Time's up.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. You're the center taking a defensive zone faceoff. The power play wants to win the draw back to the point for a shot setup. What's your goal on this draw?
opts: Drive to the net front and then get back into your defensive position.
Win it forward — kick it toward the corner or boards to prevent the PP shot setup.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Drive to the net front since the other team has committed to the other side.
U13 / Peewee.why: On a PK defensive zone faceoff, winning it forward — toward the boards or corner — takes away the PP's plan. Winning backward gives the PP exactly what they want.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK faceoff in your own zone: win it forward. Take away the point shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
opts: Hold the puck and then get back into your defensive position at that point.
Backing up at the right controlled pace, not overcommitting.
Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
Hold the puck since the other team has committed to the other side instead.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right controlled pace is the correct response.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush coming with speed? Back up controlled. Never pinch up against a fast rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 90 seconds left. You win a puck battle in the neutral zone. What's the right thing to do with the puck?
opts: Cut to the middle of the ice because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Control the puck along the boards, protect it, and force the other team to come get it — use every second.
Set up behind the net and wait and then get back into your defensive position while staying in position.
Shoot from wherever you are now because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: Game management late means using the clock. Protecting the puck in the neutral zone makes the other team come to you and burns precious seconds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late and won the puck? Protect it. Use the clock. Make them come to you.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is forechecking and the other team wins a battle in the corner. They're about to break out with speed. You're the high forward. What's the most important thing you do right now?
opts: Skate to the corner and dig it out from the point and look for support.
Hold your position and intercept the first outlet pass — you're the plug.
Skate to the corner and dig it out before they react before they score.
Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the puck changes in the corner, the high forward's job is to be the first interceptor — not to chase. Holding position and reading the breakout gives you a chance to pick off the first pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: They won the corner battle? High forward reads the breakout. Don't chase — intercept.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1. The half-wall player has the puck. The near PK forward is cheating toward the half-wall. The seam pass to the middle player is now open. What's the right play?
opts: Change lines because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice at that point.
Thread the seam pass to the middle player — the PK forward cheating toward you opened that lane.
Back up and defend because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Pull up at the hash marks because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice first.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the PK forward cheats toward you, they've vacated the seam. The middle player is open. The seam pass is the highest-danger play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK forward cheated toward you? The seam is open. Thread it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're protecting the puck behind the net. A defender has you pinned. Two teammates are in the zone but both are covered. What do you do?
opts: Throw the puck at the net and hope for a lucky bounce off the goalie.
Wheel to the other side of the net — moving forces the defender to move and may open a teammate.
Hold the puck against the boards until a teammate gets open.
Pass back to the D at the point for a shot attempt.
U13 / Peewee.why: Wheeling behind the net when both outlets are covered forces the defender to move with you, which can open a teammate, and resets the pressure.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both outlets covered behind the net? Wheel. Force the defense to move.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team just changed lines mid-play in the neutral zone. Their new players are still getting up to speed. You have the puck. What does this create?
opts: Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
A transition window — attack immediately while their fresh players aren't at full speed or in position.
Force a play through traffic rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
Force a play through traffic because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: A line change mid-play creates a brief window where the fresh players aren't at full speed or position. Attacking immediately exploits that window before it closes.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Other team just changed mid-play? Attack now. Their fresh players aren't set yet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're skating at full speed toward the net. A defender is chasing from behind. Should you slow down to take a more accurate shot?
opts: Yes — slow down and take a more accurate wrist shot.
No — shoot while moving at full speed, making it harder for the goalie.
Yes — stop completely and shoot from a still position.
Yes — slow down, cut across the crease, then shoot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Shooting while moving at full speed is harder for a goalie to track. Slowing down also lets the defender catch up. Shoot while moving.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Full speed toward the net with a defender behind you? Shoot moving. Don't slow down.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. Do you watch the puck or watch your check?
opts: Skate hard because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit first.
Watch your check — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Crash the net for a rebound since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
U13 / Peewee.why: In man coverage, losing sight of your assignment is how they get open for a pass. Keep them in front of you and use your peripheral vision for the puck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Man coverage in your own zone: watch your check, not the puck. Use your peripheral vision for the puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're forechecking. The D picks up the puck behind the net and starts to walk out to the left. You're closing from the right. Your linemate is on the left. How do you split?
opts: Force the pass through traffic when outlets are covered.
You force the D's direction from the right, your linemate cuts off the outlet to the left — trap them between you.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover again.
Back up and defend since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
U13 / Peewee.why: A two-player forecheck trap works by forcing the puck carrier in a direction while the other player cuts off the outlet in that direction.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two-player forecheck: one forces, one cuts the outlet. Trap them.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Get your stick in the lane since forcing it would be risky here and look for support.
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
Force a play and risk losing the puck to maintain possession.
Get your stick in the lane because your teammates need time to set up from this spot.
U13 / Peewee.why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.
U13 / Peewee.tip: OK angle vs. slightly covered slot? The slot wins. Higher danger is worth the risk.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your penalty kill is in a box. The power play's center has dropped below the dots to create a 1-3-1. Your box is now mismatched against their formation. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Play defensively and focus on preventing the short-handed goal.
Adjust — one PK forward drops to pressure the center who dropped in. The box has to mirror the threat.
Get your stick in the lane since the other team has committed to the other side and look for support.
Pull up at the hash marks because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-3-1 power play vs. a box requires a PK adjustment. One forward drops to pressure the center who's now below the dots — leaving them open in that area gives up the most dangerous shots in hockey.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP dropped a player below the dots? One PK forward drops to match. Adjust to the threat.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is defending. The goalie makes a save and controls the puck. The other team has three forecheckers in your zone. Your wingers are pinned. What's the right breakout option?
opts: Hold your position at the crease because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice before they score.
Goalie finds the center breaking through the middle or a winger escaping — don't give the puck to a covered player.
Wait for everyone to reset rather than pushing the transition quickly.
Skate hard since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover from this spot.
U13 / Peewee.why: After a save under forecheck pressure, the goalie needs to read the breakout. Finding the center or an escaping winger breaks the forecheck immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Goalie made the save under pressure? Find the breaking player. Read the breakout.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
opts: Collapse in the middle instead of maintaining proper positioning.
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck.
Hold your position at the crease since the other team has committed to the other side and look for support.
Skate to the corner and dig it out rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Beating the opposing player to the rebound — or at minimum getting inside position — prevents the second shot. Letting them get a clean rebound in the corner means a second chance against you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rebound in the corner? Race to it or get inside position. Prevent the second shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You catch a pass in the slot. You see an opening to the top corner. But you hesitate for two seconds while repositioning the puck for what you think will be a better shot. The goalie recovers. What should you have done?
opts: Look around and wait for more options rather than shooting immediately.
Released immediately on the catch — the opening was there when you first received it.
Pass back because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice early.
Skate hard rather than trying to force a risky play here while staying in position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Hesitating in the slot gives the goalie time to recover. If there's an opening when you catch the puck, release immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Opening in the slot? Release immediately. The best shot is the first one.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1. You've taken away the pass. The shooter pulls up and shoots. The goalie makes the save but the rebound comes right to the second attacker. What do you do?
opts: Set up behind the net and wait because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice.
Get your body in front of the second attacker — between them and the net — before they can get the shot off.
Set up behind the net and wait because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Collapse toward the puck immediately without maintaining your gap.
U13 / Peewee.why: When a rebound goes to the second attacker, your job shifts from pass prevention to body blocking. Getting your body between them and the net before they shoot is the only play.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rebound goes to the second attacker? Get your body between them and the net. Block the shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: It's a tied game in the third period. You have the puck in the neutral zone. Your team's fastest forward is breaking on your left, completely open. A defended pass is available on your right. What do you do?
opts: Pressure the carrier hard rather than trying to force something through the middle of the ice when the lane is not clean and the defenders are set.
Thread the pass to the breaking forward on the left — a tied game in the third is the time to take the calculated risk for a high-danger chance.
Swing wide and dump the puck in — a tied third period is no time to take risks.
Hold the puck and kill the clock until your line can change.
U13 / Peewee.why: A tied game in the third period is when high-danger chances matter most. An open breaking forward is a calculated risk worth taking — the reward is a scoring chance, the risk is a missed pass.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Tied late with a breaking forward open? Thread it. High-danger chances matter most now.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your goalie makes a save and controls the puck. Put the breakout steps in order.
items: D sets up behind the net and calls for the puck
Wingers sprint to their breakout lanes along the boards
Center supports low then curls through the middle
D reads pressure and hits the first open option
U13 / Peewee.why: A structured breakout has defined roles. D retrieves, wings get to their lanes, center fills the middle, and the D reads and passes. The system only works when everyone hits their spots.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Structured breakout: D retrieves, wings to lanes, center supports, D reads. Hit your spots.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play and needs to set up in the offensive zone. Put the PP entry steps in order.
items: Win the draw or gain clean possession in the neutral zone
Puck carrier enters with speed and reads the PK's setup
Supporting players stretch the PK by driving wide and high
Puck moves to the strong side and the PP gets into formation
U13 / Peewee.why: A clean PP entry is the first step to a successful power play. Possession first, enter with speed and a read, stretch the PK, then set up your formation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP entry: possess, enter with speed, stretch the PK, get into formation. Clean entries = more PP time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a defensive zone faceoff with the other team's top line on the ice. Put the defensive steps in order.
items: Everyone knows their assignment before the puck drops
Get to your assigned position around the faceoff circle
Center competes on the draw — win it back or tie up
If you lose the draw, immediately get to your coverage assignment
U13 / Peewee.why: D-zone faceoffs are structured moments. Knowing your job, getting positioned, competing on the draw, and executing coverage immediately are the steps. Teams that wing it on faceoffs give up goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: D-zone faceoff: know your job, get set, compete, execute coverage. No freelancing.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team dumps the puck into the corner to start a cycle. Put the cycle steps in order.
items: First forward wins the puck battle on the boards
Protect the puck and scan for support options
Second forward comes in low for the short pass or picks up a loose puck
Third forward stays high and the cycle rotation begins
U13 / Peewee.why: The cycle is a system: win the board battle, protect, get support, and rotate. If the first battle is lost, the cycle never starts. If the third forward goes low, you lose your high option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle: win the battle, protect, support, rotate. Lose the first battle and nothing starts.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A skilled forward is carrying the puck through the neutral zone at full speed, 1-on-1 against you. Put your defensive steps in order.
items: Pivot and match their speed while establishing your gap
Read their body language — hips, head, stick position
Close the gap at the blue line and angle them to the outside
Use an active stick to take away their preferred move and force them wide
U13 / Peewee.why: 1-on-1 defending is a sequence of reads and actions. Match speed, read their intent, close at the right time, and take away their best option. Skip the read step and you'll commit too early.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 1-on-1: match speed, read them, close at the line, steer wide. It's a sequence, not a gamble.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has the puck but can't enter the offensive zone — the D are too tight. Your center calls for a regroup. Put the steps in order.
items: Puck carrier turns back and passes to the trailing defenseman
Forwards loop through the neutral zone and rebuild speed
D-to-D pass stretches the PK or defensive setup horizontally
Forwards attack with speed on the second entry attempt
U13 / Peewee.why: A regroup is a tactical reset, not a failure. Bring it back, rebuild speed, stretch the defense, and attack again with better timing. Forcing a bad entry is worse than resetting.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Can't get in clean? Regroup. Bring it back, rebuild speed, attack again. Smart, not weak.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're set up in front of the net on the power play. A shot is coming from the point. Put your steps in order.
items: Establish position in front of the goalie — between the dots
Screen the goalie by getting big without interfering
Read the shot — is it a tip opportunity or a rebound play?
React — tip on net, collect the rebound, or jam at a loose puck
U13 / Peewee.why: Net-front presence is a skill with steps. Position, screen, read, react. Players who just stand there without reading the shot miss tip and rebound chances.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Net front: position, screen, read, react. Standing there isn't enough — you need to read the play.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with 3 minutes left. The other team is pressing. Put the puck management steps in order.
items: Make safe, simple passes — no high-risk plays in your own zone
Use the boards and the glass to clear when under pressure
Change lines quickly to keep fresh legs
Force them to earn zone entries — don't give up easy possession
U13 / Peewee.why: Protecting a late lead is about discipline. Safe passes, smart clears, quick changes, and denying easy zone time. One high-risk turnover can cost you the game.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Protecting a lead: simple plays, clear when pressured, fresh legs, deny entries. Discipline wins.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. The puck is loose along the boards near the blue line. You pinch down to get it, but your D partner has also jumped into the play low. The opponent chips it past both of you for a 2-on-0. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have pinched harder.
Both D went — one defenseman must always stay back to cover. If your partner is low, you hold the line. If you're pinching, they hold. Never both.
The forwards should have helped.
The puck bounced wrong.
U13 / Peewee.why: Two defensemen caught deep is a catastrophic defensive breakdown. One D always stays high as the safety valve. Communication between the D pair prevents this.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D can't go. Ever. One pinches, one stays. Communicate with your partner.

U13 / Peewee.sit: In the defensive zone, the puck is behind your net. Both your defensemen go behind the net to battle for it. The opposing center sneaks to the front of the net completely uncovered. They score on a pass out front. What's the mistake?
opts: The goalie should have stopped it.
Both D went behind the net — one D battles for the puck, the other covers the front of the net. Leaving the front of the net empty is one of the most dangerous mistakes in hockey.
The forwards should have been there.
The play happened.
U13 / Peewee.why: The front of the net must always be covered. When both D go behind the net, the most dangerous area on the ice is left wide open. One battles, one protects.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Both D behind the net? Someone better be in front. The front of the net is never optional.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. The puck carrier slows down to make a play at the blue line. Two forwards are flying into the zone at full speed and cross the blue line before the puck. Offside. Rush killed. What's the mistake?
opts: The puck carrier was too slow.
The two forwards didn't read the puck carrier and entered the zone early — on a rush, everyone times their entry off the puck. If the carrier slows down, you slow down.
The ref made a bad call.
The D played it well.
U13 / Peewee.why: Offside on a rush with numbers is one of the most frustrating mistakes in hockey. The forwards must read the puck carrier's speed and time their entry. The puck always crosses first.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush entry: read the carrier's speed. Puck crosses first. Always. Kill your speed if you have to.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the penalty kill, your forward leaves the box to chase the puck behind the net. The power play moves it quickly to the open slot area where your forward was supposed to be. Goal. What's the mistake?
opts: The forward should have chased faster.
The forward left their position in the PK box — on the penalty kill, you stay in structure and cover your zone. Chasing the puck out of position opens the most dangerous area on the ice.
The D should have shifted.
The goalie wasn't ready.
U13 / Peewee.why: PK discipline means staying in the box. When a player chases out of position, the slot opens up. The power play is designed to exploit exactly that mistake.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK: stay in the box. Chase out of position and the slot opens. That's where PP goals come from.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your linemate makes a beautiful cross-ice pass to you in the slot. You catch it cleanly and have a half-second to shoot. Instead, you look down to admire the pass and adjust the puck. The goalie sets and you shoot into their chest. What's the mistake?
opts: The pass was too hard.
You hesitated — in the slot with a half-second window, you shoot on the catch. Looking down and adjusting gave the goalie time to get set. Quick release beats perfect setup.
The goalie was lucky.
You should have passed it back.
U13 / Peewee.why: Slot chances are about speed of release, not perfection of setup. A half-second of puck adjustment gives the goalie time to square up. Shoot on the catch and beat them before they're ready.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Slot chance? Shoot on the catch. Don't look down. Don't adjust. Release beats perfection.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck behind the net. The forechecker is closing hard from the left side. Instead of going right or using the boards, your D skates directly into the forechecker. Turnover. What's the mistake?
opts: The D wasn't strong enough.
The D skated into pressure instead of away from it — when a forechecker closes from one side, go the other way or use the boards. Skating into pressure is a guaranteed turnover.
The forechecker was.
The forwards didn't help.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading the forecheck and going away from pressure is breakout 101. Skating into the forechecker gives them exactly what they want — a turnover in a dangerous area.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forechecker from the left? Go right. Away from pressure. Always away. Never into it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone. Your defenseman keeps backing up all the way to the top of the crease, giving the attacker the entire offensive zone to work with. What's the mistake?
opts: The D should have been more aggressive.
The D gave up too much ice — backing up to the crease gives the attacker time, space, and options. Close the gap at the blue line and take away their time to make plays.
The forwards should have backchecked.
The attacker was too skilled.
U13 / Peewee.why: A defender who backs up to the crease surrenders the entire offensive zone. Gap control means closing the distance at the blue line — taking away the attacker's time and space before they can set up.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Don't back up to your crease. Close the gap at the blue line. Take away their time and space.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team turns the puck over in the offensive zone. Two forwards are exhausted and glide slowly to the bench for a change. The other team rushes 4-on-3 and scores before the fresh players get into position. What's the mistake?
opts: The coach should have called for a change earlier.
The tired forwards changed too slowly at the wrong time — after a turnover, you sprint back first, then change when the puck is safe. Changing during transition is how you give up odd-man goals.
The defensemen should have held.
The other team got lucky.
U13 / Peewee.why: Line changes during defensive transition are deadly. Sprint back first, stabilize the defense, then change when the puck is in a safe area. Gliding to the bench during a rush against is a gift to the other team.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover? Sprint back first. Change when it's safe. Never change during a rush against.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling on the strong side. The defense has matched your cycle perfectly — nothing is opening up. Your D at the point makes a D-to-D pass to the weak side. What happens next?
opts: The cycle continues on the same side.
The weak-side D has an open shooting lane or passing option — the defense was overloaded to the strong side and the D-to-D pass shifts the play to open ice.
Nothing changes.
Pass back out immediately instead of maintaining cycle.
