U13 / Peewee.why: A D-to-D pass against an overloaded defense opens the weak side completely. The defense was committed to the cycle side and can't recover across the ice in time.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle stuck? D-to-D to the weak side. The defense overloaded to the strong side can't get there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a 3-on-2 rush. Both defenders focus on the three attackers. Your fourth player — the trailing D — jumps into the play late from the blue line, completely unnoticed. The puck carrier sees them. What happens next?
opts: The trailing D stays back.
The puck carrier drops it back to the trailing D who has a clean shot from the top of the circles — the defense never accounted for the trailer.
The puck carrier shoots alone.
Everyone regroups.
U13 / Peewee.why: The trailing player on a rush is one of the most dangerous weapons in hockey. The defense accounts for the three rushers but forgets the trailer. A drop pass to an uncontested shooter at the top of the circles is a prime chance.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Trailer on the rush? Drop it back. The D has a clean look that nobody's covering.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your center strips the puck from the opposing center in your defensive zone slot area. The other team's forwards are all deep in your zone. Your wingers are at the hash marks. What happens next?
opts: Your center dumps it out.
Your center headmans it to a winger breaking up ice — the other team is caught deep, and a quick pass to a winger with speed creates an odd-man rush the other way.
Everyone regroups in the D-zone.
Your center holds the puck and waits.
U13 / Peewee.why: A turnover in the defensive zone with the other team caught deep is a counter-attack opportunity. The quick headman pass to a winger with speed can create a 2-on-1 or breakaway before the other team recovers.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Strip the puck in your zone with them caught deep? Headman it fast. Counter-attack before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the penalty kill, your team clears the puck to center ice. The power play's defenseman retrieves it, but your two PK forwards are already forechecking hard at the blue line. What happens next?
opts: The PP sets up again easily.
Your PK forwards pressure the D and force a turnover or a bad re-entry — aggressive PK forechecking at the blue line disrupts the PP's rhythm.
Your PK falls back to the box.
The PK changes lines.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PK clear followed by immediate pressure on the retrieving D is elite penalty killing. You've broken their setup and now you're making them earn the zone entry all over again — under pressure this time.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK clears it? Don't fall back. Pressure the retrieval. Make them earn the entry again.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team loses the defensive zone faceoff clean — the puck goes back to their point player who one-times it. Your goalie makes the save but can't control the rebound. It's loose in front. What happens next?
opts: Everyone watches the goalie.
Your forwards and D collapse to the net, box out opposing players, and either clear the puck or help the goalie freeze it — loose pucks in front after a faceoff loss require an all-hands response.
The D pass it around the boards.
Your goalie comes out to play it.
U13 / Peewee.why: A loose puck in the crease after a faceoff loss is maximum danger. Everyone collapses to the net — box out, clear, freeze. Half a second of hesitation is a goal against.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Loose puck in front after a lost draw? Collapse. Box out. Clear or freeze. Everyone. Now.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You drive to the net and the goalie goes down in butterfly early. You still have the puck on your stick and you're in tight. The entire top of the net is open. What happens next?
opts: You shoot low into the pads.
You lift the puck high over the butterfly — goalie is down, the top of the net is wide open, and you have the puck and time to elevate it.
You pass it across.
You deke to the backhand.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie in butterfly early leaves the top of the net open. If you have time and the puck, lifting it over the pads into the open net is the highest-percentage play. Read the goalie, not just the net.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Goalie down in butterfly early? Go high. Lift it. The top of the net is yours.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending a 1-on-1. The forward drives wide, then suddenly stops up at the top of the circle and cuts back to the middle. You were committed to the outside angle. What happens next?
opts: You keep going to the outside.
You pivot hard and recover to the inside — the forward just beat your angle, and if you don't recover inside, they'll have the slot. Pivot feet, close the inside lane.
You stop and watch.
You go for a poke check from behind.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stop-and-cut-back to the middle is designed to beat a defender committed outside. The recovery is a hard pivot to close the inside lane before they reach the slot. Feet must move — reaching from behind creates penalties.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forward cuts back inside? Pivot and recover inside. Don't reach from behind. Move your feet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On a breakout, the safest option is always to go up the boards.
U13 / Peewee.why: The boards are often safe, but the best play depends on where the pressure is. Sometimes the middle is open. Sometimes a reverse behind the net opens the other side. Always use the boards and you become predictable. Read the forecheck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Boards are often safe, but not always best. Read the forecheck and use what's open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On the power play, moving the puck quickly to find the open lane is more effective than holding onto it and trying to beat players one-on-one.
U13 / Peewee.why: Power play efficiency comes from puck movement that forces the PK to shift. One player holding and deking lets the PK stay compact. Quick passes open seams the PK can't cover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP: move the puck. Quick passes open seams. Holding it lets the PK stay compact.

U13 / Peewee.sit: When you're under pressure in the defensive zone, you should always freeze the puck to stop play.
U13 / Peewee.why: Freezing gives the other team a faceoff in your zone — a set play with their best players. If you can make a quick play to a teammate or clear it, that's better than giving them a structured offensive opportunity.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Freezing in your zone = giving them a set play. Clear or pass if you can. Freeze only when you must.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Taking a lot of shots from bad angles and low-danger areas is just as effective as fewer shots from high-danger areas.
U13 / Peewee.why: Shot quality matters more than shot quantity. A shot from the slot is worth far more than five shots from the corner. Getting to high-danger areas and shooting from there produces more goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot quality over shot quantity. One slot shot beats five corner shots. Get to dangerous areas.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Using an active stick to poke the puck away is often more effective than going for a big body check.
U13 / Peewee.why: An active stick disrupts plays without taking you out of position. A missed body check puts you behind the play. Good defenders use their stick first and their body when the timing is right.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick first, body second. Active stick keeps you in position. Missed hits put you behind the play.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Turnovers kill more than missed shots
U13 / Peewee.sit: Turnovers in the neutral or defensive zone are more costly to your team than missed shots in the offensive zone.
U13 / Peewee.why: A missed shot is a lost possession in the offensive zone — the other team still has to exit and come 200 feet. A neutral or defensive zone turnover gives them the puck in a dangerous area with less ice to cover. Location of turnovers matters.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Missed shot in their zone? Acceptable. Turnover in yours? Deadly. Location of turnovers matters.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Best time to attack is after a turnover
U13 / Peewee.sit: The best time to create a scoring chance is immediately after your team forces a turnover, when the other team is out of position.
U13 / Peewee.why: Transition offense works because the other team is still in their offensive mindset and out of defensive position. The fastest teams in transition create the most odd-man rushes and high-danger chances.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover = attack window. They're out of position. Go now, before they recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is rimming around the boards and will end up behind your net. Two opposing forwards are forechecking hard. What do you do?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Get to the puck first and either rim it to your D's forehand on the weak side, or freeze it if your D can't get there.
Protect the puck there since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover early.
Stay in the neutral zone since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie who plays the puck well under forecheck pressure is a huge weapon. Beat the forecheckers to the puck and either set up your D on the open side or freeze it to relieve pressure. Passive goalies let forechecks succeed.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck coming behind the net with pressure? Beat them to it. Set up your D or freeze.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A point shot is coming and there's an opposing forward in front of you with their stick on the ice, ready for a tip. What should you prepare for?
opts: Try to stickhandle through them because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit right away.
The deflection — assume the puck will change direction. Stay in your stance, keep your stick on the ice, and read the deflection angle in real time.
Pressure the carrier hard rather than trying to force something through the middle of the ice when the lane is not clean and the defenders are set.
Drop back and play defense because in this situation the safest option is to keep possession and let the play develop before you commit right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A stick on the ice in front of you is a deflection setup. You have to assume the puck gets tipped. Stay low, keep your stick on the ice, and track the puck through the deflection — it usually changes angle and elevation.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick on the ice in front? Expect a tip. Stay low, track through the deflection.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're making a save from a shot on the right side. You can control where the rebound goes with your pads or blocker. What's the best target for the rebound?
opts: Pass back since the lane is open now while staying in position.
Into the left corner — away from the shooter and out of the slot.
Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Pass back and then get back into your defensive position first.
U13 / Peewee.why: Rebound direction is an active skill. Angling your pad to redirect the rebound to the far corner takes it out of the most dangerous area. A rebound in the slot is a goal waiting to happen.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Control your rebounds. Far corner. Away from the slot. Always.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shooter is coming down the right wing. You know from previous shifts they always shoot short side, top corner on the rush. What's your approach?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Cheat slightly to take away their preferred shot — respect their tendency by giving a bit more of the opposite corner.
Force a play through traffic since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover.
Crash the net for a rebound because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice and look for support.
U13 / Peewee.why: Elite goalies track shooter tendencies within a game. If a player has shown you their preferred spot multiple times, slightly cheating to take it away forces them to beat you somewhere else — often a spot they're less comfortable with.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Know a shooter's tendency? Respect it. Cheat slightly to take away their spot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You see an opposing player sneaking to the backdoor completely uncovered by your D. Your D are both focused on the corner battle. The pass hasn't come yet. What do you do?
opts: Take the outside lane because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice instead.
Yell loudly — 'Back door!' or 'Net front!' — to warn your D to get a stick on the open player.
Hold the puck because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice right away.
Carry it wide because the defenders have committed to the strong side of the ice right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: The goalie has the best view of developing threats. A loud, specific call — 'Back door!' — can get a defender's stick into a passing lane before the pass even happens. Silent goalies watch goals happen.
U13 / Peewee.tip: See a coverage problem? Call it. Loud. Specific. 'Back door!' stops goals.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is shot around the boards behind your net. A forechecker is closing from your left. Your D is coming to the left side. Your other D is wide open on the right side. What's the best play?
opts: Stay centered in the net rather than moving to improve your angle.
Stop it, then make a short pass to your right D who is wide open on the weak side — a clean D-to-D breakout under pressure.
Take the outside lane since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover on the play.
Crash the net for a rebound since forcing the pass through traffic in the neutral zone usually leads to a turnover right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: A goalie who can play a D-to-D pass under pressure is a major asset. Identifying the uncovered defender and getting them the puck clean starts the breakout before the forecheck sets up. This is an advanced skill worth developing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Weak-side D wide open? Play it to them. D-to-D breaks forechecks.

U13 / Peewee.q: Tap the breakout players in order 1 → 4.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Retriever → support → center → winger. Build up the ice.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You receive a pass at the blue line on the power play. A penalty killer is closing fast. The slot is open. You have one second. What's right?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
One-time it or shoot immediately into the open slot.
Pass the puck back to the other point.
Skate into the zone yourself with it.
U13 / Peewee.why: One second on the power play point with the slot open is a shooting opportunity — not a thinking opportunity. The PK player closing on you takes that lane away in two seconds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Point with an open shot? Release it fast. The lane closes quick.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling in the offensive zone below the dots. You're low in the corner with the puck. A defender is on you. Your winger is at the half-wall. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck alone in the corner and wait for help.
Pass to the half-wall winger and let the cycle continue.
Shoot from the corner and hope for a rebound near the net.
Pass all the way back to the point to reset the play again.
U13 / Peewee.why: When you're pressured low in the corner, passing to the half-wall winger moves the puck to a player with more options — they can shoot, pass to the slot, or continue the cycle.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pressured in the corner? Pass to the half-wall. They have more options than you do.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're on the power play at the half-wall. The puck carrier passes it to you in stride. The goalie is leaning to their left. What's the right play?
opts: Stop the puck first and then look around carefully for a good pass.
One-time it to the right side where the goalie hasn't moved yet.
Stickhandle and wait.
Pass it back up to the point.
U13 / Peewee.why: A one-timer on the power play catches the goalie before they can reset. Stopping the puck gives them time to adjust — a quick release doesn't.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pass comes in stride with the goalie leaning? One-time it. Quick release wins.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. The power play passes to the point. You're the near penalty killer forward. What do you do?
opts: Skate all the way into the corner to help your teammate battle for the loose puck down low.
Close on the point player to pressure the shot or take away the dangerous pass options.
Stay in the middle of the ice to block the cross-seam pass and protect the slot area well.
Back all the way up to your own crease to help the goalie see shots through heavy traffic.
U13 / Peewee.why: Closing on the point player on the PK takes away a clean shooting lane or forces a rushed decision. You don't have to get the puck — just make them uncomfortable.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK: close on the point player. Make it hard for them to get a clean shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the puck carrier on a 2-on-1. The lone defender commits fully to your teammate. The goalie slides across expecting your pass. What's the right call now?
opts: Pass to your teammate just as planned — stick with it.
Shoot — the goalie has moved and there's a lane on your side.
Pass back to the other side again.
Pull up and regroup back.
U13 / Peewee.why: When the defender commits to your teammate AND the goalie slides to take away the pass, they've left your side open. Shoot — the goalie has moved.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1: if the goalie chases the pass, shoot. Read the goalie.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team turned the puck over and now three attackers are coming at your two defensemen. You're the left D. The puck carrier is on your side. What's your job?
opts: Rush hard at the puck carrier to try to strip the puck before they get into your zone.
Take your side and force the carrier wide — your partner protects the middle attacker.
Chase the trailing attacker down the far side and leave the puck carrier to your partner.
Back up all the way to your crease.
U13 / Peewee.why: On a 3-on-2, each D takes a side and the D on the puck side forces the carrier wide. The partner covers the middle — the most dangerous attacker.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 3-on-2 against you: take your side. Protect the middle. Don't both go one way.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're about to receive a pass in the offensive zone. Before the puck reaches you, what should you already be doing?
opts: Waiting with your stick flat on the ice, ready to catch and control the puck cleanly on contact.
Looking up and deciding where you're going with the puck before it arrives — pre-reading the play.
Calling loudly for the puck so your teammate knows you are ready.
Watching the puck travel the whole way to make sure that you don't miss it when it arrives.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pre-reading the play — deciding where you're going before the puck arrives — is what separates slow players from fast ones. By the time the puck hits your stick, your decision should already be made.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pass coming? Decide what you're doing with it before it arrives. Pre-read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the power play. The penalty kill gets the puck and two players immediately break the other way 2-on-2. What should your team do?
opts: Chase the puck carrier with everyone and try to strip the puck back fast.
One forward pressures the puck, the others recover their positions — don't panic.
All five skaters retreat to your own zone.
Ignore it — you'll get the puck back soon.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PP counter-attack is dangerous but not a guaranteed goal. One player pressures, everyone else recovers. Panicking and chasing creates a turnover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK breaks out on a counter? One pressures, others recover. Don't all chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has a power play. You're standing in front of the goalie. Your teammate at the point winds up for a shot. What's your job?
opts: Skate out of the way so the goalie can see the puck clearly and make an easy save there.
Stay planted and block the goalie's sightline — screen them, and be ready to tip the shot.
Skate to the corner to get position early for the rebound off the boards.
Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can score from a better angle.
U13 / Peewee.why: A screener in front of the net on the power play is one of the highest-value positions. You block the goalie's view and tip shots combination creates goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: In front of the net on the PP? Screen the goalie and tip the shot. Stay planted.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the right defenseman covering an attacker on your side. Another attacker cuts across in front of the net toward your side. Your partner calls 'switch!' What do you do?
opts: Stay with your original assignment and completely ignore the switch call from your partner.
Take the attacker cutting to your side, let your partner recover their original assignment.
Call the referee for a penalty.
Back up all the way to the crease and try to cover both of the attackers at once.
U13 / Peewee.why: A switch call means you take the player coming to your side while your partner recovers their original assignment. It's communication and trust.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Partner calls switch? Take the player coming to you. Trust the call.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the defenseman and the opposing team's winger wins a puck battle in the corner in your defensive zone. They're about to pass to someone in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Skate down into the corner and try to re-win the puck battle from the opposing winger.
Seal off the passing lane to the slot — get between the puck carrier and the dangerous area.
Skate to your blue line and hope your teammate wins the battle in the corner.
Watch the puck carrier closely and only react after the pass has already been made.
U13 / Peewee.why: Once the puck battle is lost, the priority shifts to cutting off the next play — which is almost always a slot pass. Get between the puck carrier and the slot immediately.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Lost the puck battle? Don't chase the puck. Seal the passing lane to the slot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure. Your D picks up the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are pinching in from both sides. What breakout option gives you the best chance?
opts: Wheel around the net and try to carry the puck out all by yourself straight through the pressure up the middle of the ice.
Reverse the puck to the other side — one forechecker can't follow, buying time for your winger to get position.
Rim it hard around the boards.
Shoot it down the ice as far as you can for icing to get a whistle and a fresh faceoff in the defensive zone right now.
U13 / Peewee.why: Under heavy forecheck pressure with two pinching forecheckers, the reverse gives you an extra second. One forechecker can't cover both sides simultaneously.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two forecheckers pinching? Reverse the puck. One of them can't follow.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is sitting back in a 1-2-2 neutral zone setup. Every time your team tries to enter, they get stopped at the red line. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Keep carrying the puck in at full speed until the trap finally breaks down and lets you by.
Pull the puck back to your defensemen, use a D-to-D pass to overload one side, or try a stretch pass to a breaking forward.
Dump the puck deep every single rush and grind them down with a heavy forecheck eventually.
Skate around the outside of the trap along the boards and curl back into the middle with speed.
U13 / Peewee.why: A 1-2-2 trap is designed to stop straight-line carries. The answer is patience and lateral movement.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Against a trap: go backward to go forward. Change the angle. Be patient.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is taking a defensive zone faceoff. Before the puck drops, what should every player already know?
opts: Nothing at all — players should just react to where the puck goes after the official drops it.
Their assignment — who they're covering and where they're going based on whether you win or lose the draw.
Where the nearest door to the bench is in case their shift ends right after the puck drops.
To skate as fast as they possibly can toward the puck the moment it hits the ice surface.
U13 / Peewee.why: A faceoff is a set play. Every player should know their assignment before the puck drops — what to do if you win, what to do if you lose. The setup before the drop is as important as the drop itself.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Before the faceoff drops: know your assignment. Win or lose — be ready to execute.

U13 / Peewee.sit: An opposing winger has broken the puck out of their own zone and is coming at you with speed. You're at the red line. The attacker is fast and has a step on you. What's your priority?
opts: Charge right at them and try to take the puck off their stick before they can get across the red line and into your zone.
Back up at a pace that doesn't let them get speed on you, angle them toward the boards, and wait.
Turn around and race them straight back to your net to try to beat them to the front before they can fire off a shot.
Stand completely still at the red line and wait for them to come to you before you commit to any kind of defensive move.
U13 / Peewee.why: An attacker with speed is trying to blow past you. Backing up at the right pace while angling keeps you ahead of them and denies the clean entry.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Fast attacker with a step on you? Back up fast and angle. Don't let them get speed going.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in tight to the net — five feet out. The goalie is set. You have the puck on your backhand but there's barely any time. What do you do?
opts: Try to switch the puck from your backhand to your forehand for a stronger shot.
Release your backhand immediately — the goalie is set for the forehand anyway.
Pass to a teammate outside the slot.
Skate around the net to get a better shooting angle.
U13 / Peewee.why: In tight to the net with time running out, a quick backhand release is often better than taking the time to switch to your forehand. The goalie is already positioned for the forehand.
U13 / Peewee.tip: In tight with time running out? Shoot what you've got. Don't switch hands.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is cycling in the offensive zone. The cycle isn't working — the defenders have perfectly matched your movement for 20 seconds and nothing is opening. What should you do?
opts: Keep cycling the same way — something has to open up for a shot eventually.
Stop the cycle — dump the puck deep, change lines, and come back with a new entry.
Shoot from the corner area.
Pass all the way back to the blue line point.
U13 / Peewee.why: A cycle against a perfectly matched defense is just burning your legs. Recognize when the cycle isn't creating opportunities and make the change — dump, line change, come back fresh.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle going nowhere? Dump and change. Come back with a fresh entry.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team just got called for a penalty. Before you leave the ice, what should the remaining four players know?
opts: Nothing really — just go out and defend hard.
Their specific penalty kill assignments — who covers the point, who covers the half-wall, and what the plan is if they get the puck.
To skate as fast as they can and chase every puck carrier around the zone without ever stopping until the penalty ends.
To surround the puck carrier at all times with all four skaters so there's no chance for a clean pass or shot on the goalie.
U13 / Peewee.why: The penalty kill is a system. The four remaining players need to know their assignments before the power play sets up. Improvising on the PK gives up goals.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Going on the PK? Know your assignment. The system works when everyone knows their job.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the last defender back and two attackers are coming at you on a 2-on-1. Your goalie is set. One attacker has the puck on the left. One is cutting to the net on the right. What do you do?
opts: Commit hard to the puck carrier on the left and try to take the puck off their stick before they can make any kind of play.
Stay in the middle — take away the pass to the cutter and force the shot. Your goalie handles the shot.
Chase the cutter all the way to the right post to make sure they can't tap in any pass that comes across to the back door.
Back up all the way to your own crease so you're right next to the goalie and can cover any rebound that comes out on a shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass, force the shot, trust your goalie. Don't commit to either attacker until they force your hand.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Last defender back on a 2-on-1: take away the pass. Let the goalie have the shot.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Read the goalie on a breakaway
U13 / Peewee.sit: You're on a breakaway. The goalie is skating aggressively out to cut your angle. What's the most effective play?
opts: Make the safe play — pass backwards or to the boards.
Pull the puck wide, make the goalie commit to one side, then shoot or go to the other side.
Try a quick straight backhand shot right down the middle before the goalie can get set.
Slow way down and wait until you are right on top of the goalie before making a move.
U13 / Peewee.why: An aggressive goalie is looking for an early shot or for you to go straight. Make them commit — take the puck wide, let them follow, then shoot or go opposite.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Aggressive goalie on a breakaway: make them commit first. Then go where they're not.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your power play is in a 1-3-1 and has been set up for 30 seconds without generating a shot. Two PK forwards are pressuring the top. What should the top player do?
opts: Try to hold the puck and fight through both PK forwards to get a shot from the top.
Move the puck quickly across to the half-wall before both pressure players arrive.
Take a slap shot from distance through all the traffic and hope for a rebound chance.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone and start the whole power play over.
U13 / Peewee.why: Two PK forwards pressuring one point means the rest of the formation has a gap — the half-wall is almost always where that gap is. Move the puck before both arrive.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two PKers coming? Move the puck before they get there. They can't cover two spots at once.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is winning by one goal with two minutes left. You win a faceoff in the offensive zone. What's most important?
opts: Take a big slap shot immediately on the faceoff draw to try to extend your lead early.
Control the puck, cycle below the dots, use every second — only shoot on a high-quality chance.
Dump the puck deep into the far corner and chase it hard with all your forwards.
Pass the puck all the way back to your own zone to be safe and kill more clock.
U13 / Peewee.why: Late-game puck management is a skill. Cycling below the dots forces the other team to chase, burns clock, and keeps the puck away from their skilled players.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Leading late: the clock is your ally. Protect the puck. Make them chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the D and you pick up the puck in your own corner. The forechecker is coming from your left. Your left winger is up the wall on your left — open, but the forechecker is between you and that pass. Your right winger is also up the wall on the right, completely open. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass to the left winger — they're your first option immediately.
Pass to the right winger — the forechecker has already cut off the left.
Carry it out yourself.
Rim it around the boards.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading the forechecker's angle tells you which options they've taken away. If they're cutting off the left, the right side is the clean exit.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Forechecker cutting off one side? The other side is your outlet. Read the angle.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A skilled forward is coming at you 1-on-1 at full speed. They're a good stickhandler and have already made two players fall down tonight. What do you avoid doing?
opts: Backing up and staying patient until they make their move.
Poking check from maximum distance or charging them.
Angling them toward the boards and closing your gap.
Waiting for their first move before reacting.
U13 / Peewee.why: A skilled stickhandler wants you to commit early — a premature poke check gives them exactly the step they need to blow past you. Stay patient and contained.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Skilled forward 1-on-1? Don't commit early. Stay patient. Make them decide first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck at the offensive blue line. A defender is closing hard from your right. Your center is open five feet behind you. Your only shooting lane is gone. What do you do?
opts: Try to hold the puck and fight through the pressure until a lane opens.
Drop a back pass to your center — they have time and space and you don't.
Shoot it right into the defender.
Dump the puck deep into the corner and chase.
U13 / Peewee.why: When your shooting lane is gone and a player is closing fast, a back pass to a player with time and space is the right play. Don't force the situation — solve it.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shooting lane gone with pressure coming? Drop it back. Someone behind you has time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're about to shoot from the slot. Before you release, you have a split second to look at the goalie. They're leaning hard to your right. Where should you shoot?
opts: Top right — shoot high over their shoulder on the same side they are leaning hard toward.
Low left — the opposite side of where they're leaning. They can't recover in time.
Right at their chest — power wins.
Wait for the goalie to reset their position before you decide to release the shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pre-shot reads of the goalie give you a target before you even release. A goalie leaning hard to one side is leaving the other side vulnerable — shoot there.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Goalie leaning hard one way? Shoot the opposite side. They can't recover in time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're on a line change. Your linemates are still on the ice finishing a rush. The play is still alive. When do you go on?
opts: Go immediately — you're fresh and your team needs the help.
Wait until the puck is dead, there's a stoppage, or your teammates are safely at the bench and can't be caught short.
Jump onto the ice as soon as your teammate steps off, even if they're still in the play and skating hard.
Focus on your own play and let others figure out theirs.
U13 / Peewee.why: Taking a too-many-players penalty on a rush because you jumped on too early is a critical mistake. The line change is clean only when the puck is dead or your teammate is safely off.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Line change during a rush: wait for a stoppage or until your teammate is safely off. Don't jump on early.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team just won a puck battle in your own zone. Two of your forwards are already at center ice. The other team has one player back. A 3-on-1 is developing. What's the trigger?
opts: Wait until all three forwards are together in the neutral zone first.
Go immediately — the 3-on-1 window closes fast as the other team gets back.
Regroup back at your own blue line first.
Dump it in deep and head to the bench.
U13 / Peewee.why: Transition opportunities close fast. If two forwards are already at center and you have a 3-on-1 developing, going immediately before the other team recovers is how you exploit it.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Transition chance opening? Go now. Those windows close in seconds.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're a defenseman in your own zone. The opposing winger has the puck in the corner and keeps looking at the player in the slot. What do you do?
opts: Watch the puck carrier closely and wait for the pass to actually happen first.
Step in front of the anticipated pass lane — intercept it before it arrives.
Skate into the corner to pressure the puck carrier.
Back up and hug your crease.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading a puck carrier's eyes tells you where the pass is going. If they keep looking at the slot, get in that lane before the pass is made — not after.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck carrier keeps looking at one spot? Get in that lane. Intercept before the pass arrives.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. You're being pressured. The other team has a 2-on-1 developing in your end. You have the puck behind you. What do you do?
opts: Keep skating toward the offensive zone and try to create your own chance before help arrives.
Recognize the 2-on-1 developing and get the puck to your D immediately so they can deal with the threat.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone right away and change lines for fresh legs to come.
Stop where you are and wait for your teammates to catch up before making your next decision.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading the play behind you — a developing 2-on-1 — is an important defensive awareness skill. Getting the puck to your D before the 2-on-1 is established gives them a chance to deal with it.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1 developing in your end and you have the puck? Get it back to your D. Don't skate forward.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending in your own zone. An attacker cuts from the left side to the right side in front of the net. Your partner was covering them but now they're on your side. What do you do?
opts: Ignore the attacker completely and trust that your partner will just follow them over.
Call switch and take the attacker coming to your side so your partner can recover.
Call for the referee to whistle a penalty on the crossing play.
Back up all the way to the crease and let the attacker set up wherever they want to go.
U13 / Peewee.why: When an attacker cuts across in front of the net, the defending pair needs to communicate a switch. You take the player coming to your side — your partner recovers.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Attacker cuts to your side? Call switch and take them. Let your partner recover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone. All three forwards are on the left side of the ice — bunched up. The entire right side is empty. You're the right winger. What do you do?
opts: Stay on the left side of the ice where the puck is and help your teammates battle for possession along the boards in the zone.
Move immediately to the right side — the space is there and the D will have to follow you or leave you open.
Go to the bench for a line change.
Stand in front of the opposing net.
U13 / Peewee.why: When everyone goes to the same side, the other side opens up completely. Moving to the open side as the right winger creates an immediate passing option and forces a defensive reaction.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Everyone bunching up on one side? Go the other way. The space is there.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill and in a box formation. The power play moves the puck quickly around the outside. One of your PK players starts chasing every pass. What goes wrong?
opts: Nothing at all — chasing pucks is actually a great PK strategy.
Chasing breaks the box — gaps open up and the power play gets into the dangerous areas you were protecting.
Chasing tires out the power play faster.
Chasing makes the power play players hesitate and second-guess every pass they make around the outside of the box setup.
U13 / Peewee.why: The box on the penalty kill works because every player holds their position and protects the dangerous areas. Chasing every pass breaks the structure and creates gaps the power play exploits.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK box: hold your spot. Don't chase every pass. The box works when everyone stays in it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're at center ice with the puck. You see a 3-on-2 developing in the offensive zone. One winger is on the left, one is on the right, and a third forward is trailing. Both defenders are inside. What's the read?
opts: Pass immediately to the left winger because that's the first open option you see and quick passes are always the right play.
Look at both defenders first — pass to whichever winger the defenders are leaving more open, or drive the middle if it's open.
Shoot the puck from center ice right at the goalie to try to surprise them and maybe sneak one in through traffic.
Dump the puck deep into the offensive zone and chase it down with all three forwards to start a forecheck right now.
U13 / Peewee.why: On a 3-on-2, reading both defenders first tells you which option is actually open before you commit. The best puck carrier reads the whole ice, not just one option.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 3-on-2 developing? Read both defenders before picking your option. Don't just default to one side.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the center on a rush. The defenders are both backing up. You're at the top of the circles moving at full speed. You have a shooting lane. What do you do?
opts: Pass to a winger immediately — passing first on a rush is always the right play.
Shoot — you're at full speed and the goalie has to set against a moving shooter.
Pull up and stickhandle.
Dump the puck in deep and reset.
U13 / Peewee.why: A moving shooter at full speed at the top of the circles is harder for the goalie to set against than a stationary shooter. Use the speed — shoot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Full speed at the circles with a lane? Shoot. Moving shooters are harder to stop.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck. The forechecker is coming from the left. Your left winger is covered. Your right winger is also covered. Your center is late getting back. What's the option?
opts: Force a hard pass to one of the covered wingers and hope they can win the battle down the wall.
Reverse behind the net — the forechecker on the left has to follow, which buys time for the center to get back.
Carry the puck straight out yourself and try to skate through the forechecker coming from the left side.
Shoot the puck down the ice and take the icing whistle to get a fresh faceoff in your own zone.
U13 / Peewee.why: When all outlets are covered and a forechecker is coming from one side, reversing behind the net forces the forechecker to follow — buying a few seconds for outlets to get open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: All outlets covered and a forechecker coming? Reverse behind the net. Buy time.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the penalty kill. A power play player is standing right in front of your goalie screening them. The puck is at the point. What's your immediate priority?
opts: Go pressure the defenseman at the point who has the puck right now so they can't get off a clean shot through the screen.
Move the screener out of the crease — your goalie can't see the shot and that's more dangerous than the shot itself.
Chase the puck instead of covering your assignment.
Back up all the way to the crease yourself to help the goalie block any shot that might be coming from the point.
U13 / Peewee.why: A screener blocking your goalie's sightline on the PK is the most dangerous player on the ice. Moving them out is the priority — before the shot is taken.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Screener blocking your goalie on the PK? Move them out. Your goalie needs to see.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate is about to shoot from the half-wall. The goalie is set. You're the weak-side winger. What should you be doing?
opts: Stay right where you are in case the shot misses wide and comes out to your side cleanly.
Crashing the net hard on the weak side — be there for the rebound before the goalie can cover.
Back up all the way to your own blue line to protect against a breakaway the other way.
Call loudly for a pass instead of the shot so you can get a better chance from your side.
U13 / Peewee.why: The weak-side winger crashing the net on a shot is one of the highest-value plays without the puck. Rebounds bounce weak side — being there before the shot lands is how goals are scored.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Teammate shooting? Crash the net. Weak-side rebound is your job.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team wins a faceoff in the offensive zone. The opposing center had to tie the draw. The puck goes back to your defenseman at the point. Before the opposing center gets their bearings, what should you do?
opts: Wait patiently for the play to develop normally before you decide what to do with your positioning in the offensive zone right now.
Drive hard to the net immediately — the opposing center just spent all their energy on the draw and the lane to the net may be briefly open.
Skate all the way over to the corner and see what kind of play develops down low before you commit to any real position.
Call for a pass from the defenseman at the point so you can get the puck back and try to set up a play in the high slot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Winning a faceoff in the offensive zone creates a split-second window where the opposing center is off-balance. Driving hard to the net immediately exploits that window before they recover.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Win the OZ faceoff? Drive the net immediately. The center just fought the draw — they're recovering.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. Two teammates are available — one is at the half-wall with a clear shot lane, and one is right in front of the net with no lane but good positioning. Which do you pass to?
opts: The teammate in front of the net — they're in the best scoring position right on the doorstep.
The one at the half-wall — they have the open lane right now and can shoot immediately.
Hold the puck yourself.
Pass back to the point to reset the play.
U13 / Peewee.why: The immediate open lane wins over the better position without a lane. The half-wall player can shoot right now — the net-front player would need to receive, control, and find a lane. Open lane first.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two options — one with a lane, one without? Give it to the one who can shoot right now.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is in the defensive zone. The puck goes from the corner to the half-wall. As the nearest forward, you move to pressure the half-wall player. Who covers your corner assignment?
opts: Nobody — you handle both.
Your D partner rotates to cover the corner behind you the rotation.
Your winger on the other side skates across and trust the play to develop.
You stay in both spots somehow right away.
U13 / Peewee.why: When a forward pressures the half-wall, the D partner behind them rotates to cover the area the forward left. This is how defensive zone rotations work — everyone shifts together.
U13 / Peewee.tip: You go pressure the half-wall? Your D rotates behind you to cover. Everyone shifts.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three attackers are rushing at your two defensemen. All three attackers are at top speed. What's the biggest mistake a defenseman can make here?
opts: Backing up too fast and giving the attackers too much free space between the blue lines.
Backing up at the right pace, not overcommitting to the rush.
Staying in the middle of the ice and not moving to one side to take a specific attacker away.
Talking to their partner during the rush instead of just skating back in total silence.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching up against a fast rush hands the attacking team a massive speed advantage. Backing up at the right pace — not too fast, not too slow — is the correct response to a rush with speed.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rush coming with speed? Back up controlled. Never pinch up against a fast rush.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. Your D has the puck and is looking to pass. You're a winger and you're currently covered. When should you break to the wall to get the pass?
opts: Stay right where you are and trust that your defenseman will figure out the play on their own.
Skate to the wall hard, using your speed to lose the forechecker for a split second — make the D's decision easy.
Don't break to the wall until the forechecker has already left your area and given up the chase.
Stand completely still in your spot and call loudly for the puck to get your D's attention.
U13 / Peewee.why: Creating separation from your forechecker — even for a split second — is how you get open. Skate hard to the wall to create the separation that makes the pass possible.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Covered on the breakout? Skate hard to create separation. Make yourself open.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. You can shoot from where you are — a decent but not great angle. OR you can pass to a teammate in the slot who's slightly covered but would have a much better shot. What do you do?
opts: Shoot — you have the puck and a decent angle, so take the shot before anything changes.
Pass to the slot player — the higher danger location is worth the slight coverage risk.
Hold the puck and wait for a better chance.
Pass all the way back to the point and let the defenseman set up a new play from there.
U13 / Peewee.why: A slightly covered player in the slot still represents a higher-danger chance than a clean shot from a poor angle. The math of chance quality favors the slot, even with some risk.
U13 / Peewee.tip: OK angle vs. slightly covered slot? The slot wins. Higher danger is worth the risk.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're breaking into the offensive zone with speed and a pass is coming to you in stride. The goalie is set in the middle of the net. You have a half-second. What's the best play?
opts: Wait until you have the perfect angle to shoot.
One-time it low blocker side — the goalie hasn't moved yet and a one-touch in stride catches them off-balance.
Stickhandle to slow yourself down and aim carefully at the open corner before you take the shot at the set goalie.
Pass across to a winger on the other side who might have a better angle at the net than you do at this particular moment.
U13 / Peewee.why: A one-touch shot in stride catches the goalie before they can set against a stationary shooter. Stopping the puck gives the goalie time to adjust.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Pass coming to you in stride with a lane? One-time it. Don't stop the puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: There's a loose puck in your defensive zone right in front of your net. You and an opposing player are racing to it at the same time. What's your priority?
opts: Get to the puck first and try to skate it all the way out of the zone yourself before the opponent can close on you.
Get your body between the opponent and the puck — inside position wins this battle even if you get there second.
Poke at the puck with your stick from a distance and try to knock it loose without getting into the physical battle at all.
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and get the puck while you block the opposing player from reaching the front of the net.
U13 / Peewee.why: Inside position on a loose puck in front of your own net wins the battle even if you get there a step late. Body between opponent and puck the priority.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Loose puck in your own zone? Get inside position. Body between opponent and puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is on the power play trying to enter the offensive zone. The penalty killers are set at their blue line — two of them. How should your team attack?
opts: Carry the puck straight up the middle every time since you have the extra skater advantage anyway.
Use the extra skater advantage: hold the puck until one PK player commits, then move it to the open player.
Dump the puck deep into the corner and send all five skaters in hard to chase and recover it.
Skate back into your own zone and regroup over and over until the PK gets tired and breaks.
U13 / Peewee.why: The extra skater on the power play means someone is always open. Don't just carry into a set defense — hold until a PK player commits, then move the puck to who they left open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PP zone entry vs. set PK: hold until they commit. Then use the extra skater.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck carrier has the puck in the offensive zone but is being smothered by defenders. You don't have the puck and a defender is near you. What's the best thing you can do?
opts: Stand still and call for the puck loudly so the puck carrier knows exactly where you are and can make the pass to you.
Skate into open space — cut to the net, move to a seam. Make yourself available AND drag your defender out of the puck carrier's area.
Go help the puck carrier in the battle for the puck by skating right into the pile to try to get the puck loose for the team.
Skate out to the blue line right away so you're ready to make a breakout pass if the other team gets the puck back and clears.
U13 / Peewee.why: Off-puck movement does two things: it makes you available for a pass and it drags your defender away from the puck carrier — creating more space for them.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Puck carrier in trouble? Move without the puck. Create space for them by dragging your defender away.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your penalty kill clears the puck down the ice. Icing is called and the faceoff comes back to your defensive zone. Your two PK players are tired. What matters most before the next faceoff?
opts: Change lines immediately even if it causes confusion in the bench area because the tired players really do need a rest right away.
Get your two best faceoff players and PK coverage players out — fresh legs and the right assignments matter more than speed.
Stay out on the ice because the familiar lineup is better than rolling new players out cold without any time to prepare or set.
Send out the power play line instead because they have the best sticks on the team and can probably win the faceoff cleanly.
U13 / Peewee.why: A faceoff after an icing on the PK is a chance to reset with the right personnel. Getting the right PK players and faceoff specialist on the ice matters more than rushing.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK icing faceoff? Get the right players out. Fresh legs and right assignments.

U13 / Peewee.concept: Read the rush before it happens
U13 / Peewee.sit: You're in the offensive zone and the puck is about to turn over. Before it does, where should you already be moving?
opts: Stay exactly where you are in the offensive zone and wait to see if the turnover really happens.
Start transitioning early — turn toward your own zone before the turnover is complete. Get a head start.
Skate to the bench.
Go to the corner.
U13 / Peewee.why: The best backcheckers start their transition before the puck has fully changed hands. Reading that a turnover is about to happen and moving early gives you a huge head start.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Turnover about to happen? Start moving early. Get a head start on the backcheck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck in the offensive zone. A teammate is in the slot but a defender is between you and them. You can see a sliver of the teammate. What do you do?
opts: Force the pass through the defender — your teammate is in the slot and you can see them.
Find a better option — a covered pass through a defender is high-risk. Look for the winger or the point.
Hold the puck and skate around.
Take the shot from where you.
U13 / Peewee.why: A pass through a defender risks a turnover and an immediate counter-attack. If a defender is between you and your target, find a different option — the pass isn't open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender in your passing lane? Find a different option. A blocked pass is a turnover.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the defenseman at the offensive blue line. Your winger just gained possession in the corner. Should you pinch to join the attack below the dots?
opts: Yes — always pinch hard and join every attack so you can keep the puck deep in the zone.
Only if the other winger or center is positioned to cover the blue line behind you — otherwise hold the line.
Yes — pinch every single time your winger touches the puck, no matter who is covering behind you.
No — never pinch from the blue line under any circumstances because it is always too risky.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pinching is right only when someone is positioned behind you to cover the blue line. Pinching without coverage gives up a breakout against you if the puck turns over.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Want to pinch? Make sure someone covers the line behind you first. Then go.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team is trying to enter your zone 4-on-4. Your team has set up in the neutral zone with all four players between the puck and your net. The puck carrier is coming at you. What's the key to making this work?
opts: Everyone chase the puck carrier at the same time to surround them and force a quick turnover.
Hold your positions and make the puck carrier make a decision — force them one way and take away the easiest options.
All four players skate backwards to your own blue line to protect the net area immediately.
One player steps up to challenge while the other three retreat all the way back to the crease.
U13 / Peewee.why: A neutral zone defensive structure works when everyone holds their lane and forces the puck carrier into a decision. If everyone chases, the structure collapses.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Neutral zone defense: hold your lane. Force the puck carrier to decide. Don't all chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team wins a faceoff in the neutral zone and the puck goes back to your defenseman. You're the center — you just took the faceoff. What do you do now?
opts: Stand completely still at the faceoff dot and watch what your defenseman decides to do with the puck before you even consider making any kind of next move up the ice at all.
Transition immediately to your assignment — if you won the draw, get to your position in the rush development. The faceoff result gives your team the advantage if everyone executes.
Skate straight back to the bench for a quick line change because the puck is already safe with your defenseman and your shift is basically over, so the next line can handle the rush.
Celebrate winning the draw with your linemates and tap sticks, because winning a neutral-zone faceoff is a big deal and your whole team should enjoy the moment before the next play.
U13 / Peewee.why: Winning a faceoff is just the start — executing the assignment immediately after is what turns a faceoff win into a scoring chance. The center transitions to their rush role.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Win the faceoff? Execute your next assignment immediately. Don't watch — go.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shot comes from the blue line. You're standing in front of the net. The puck is coming at the middle of the net right at the goalie. What's your job?
opts: Get out of the way fast so your goalie has a completely clear look at the incoming shot from the point.
Tip the puck — even a slight redirection completely changes the trajectory and defeats the goalie's.
Call loudly for the shot to go wide of the net so the puck rims around for a rebound chance.
Skate away from the blue paint quickly so you don't block your goalie's view of the incoming shot.
U13 / Peewee.why: Tipping a shot on net is one of the most effective plays in hockey. Even a small deflection changes the trajectory enough to beat a goalie who is tracking the original shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Shot coming from the point right at the goalie? Tip it. Any deflection beats their tracking.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck in your own zone under pressure. You have two options: a tricky cross-ice pass to a teammate at the blue line, or a simple short pass to your D partner three feet away. Which do you take?
opts: The tricky cross-ice pass — it leads to a faster breakout.
The simple short pass to your D partner — take the safe option under pressure and let them move the puck from a better position.
Try to carry it yourself around the back.
Wait for a better option to appear because one of your teammates will probably get open soon if you just hold the puck.
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure in your own zone, the simple play is almost always right. A tricky cross-ice pass into pressure risks a turnover in the worst possible area.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two options under pressure? Take the simple one. Safe pass first, then build.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team is defending in your own zone. The puck is in the far corner. The other team has three attackers in the zone and you have two defenders and one forward back. Where should the forward position?
opts: Skate down into the corner to help your partner battle for the puck and win it back quickly.
Hold the high slot — be between the corner player and the open attackers in the high-danger area.
Go all the way to your blue line.
Follow the nearest attacker wherever they go around the zone and stay glued to them tightly.
U13 / Peewee.why: With three attackers in the zone and only three defenders, the forward holds the high slot to take away the most dangerous pass options. Going to the corner leaves the front of the net completely open.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Three attackers, you're the lone forward back? Hold the high slot. Don't go to the corner.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The other team takes a shot and your goalie makes the save. The rebound goes to the corner. An opposing player is racing to it. What do you do?
opts: Watch carefully to see if your goalie covers the rebound or plays the puck before you commit to going into the corner.
Get to the rebound before the opposing player — or at least get your body position between them and the puck.
Call loudly for your goalie to come out and play the puck in the corner before the opposing player can get to it first.
Back up all the way to the crease.
U13 / Peewee.why: Beating the opposing player to the rebound — or at minimum getting inside position — prevents the second shot. Letting them get a clean rebound in the corner means a second chance against you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Rebound in the corner? Race to it or get inside position. Prevent the second shot.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D picks up the puck in your own corner. They're looking up but they can't see behind them. Your winger is open up the wall but a forechecker is about to close on them from the blind side. What do you do?
opts: Say nothing and let the D figure it out themselves.
Call out 'man on!' or tell the winger to hold — give the information that only you can see.
Skate over yourself to get between the forechecker and the winger before the pass arrives.
Call for the pass yourself.
U13 / Peewee.why: You can see something neither the D nor the winger can see. That information is valuable — communicating it immediately makes the play better.
U13 / Peewee.tip: You see something others can't? Say it loud and fast. That's your job.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your penalty kill clears the puck all the way into the other team's zone. You're the first PK player over the other team's blue line. What do you do?
opts: Slow down because it's probably just going to come back out of their zone anyway so there's no point in really chasing it.
Attack hard — a PK icing or clear can become a shorthanded chance if you pursue it aggressively. Speed wins that race.
Go straight to the bench for a quick line change since the clear is long enough to get fresh legs out onto the ice safely.
Wait for your penalty kill teammate to come up the ice with you so you can pressure the puck together as a tandem up there.
U13 / Peewee.why: A PK clear that gets into the other team's zone is a chance for a shorthanded goal if you pursue aggressively. The other team is coming back for it — getting there first wins the race.
U13 / Peewee.tip: PK clear going deep? Attack it. Speed wins the race and shorthanded goals are game-changers.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your team has been hemmed in your own zone for 45 seconds. Your D picks up the puck. Before they do anything else, what's the single most important question they should answer?
opts: What is the current score of the game and how much time is left on the clock right now?
Which outlet is actually open — not which one I want to use, but which one is actually free?
Where is the nearest bench door so I can get a fresh change of players out on the ice now?
What did the coach say about the breakout during the pre-game meeting in the locker room?
U13 / Peewee.why: Under pressure after a long zone time, the temptation is to default to a favorite outlet. But reading which option is actually open — not which one you want — is what leads to a clean exit.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Long zone time, D finally has the puck? Read what's actually open. Don't default.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're defending in your own zone. The puck is in the corner behind your net. Your assignment is an attacker near the half-wall on the opposite side. The puck is far away from them. Do you watch the puck or watch your check?
opts: Watch the puck carefully because it's way more important to know where the puck is than to know where your assignment is.
Watch your check — keep them in front of you so you know when they move. You can track the puck with your peripheral vision.
Watch both equally at once.
Go get the puck yourself in the corner.
U13 / Peewee.why: In man coverage, losing sight of your assignment is how they get open for a pass. Keep them in front of you and use your peripheral vision for the puck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Man coverage in your own zone: watch your check, not the puck. Use your peripheral vision for the puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're playing hard in the offensive zone on a long shift. The puck turns over and the other team is breaking out. Your legs are tired. What's the responsible play?
opts: Push through your tiredness because the play needs you and your linemates are counting on you to come back on the backcheck.
If you can't backcheck at full speed, get off and get a fresh player on. A tired player is worse than no player.
Slow jog back to the D-zone.
Call out to the other team to stop their rush and give your team a chance to get set up in the defensive zone properly.
U13 / Peewee.why: A tired player who can't backcheck at full speed is a liability, not an asset. The responsible play is to get off the ice and get a fresh player in who can actually defend.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Legs gone and the other team is breaking out? Get off. Fresh player is better than a tired one.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You have the puck at center ice. Three options: your winger is open on the left but a defender is closing on them. Your center is open in the slot with a clear lane. Your D is open at the blue line on the right. What do you do?
opts: Pass to the winger on the left — they were the first option you saw in your vision.
Pass to the center in the slot — open lane, dangerous position, immediate scoring threat.
Pass to the D on the right — they're the safest option and nobody is close to them.
Carry it in yourself.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading all three options before picking tells you the center in the slot with a clear lane is the best play. The winger has a closing defender and the D is the safest but least dangerous option. Best chance wins.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Multiple options? Read all of them before you pick. The open slot beats everything else here.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Three players from both teams are battling in front of your net. Sticks and bodies everywhere. The puck is somewhere in the mess. What's your priority?
opts: Watch the bodies carefully.
Keep your eyes moving — track the puck through the traffic. Move with the puck, not with the players.
Wait patiently for the puck to come out of the pile into a clear area where you can see it and make a save.
Drop immediately into your butterfly to cover the bottom of the net in case the puck squeaks out right at the goal line.
U13 / Peewee.why: Traffic in front is the hardest goalie situation. The key is to track the puck through the bodies, not get distracted by movement. The puck is what can score — bodies can't.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Traffic in front? Track the puck, not the bodies. The puck tells you everything.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck is on the half-wall. You're set on the post. A player cuts to the back door completely open on the far side. What do you do?
opts: Stay on your near post.
Quickly push across to take away the back-door pass — your near post is less dangerous.
Skate out hard toward the puck carrier to challenge them right at the top of the crease.
Stay exactly where you are.
U13 / Peewee.why: A back-door pass to an open player is one of the highest-danger plays in hockey. When you see the cut, you have to push across quickly to take away that option — the shooter at the half-wall is less dangerous than an open player at the back post.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Back-door cut? Push across. The post can wait — the open player is the emergency.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You make a save and the puck is loose right in front of you. Two opposing players are about to get to it before your team. Should you cover it or try to play it?
opts: Always cover the puck for a faceoff.
Read the situation — if two opponents are about to get it, cover it immediately. If your team has it, leave it for them.
Always leave the puck for your team no matter what's happening in front of you because your team needs to start the breakout play.
Kick the puck away from the crease as hard as you can to anywhere so the other team can't get a second shot on you at all.
U13 / Peewee.why: The decision to freeze or play is a real read. If opponents are about to get to a dangerous rebound, cover it. If your team has control, leaving it lets them start the breakout faster.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Freeze or play? Read who's going to get it. Cover when opponents are closest.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shooter is coming at you. You notice their shoulder is turned slightly toward the left side of the net before they shoot. What does this tell you?
opts: Nothing — ignore the shoulder and just play the puck straight up the way you would with any other shooter coming at you.
They're likely shooting to your right — their body is opening to the left which usually means the shot goes.
They're going to deke around you to the left.
They'll pass.
U13 / Peewee.why: Reading a shooter's body language — especially shoulder position — is an advanced skill that top goalies develop. A shoulder turning left often means the shot goes right because the body follows the shot direction.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Read the shooter's shoulders and hips before they release. They tell you where it's going.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're down and out of position — the shooter has a completely open net. You can't get back to your feet in time. What do you do?
opts: Give up the goal and just see what happens next because there's really nothing else you can do from flat on the ice like this.
Use whatever you can reach — your stick, your glove, your skate — to get something in the way. Any part of you in the lane is better than nothing.
Stay completely still on the ice and hope the shooter somehow misses the wide-open net that you've given them with the mistake.
Call for help from your defensemen so they can slide in front of the shooter and take away the shooting lane from where you're down.
U13 / Peewee.why: Desperation saves are real. Even if you're out of position, getting any part of your body or equipment into the shooting lane can change a goal into a save. Never stop until the whistle.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Out of position? Reach for it anyway. Desperate saves happen. Never quit.

U13 / Peewee.sit: The puck moves quickly from the left corner to the right corner. You were on your left post. What's the fastest way to get to the right post?
opts: Skate slowly and carefully across the crease to make sure you don't lose your balance or get caught out of position by the play.
Push off the left post and use a strong T-push or shuffle across — keep your eyes on the puck the whole time.
Turn around and skate quickly across.
Stay on the left post a bit too long and hope the puck comes back to your side before you commit to moving across the crease.
U13 / Peewee.why: A T-push off the post is the fastest way for a goalie to move across the crease. You push with your post foot and slide across, keeping your eyes on the puck.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Post-to-post: T-push off the near post. Stay low, eyes on puck. It's a race.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the lone defender on a 2-on-1 rush. Put your defensive steps in order.
items: Skate backward and get between the two attackers
Take away the pass by positioning your stick in the passing lane
Force the puck carrier to the outside
Trust your goalie to handle the shot
U13 / Peewee.why: On a 2-on-1, the defender's job is to take away the pass and force a shot. Get position, stick in the lane, steer the shooter wide, and let the goalie do their job.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1 defense: get between, take the pass away, force wide, trust your goalie.

U13 / Peewee.q: Tap your team in the order they should touch the puck on a clean breakout: D1 picks it up, then?
U13 / Peewee.tip: D1 → weak-side D (D2) for a reverse → strong-side winger up the wall → center through the middle.

U13 / Peewee.q: You're F3, trailing the rush. Draw your path to the best support position.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Trail high — through the middle to the top of the slot gives you shot support AND backcheck coverage if it turns over.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're about to receive a pass along the wall. When should you first look up?
opts: Before the puck gets to you.
Right when you touch it.
After you've stickhandled a couple times.
Only if a defender is near.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pre-scan — look up BEFORE the puck arrives. When it's on your stick, you already know what to do.
U13 / Peewee.tip: See it before you feel it.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Elite players look up at least two or three times during a single puck possession.
U13 / Peewee.why: Pre-scanning is a habit the best players do constantly. Each look updates the map — where teammates and defenders moved.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Head up, over and over.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You've got the puck in the offensive zone. Your eyes should mostly be on...
opts: Space, teammates, and defenders.
The puck.
The goalie.
Your own skates.
U13 / Peewee.why: The puck is on your stick — you can feel it. Your eyes should be scanning the ice for options.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Eyes = teammates. Feel = puck.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your goalie freezes the puck. Your D-partner grabs it behind the net for a breakout. Where should you be?
opts: Open on your side boards, ready to receive.
Crashing the net from behind.
Standing behind your D.
At center ice.
U13 / Peewee.why: Wingers hug the wall on their side for the D-to-W pass. That's the cornerstone breakout.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Winger on the wall, stick on the ice.

U13 / Peewee.sit: If the winger on your breakout is covered, a good option is to reverse the puck behind the net.
U13 / Peewee.why: The reverse (D-to-D behind the net) is a staple escape when the first read isn't there.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Winger covered? Reverse.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the center on a breakout. Where's your ideal route?
opts: Swing low, then build speed through the middle.
Stand at the blue line.
Stay next to the wall.
Behind your goalie.
U13 / Peewee.why: Centers swing low, pick up speed, and provide the late middle option. That timing pressures the other team all the way up.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Center = low-to-high, fast.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shooter breaks in 1-on-1 on you from the right wing. First priority?
opts: Get to the top of your crease and square to the shooter.
Drop into a butterfly right away.
Skate to the hash marks.
Close your eyes.
U13 / Peewee.why: Challenge the angle first. Square and take away net — then decide if the shot or deke forces you into the butterfly.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Square. Challenge. Then react.

U13 / Peewee.sit: On a cross-crease pass, the goalie's push should lead with the far-side leg.
U13 / Peewee.why: Leading with the far-side leg (T-push or butterfly slide) seals the ice and gets you across faster than a two-foot shuffle.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cross-crease? Far leg first.

U13 / Peewee.sit: A shot is screened by your own defenseman. What do you do?
opts: Drop into butterfly and take away the bottom half of the net.
Try to skate around the screen.
Yell at your D.
Wave your glove.
U13 / Peewee.why: Screened shots beat goalies low. Drop, get big, and take away the ice you can't see.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Screened? Get big, get low.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your D-partner is chasing a puck into the corner in your own end. Where should you be?
opts: In the slot, covering net-front.
Right next to them in the corner.
At center ice.
Behind your own net.
U13 / Peewee.why: One D to the puck, the other to the net-front — that's the pairing rule. Never leave the slot empty.
U13 / Peewee.tip: One to puck, one to net.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Good support = always right next to your teammate with the puck.
U13 / Peewee.why: Too close = you're not a release valve. Support is about being reachable AND useful — usually a zone away, not a foot away.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Close enough to help, not crowd.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your winger gets hemmed along the wall. You're the center. Best support position?
opts: High slot, ready for a chip-up or to cover if they lose it.
Down low with them.
Behind the goalie.
At the red line.
U13 / Peewee.why: Centers play high to give the chip-up and to back-pressure if the battle gets lost.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Winger low, center high.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're skating into the offensive zone with speed and a 1-on-2 against you. What's the play?
opts: Chip it deep and forecheck.
Try to skate through both defenders.
Pass back to your D at the line.
Dump it out of the zone.
U13 / Peewee.why: 1-on-2s rarely produce scoring chances. Chip-and-chase flips the rink and puts you on the forecheck — way better odds.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 1-on-2? Chip and chase.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Great decision-making = always making the flashy play.
U13 / Peewee.why: Great decision-making = picking the right play for the moment. Sometimes it's flashy, usually it isn't.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Right > flashy.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Late in a period with a one-goal lead, you have the puck in the neutral zone with a risky stretch-pass available. What's the call?
opts: Safe chip or soft dump-in.
Force the stretch pass.
Skate backward.
Shoot it toward their empty blue line.
U13 / Peewee.why: Game management — late with a lead, high-risk passes go the wrong way. Chip it deep, change lines, reset.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Up a goal late? Simple.

U13 / Peewee.q: A forward is coming at you with speed through the neutral zone. Drag to your ideal gap.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stay 1-2 stick lengths away, angle them toward the boards, stick in the lane.

targets.feedback: ✓ Tight gap, angling them outside, stick available. Classic D read.
Way too passive. You just gave them the whole neutral zone.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You're the first forward in on the forecheck. The opposing defenseman just picked up the puck behind their net.
U13 / Peewee.q: Which of these are GOOD reads for F1?
opts: Take an angle that pushes the puck-carrier to one side
Skate straight at them down the middle of the ice
Keep your stick on the ice in the lane to the other defenseman
Communicate which side you're forcing so your linemates know
U13 / Peewee.tip: F1 forces a side — never charge straight at the puck.
U13 / Peewee.why: Charging straight gives the puck-carrier both sides of the ice. Angling, taking the D-to-D pass with your stick, and calling your side are F1's three jobs.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Your teammate has the puck along the boards in the offensive zone, and a defender is closing in fast.
U13 / Peewee.q: Which of these are good support options for YOU as the closest forward?
opts: Stand still and yell for the puck
Skate to a soft area where your teammate can pass to you
Keep your stick on the ice as a target
Make eye contact and call for it once you're open
U13 / Peewee.tip: Good support is moving + reachable + audible.
U13 / Peewee.why: Standing still makes you easy to cover. Moving to space, showing your blade, and a quick call — that's how you become an outlet.

U13 / Peewee.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck up your weak side as you skate backward.
