U13 / Peewee.q: Which of these are part of good 1-on-1 defense?
opts: Match their speed so the gap stays the same
Keep your stick on the ice in the puck-side lane
Look at their feet so they can't fake you out
Keep your hips squared so you can pivot either way
U13 / Peewee.tip: Watch the chest, not the feet — feet lie, the chest goes where they're going.
U13 / Peewee.why: Watching the feet is how you get burned by a fake. Speed match, stick in the lane, and squared hips are the fundamentals.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You and your linemate are setting up a cycle in the offensive zone. The puck is on the half-wall.
U13 / Peewee.q: Which of these are good cycle options?
opts: Pass back to the defenseman at the point
Throw a blind backhand to the slot hoping someone's there
Keep moving — find soft ice for the next pass
Bump it down low to a teammate behind the net
U13 / Peewee.tip: Cycle = puck movement + skater movement. Blind passes kill possessions.
U13 / Peewee.why: Throwing it blind into the slot is how you get a turnover. Point, low D, and finding soft ice are all designed cycle reads.

U13 / Peewee.sit: You enter the offensive zone on a 2-on-1. The defender has committed his stick and body weight to the puck side. What's the highest-percentage play?
opts: Shoot — the goalie is square and might be beatable
Pass cross-ice — your teammate has an open lane
Drive wide and protect the puck
Chip and chase past the defender
U13 / Peewee.why: Defender stick is on the puck side — he's taking away your shot. Your teammate has a clean cross-ice lane to a goalie who has to push across the crease. Pass creates a 50%+ scoring chance vs. ~10% for a shot against a square goalie. Textbook 2-on-1 read: read what he's taking away, do the opposite.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Defender stick is on the puck side — he's taking away your shot.

opts: True

U13 / Peewee.why: Stick is on your side. Pass lane is wide open. A shot is the lower-percentage play even if it occasionally scores.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick is on your side.

opts: Hotspot target: the open ice between the defender and the teammate on the right side
U13 / Peewee.why: That's the open lane. Lead your teammate slightly so the puck arrives on his stick at full stride.
U13 / Peewee.tip: That's the open lane.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Fill in the blank: When the defender on a 2-on-1 plays the puck carrier, the right play is to ___.
opts: Accepted answers: pass / pass cross-ice / make a cross-ice pass
U13 / Peewee.why: Read what's taken away, do the opposite. When the defender commits to the puck, the pass lane is what he's giving you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Read what's taken away, do the opposite.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. U13 / Peewee.sit: Which physical cue most reliably tells you what a defender is taking away?
opts: Body lean
Stick position
Skating speed
Eye contact
U13 / Peewee.why: A stick can only be in one place. Body lean and skating speed can mislead. Stick position is the single most reliable read on any defender — train your eyes to find it first.
U13 / Peewee.tip: A stick can only be in one place.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. U13 / Peewee.sit: What hockey concept describes the read in this image?
opts: Give-and-go
Cycle
Cross-ice read
Net drive
U13 / Peewee.why: A cross-ice read exploits the geometry of one defender trying to cover two attackers across the width of the ice. The defender can't move fast enough laterally to recover from a committed position — that's the entire reason this read works.
U13 / Peewee.tip: A cross-ice read exploits the geometry of one defender trying to cover two attackers across the width of the ice.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Why is passing the higher-percentage play here, even when the goalie looks beatable?
opts: Passing is always better than shooting on a 2-on-1
The defender has committed to your side, leaving the cross-ice lane open AND forcing the goalie to scramble across the crease
Your teammate is closer to the net
The goalie can't react to passes
U13 / Peewee.why: Two things compound here. The defender took away your shot AND positioned the goalie for you specifically. A pass exploits both — your teammate gets the puck, AND the goalie has to push across the crease to face him. That's how the percentages get to 50%+ vs. ~10% for the shot.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Two things compound here.

U13 / Peewee.sit: If this same defender had his stick extended to the OTHER side (toward your teammate's pass lane), what would your best play become?
opts: Still pass
Shoot — the lane to the net would be open
Skate around the defender
Drop the puck back
U13 / Peewee.why: Stick on you = pass. Stick on the pass lane = shoot. The same image with the stick flipped becomes a completely different decision. This is why you don't pre-decide before reading — you decide based on what the defender shows you.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick on you = pass.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. U13 / Peewee.sit: Put these decision steps in the correct order for reading a 2-on-1.
opts: Sequence to order: (1) Identify what the defender is taking away, (2) Cross the blue line with speed, (3) Choose the option the defender is giving you, (4) Execute the play with confidence
U13 / Peewee.why: The mistake most young players make is choosing the play before reading the defender. Order matters: enter the zone, read what's available, make the call, commit fully. Pre-deciding before reading is what causes blown 2-on-1s. CORRECT ORDER: 2 → 1 → 3 → 4 (Cross blue line → Identify → Choose → Execute).
U13 / Peewee.tip: The mistake most young players make is choosing the play before reading the defender.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. U13 / Peewee.sit: A real 2-on-1 happens in about 2 seconds. What ONE thing should your eyes find first when you cross the blue line?
opts: Accepted answers: the defender's stick / where his stick is / stick position / the stick
U13 / Peewee.why: Stick first. Always. Coaches who say 'read the play' really mean 'find the stick.'
U13 / Peewee.tip: Stick first.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. U13 / Peewee.sit: Many young players shoot in this exact situation instead of passing. What's the mental error driving that mistake?
opts: They want the goal for themselves
They haven't been taught to read the defender
They see the goalie and forget to look at the defender
All of the above
U13 / Peewee.why: All three are real and they reinforce each other. Players see the goalie because the goalie is the goal — but the defender is the decision. Selfish shots feel good even when they're wrong. And without coaching that names 'read what he's taking away,' the read never becomes automatic.
U13 / Peewee.tip: All three are real and they reinforce each other.

media.alt: The defender's stick blade is fully extended to his right (the puck-side passing lane), and his right skate is angled outward toward the puck side, indicating his weight has committed to the puck side. The right side of the ice between the defender and the teammate is visibly open, leaving the cross-ice pass as the correct read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: True or False: A shot that goes in the net was the right play, even if a pass would have created a better chance.
opts: True
False
U13 / Peewee.why: Outcomes lie. A bad decision can produce a good result, and a great decision can produce a bad result. In hockey, the read is judged by what was available, not what happened. A goal off a bad shot is still a bad shot. A missed pass that should have been a goal is still the right read.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Outcomes lie.

opts: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight
U13 / Peewee.why: Outcome bias is judging a decision by how it turned out instead of whether it was the right call given what you knew at the time. Smart players (and smart coaches) judge the read, not the result.
U13 / Peewee.tip: Outcome bias is judging a decision by how it turned out instead of whether it was the right call given what you knew at the time.

U13 / Peewee.tip: When the defender steps up, the gap is the read.

U13 / Peewee.sit: Many young players get hit or turn over the puck in this exact situation. What's the mental error driving that mistake?
opts: They freeze under time pressure
They try to skate around the defender (which works against passive defenders, not aggressive ones)
They don't trust 'losing the puck' even when chipping it past is the right play
All of the above
U13 / Peewee.why: All three. Players freeze under time pressure because they haven't built the pattern recognition to know what to do in a sub-second window. They try to skate around because that's what works against passive defenders. And they don't trust the chip because they think 'losing the puck' is bad — but a chip into open space isn't losing it, it's relocating it.
U13 / Peewee.tip: All three.

False

A turnover off a chip-past doesn't mean the chip was wrong — sometimes plays don't work because of execution, ice conditions, or your teammate not reading the situation. The READ (chip past an aggressive defender) is correct. Whether your teammate gets there in time is a separate variable.

opts: Accepted answers: outcome / outcome bias / results / hindsight / results-oriented
U13 / Peewee.why: Outcome bias works in both directions in time-pressure plays. A successful chip past an aggressive defender doesn't mean the chip was the only right play. A turnover doesn't mean it was the wrong read. The skill is judging the read, not the outcome.

I can't deke or skate around because his momentum is coming AT me — I'd just run into him. His aggression created the space — I'm using it against him. Even if it doesn't work out, the read was right.'

U13 / Peewee.q: 2-on-1 coming at you, you're the lone D. Your goalie is set. What do you do?
choices: Take the carrier — force them to pass through you
Take away the cross-ice pass — let the goalie handle the shot
Back off and let your back-checker arrive
U13 / Peewee.why: On a 2-on-1, the D's job is to TAKE THE PASS. The goalie owns the shooter — but only if you don't try to play both. Sliding to break up the cross-ice forces a tougher shot angle on the carrier.
U13 / Peewee.tip: 2-on-1: take the pass. Goalie has the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An attacker is carrying the puck through the neutral zone at you. You're the lone D back. Where should your gap be?
opts: One stick length at the top of the circles to force a quick decision.
Back up to your own goal line to give yourself maximum time to react.
At the far blue line so you can close the distance with a big.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight gap at the top of the circles forces the attacker to make their move early. Too far back gives them time and space.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Gap control: top of the circles. Force their hand early.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier dumps the puck in and skates past you along the boards. They no longer have the puck. Should you finish the check?
opts: Yes — finishing the check discourages them from coming back for it.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Yes — hit every player near you regardless of where the puck went.
No — save your energy for the next play and let them go past you.
U15 / Bantam.why: Finishing the check on a player who just released the puck is legal and smart. It separates them from the play.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Just dumped it? Finish the check. Legal and effective.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D has the puck behind the net. The forecheck sends one player hard. You're the center. Where should you be?
opts: In the middle around the hashmarks providing a short central option.
In the corner on the same side helping the D win the board battle.
At the far blue line waiting for a long stretch pass over instead.
Behind the net with the D so you can take a direct handoff.
U15 / Bantam.why: The center's breakout job is to be the middle option at the hashmarks — short, safe, and transitions the puck quickly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Center on the breakout: hashmarks. Be the middle option.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team turns the puck over in the offensive zone. You're the high forward. The opposing center has the puck. What's your job?
opts: Pick up the most dangerous opposing player and skate with them back.
Chase the puck carrier from behind and try to poke check.
Skate to your bench for a change since fresh legs are more instead.
Stay in the offensive zone in case your team gets the puck back.
U15 / Bantam.why: The high forward's backchecking job is to pick up the most dangerous player. Chasing the puck carrier from behind rarely works.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Turnover? Pick up a check. Don't chase — cover the dangerous player.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is killing a penalty in a box formation. The PP moves the puck from the half-wall to the point. What does the box do?
opts: Shift up and toward the puck as a unit to pressure and close lanes.
Collapse to the net and let the point player shoot.
Sag the stick-side forward into the shot lane and force the goalie to see it clean.
Rotate your low kill into a 1-3 so one forward can attack the puck aggressively.
U15 / Bantam.why: The PK box shifts as a unit toward the puck. When it goes to the point, the box shifts up to close shooting and passing lanes.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK box: shift as a unit toward the puck. Stay connected.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're F1 on the forecheck. The D picks up the puck and starts to skate behind their net. How do you approach?
opts: Angle your skating to cut off one side and force them toward your F2.
Skate directly at them at full speed to try to get a big hit.
Peel off and go back to the neutral zone since they have possession.
Slow down and wait to see what they do before committing to any.
U15 / Bantam.why: Angling as F1 takes away options. Cut off one side and force them into your support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: F1 forecheck: angle and cut off one side. Force them into support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D and an opposing forward is setting up in front of your net for a screen or deflection. What do you do?
opts: Tie up their stick and box them out of the prime scoring area there.
Push them hard from behind with both hands to get them away.
Ignore them and focus only on the puck carrier since that's.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.

U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent at your net front? Tie up stick. Box out. Protect goalie's eyes.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your coach runs a 2-1-2 forecheck. You're one of the two forwards going in first. How does this differ from a 1-2-2?
opts: You and your partner pressure together aggressively to create turnovers.
You go in alone first and your partner hangs back at the red line area.
You both stay at center ice and wait for them to bring the puck to you.
You pinch from the blue line while your partner forechecks alone there.
U15 / Bantam.why: In a 2-1-2, two forwards pressure together. More aggressive than 1-2-2 with immediate support.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 2-1-2: two in, pressure together. More aggressive with immediate support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has the puck in the O-zone and it gets turned over. You're the nearest forward. What's the first thing you do?
opts: Apply immediate pressure on the new puck carrier to force a quick error.
Chase the puck carrier all the way back into your defensive zone alone.
Skate straight to the bench for a change since your offensive shift.
Stand still and watch what the other team does before you react.
U15 / Bantam.why: Immediate pressure on the turnover is the first line of defense. Force a quick decision before they break out.
U15 / Bantam.tip: OZ turnover? Pressure immediately. Force a bad decision.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're having a great shift creating chances. But you've been on the ice for over a minute. What do you do?
opts: Get off the ice for a change — tired players make costly mistakes.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Wait until the puck is in the O-zone and then stay on.
Keep playing until you score since you are the best option out.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shift discipline matters more than individual performance. Tired players make turnovers that cost goals.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Great shift but tired? Get off. Discipline over heroics.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck carrier enters the neutral zone with speed. You're the D. They're fast. How does their speed change your gap?
opts: Slightly bigger gap so they can't blow past you with their speed.
Tighter gap because fast players need to be challenged before.
Same gap regardless of how fast the puck carrier is skating at.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a fast puck carrier, a slightly bigger gap prevents getting caught flat-footed.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Fast carrier? Slightly bigger gap. Don't get caught flat-footed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is killing a penalty. The opposing D has the puck in their zone setting up their PP breakout. What's your approach as the forechecker?
opts: Controlled pressure — take away the easy first pass without going deep.
Send both forwards hard on the forecheck to force a turnover in their zone.
Stand still at your blue line and let them walk the puck up unchecked.
Drop into a 1-3 NZ trap and dare them to dump it in.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK forechecking is controlled pressure. Take away the easy pass without overcommitting.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK forecheck: controlled pressure. Don't get caught deep.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has an offensive zone faceoff. The coach calls a set play — puck back to the point for a shot. What's everyone's job?
opts: Win the draw back, forwards set a screen, defenseman shoots through.
Everyone crashes the net immediately after the puck drops.
The center tries to score directly off the faceoff since that's instead.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: Set plays require execution from all five players. Win it back, screen, shoot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Faceoff set play: everyone has a job. Execute together.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team has been running a 2-1-2 forecheck but the other team keeps breaking it with quick D-to-D passes. What adjustment should you suggest?
opts: Take away the D-to-D pass by positioning F1 between the two D when they have the puck.
Keep running the same 2-1-2 since eventually it will work if you just skate.
Switch to a 1-2-2 trap and don't forecheck at all since nothing seems to be working.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: If D-to-D passes are beating your forecheck, F1 needs to position between the two D to take that option away. Adjusting forecheck positioning is smarter than just skating harder.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D-to-D beating you? F1 positions between the two D. Take it away.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is breaking out cleanly. You're a D and the forwards have speed through the neutral zone. There's a trailer lane open. Should you jump into the rush?
opts: Yes — fill the trailer lane and give your team an extra option in the offensive zone attack.
No — both D should always stay back regardless of how clean the breakout looks.
Only jump if the other team has already committed all their players back into their zone.
Wait until the forwards dump the puck in and then join the forecheck as the fifth.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the breakout is clean and there's a trailer lane, an activating D creates an overload in the offensive zone. The key is reading when it's safe.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Clean breakout with a trailer lane? Jump in. Extra option in the attack.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's PP is set up in the zone. The PK is in a tight box. Every pass to the slot is getting intercepted. What do you do?
opts: Move the puck around the perimeter faster to force the box to shift and create the seam.
Keep forcing passes to the slot since eventually one will get through if you throw instead.
Pull the puck out of the zone and re-enter to reset since the formation isn't.
Have all five players crash the net at the same time to create chaos in front.
U15 / Bantam.why: A tight PK box is beaten by fast puck movement around the perimeter. Force them to shift — the seam opens when they can't rotate fast enough.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tight PK box? Move the puck faster around the perimeter. Force them to shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're one of two D on a 3-on-2 rush. The three attackers are spread across the ice. The puck is in the middle. What's your read?
opts: You take the wide player on your side and trust your partner to take the other and play shot.
Both D collapse to the middle to take away the shot and leave the wide players for your.
Both D back up to the goal line together and let the goalie handle everything.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: On a 3-on-2, each D takes a wide player. The middle player (shooter) is the goalie's responsibility. Collapsing both D to the middle leaves two wide options open.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 3-on-2: each D takes a wide player. Goalie plays the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An opposing forward receives a pass in the slot with their head down, looking at the puck. You're the nearest D. What's the right play?
opts: Close quickly and deliver a clean check through their body to separate them from the puck.
Stand back and poke check from a distance since body contact in the slot is too risky.
Wait for them to shoot and then try to block the shot with your body as it comes instead.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
U15 / Bantam.why: A player with their head down in the slot is vulnerable. A clean check through the body separates them from the puck and sends a message.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Head down in the slot? Close and separate. Clean hit, big message.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D at the offensive blue line. The puck is dumped into the corner by your forward. The opposing D gets to it first. You see their winger starting to leak out. What do you do?
opts: Hold the blue line and be ready to close on the winger if they try to exit on your side.
Pinch into the corner to try to force a turnover since your forward needs help winning it.
Skate backward to center ice to give yourself a head start in case the puck comes back out.
Leave the blue line completely and go help your forward in the corner win the puck instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: With a winger leaking out, holding the line is critical. If you pinch and the puck gets to that winger, it's a breakaway.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Winger leaking out? Hold the line. Don't pinch into a breakaway against.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on the PP half-wall. The puck is at the point. You see the PK is cheating toward the point shooter. The back-door player on the far side is open. What do you call for?
opts: The cross-ice pass from the point to the far side for the one-timer since the PK shifted away.
A pass to you on the half-wall since you're in a better position to shoot from your.
A shot from the point through traffic since that's the highest percentage play from that instead.
The puck back to the D in the corner to reset and try a new play since this look isn't.
U15 / Bantam.why: When the PK shifts toward the point, the far side opens up. A cross-ice pass for a one-timer exploits that shift perfectly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK shifting to the point? Cross-ice one-timer on the far side. Exploit the shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The opposing team is cycling in your zone. The puck carrier is below the goal line. You're the weak-side D. Where should your eyes be?
opts: On the weak-side player and the slot — the most dangerous pass is coming to your side next.
Only on the puck carrier since you need to see what they do before you can react to.
On your goalie to make sure they're set and tracking the puck through all the traffic.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: The weak-side D must have eyes on the weak-side player and the slot. The cross-ice pass or back-door play is the most dangerous option when the puck is below the goal line.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Weak-side D during a cycle: eyes on the weak-side player and slot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team's forecheck pressure has been working — you're getting turnovers. Entering the zone, a defender backs off and gives you space at the blue line. Do you carry or dump?
opts: Carry the puck in since the defender is backing off and giving you time and space to attack.
Dump it in deep because that's what the forecheck system calls for regardless of what.
Pass it back to your D and regroup since you're not sure what to do with the extra space given.
Stop at the blue line and wait for all five players to get set before you make any instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: When a defender backs off and gives you space, carrying the puck in is the right read. Dumping when you have space wastes a possession advantage.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defender giving you space? Carry it in. Don't dump when you have time.

U15 / Bantam.concept: Puck protection below the goal line
U15 / Bantam.sit: You have the puck below the goal line in the offensive zone. A defender is closing on you. You're not in a position to pass. What do you do?
opts: Use your body to shield the puck and buy time until a teammate gets open for a pass option.
Try to force a centering pass through the defender's legs since that might create a chance.
Skate behind the net at full speed to the other side since speed alone will beat.
Leave the puck against the boards and skate away since there's no play to be made.
U15 / Bantam.why: Below the goal line, puck protection is king. Use your body as a shield, keep the puck on your backhand, and buy time for support to arrive.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Below the goal line with pressure? Shield the puck. Buy time. Wait for support.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In the defensive zone, your winger's check cuts across the ice toward your side. Your check stays put. Should you and your winger switch assignments?
opts: Yes — switch and communicate so each D-man stays in their zone and covers the nearest threat.
No — always stay with your original assignment regardless of where they go on the ice during.
Only switch if the coach specifically told you to switch before the game or during the last timeout.
Let the winger decide since they had the original assignment and it's their responsibility.
U15 / Bantam.why: Switching when opponents cross is smart defensive zone play. The key is communication — both players need to know who has who.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Opponent crosses zones? Switch and communicate. Don't chase across the ice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You notice the opposing team always sends their center up the middle on breakouts. Your team's F2 isn't picking the center up. What's the adjustment?
opts: Have F2 take away the middle lane on the forecheck so the center can't receive the breakout pass.
Have F1 chase the D harder into the corner since that will prevent the breakout pass after that.
Pull all three forwards back to the neutral zone and stop forechecking completely to stay.
Ignore the center and focus on the wingers since the center usually isn't the most dangerous option.
U15 / Bantam.why: If the opposing center is consistently getting the middle breakout pass, F2 needs to take that lane away. Recognizing patterns and adjusting is systems hockey IQ.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Center always getting the middle pass? F2 takes the lane away. Adjust.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is under heavy forecheck pressure in your own zone. You get the puck behind your net. Two forecheckers are closing. Your only option is the glass. Do you use it?
opts: Yes — get the puck off the glass and out of the zone to relieve the immediate pressure safely.
No — always try to make a play with the puck even if that means holding it under heavy pressure.
Yes — but aim for a teammate at the blue line to turn the clear into a breakout opportunity.
No — freeze the puck and take a faceoff instead since that resets the play more effectively.
U15 / Bantam.why: Using the glass to exit the zone under heavy pressure is a smart play. It relieves pressure and gets the puck out safely.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Heavy pressure, no options? Use the glass. Get it out. Live to play another shift.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on the PK in the box. The PP moves the puck to the half-wall and the shooter looks like they're going to shoot. You're the nearest PK player. What do you do?
opts: Get your stick in the shooting lane to block or deflect the shot without fully committing out.
Charge at the shooter to close the distance and try to block the shot with your body.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Drop to both knees and slide toward the shooter to cover as much of the lane as you possibly.
U15 / Bantam.why: Stick in the lane without overcommitting keeps you in the PK structure while disrupting the shot. Charging out leaves a gap in the box.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: stick in the lane. Don't overcommit. Disrupt without leaving a gap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're forechecking and the D is going to the boards to play the puck. You arrive at the same time. How do you win this battle?
opts: Arrive with speed and pin them to the boards — use your body to separate them from the puck first.
Slow down before you arrive and try to poke check the puck away without making any body contact.
Skate past them and go behind the net to wait for the puck to come around the boards to you.
Stop before the boards and wait to see what they do with the puck before engaging in any.
U15 / Bantam.why: Winning the wall means arriving with speed and using your body. Pin them, separate them from the puck, and get possession.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Forechecking to the wall: arrive with speed. Pin. Separate. Win the puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D making a breakout pass. Your winger is open on the wall but skating at full speed. Where do you put the pass?
opts: Tape-to-tape — lead them slightly so the puck arrives in stride without them slowing down at all.
Dump it in and change immediately instead of pushing the transition.
Behind them so they have to turn around and come back for the puck.
Loft it high in the air so it goes over the forechecker's stick and lands near your winger up ahead.
U15 / Bantam.why: A breakout pass to a player in stride must lead them. Tape-to-tape in stride keeps the speed of the transition — a pass to their feet kills the momentum.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakout pass to a player in stride: lead them. Tape-to-tape. Keep the speed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up by one goal with two minutes left. The opposing team is pressing hard. You're the D. How does your gap change?
opts: Slightly tighter gap — force quick decisions and don't give them time or space to create anything.
Much bigger gap — play ultra-conservative and back up to the goal line to make sure nothing gets.
Same gap as always since you shouldn't change your defensive approach based on the game.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: With a lead late, slightly tighter gap forces the other team into quick, low-percentage decisions. Backing up too far invites pressure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Protecting a lead? Slightly tighter gap. Force quick decisions. Don't back up.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Their best player is coming at you 1-on-1. They're known for their speed and deking ability. They've beaten you before with a wide fake and cut back inside. How do you approach?
opts: Take away the inside lane and force them to the outside — accept the lower-percentage shot from the angle over a deke from slot.
Aggressively close your gap and try to force a turnover at the blue line before they can get up to speed and start their moves.
Back up to the goal line and give them space since they're too skilled to challenge one-on-one at any gap in the neutral.
Stand completely still at the top of the circles and wait for them to make the first move before reacting to anything they do.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a skilled deking player, taking away the inside lane and forcing them wide reduces their options. They have to beat you from a bad angle instead of the slot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Skilled deker? Take away inside. Force them wide. Bad angle beats slot deke.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An opposing forward establishes position in front of your net. They're screening your goalie and deflecting pucks. You've tried to box them out but they keep coming back. What's your next approach?
opts: Tie up their stick on every shot attempt and use your body to redirect their momentum away from the net front between whistles only.
Hit them as hard as you can from behind every time they set up in front since that's the only way to permanently remove them.
Give up on the net front and go cover someone else since if they want that position that badly there is nothing you can do to stop.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
U15 / Bantam.why: Net-front defense requires continuous effort. Tying up their stick prevents deflections, and using body position between whistles resets the battle. It's a war of attrition.
U15 / Bantam.tip: They keep coming back to the net front? Keep battling. Tie up stick on shots. Reset between whistles.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up by one with three minutes left. The coach calls for a 1-3-1 neutral zone trap. You're F1. How aggressive should you be on the forecheck?
opts: Controlled — angle and steer but don't overcommit, maintain the trap structure.
Very aggressive — pressure hard to force turnovers since a second goal would end the game and the best defense right now is a.
Improvise instead of following the system.
Ignore the trap call and play your normal game since changing your system with only three minutes left will confuse the team.
U15 / Bantam.why: Late-game trapping F1 is about controlled pressure. Angle and steer — don't overcommit. The trap works when F1 funnels the puck into the structure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Late-game trap: F1 controls the funnel. Angle, steer, don't overcommit.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone with full possession. Both wingers are below the dots cycling. Both D are at the blue line. The opposing team has dropped into a passive box. Should one D jump into the play?
opts: Yes — one D steps down to create a numerical advantage low while the other holds the line as safety in case of a turnover or breakout.
No — both D should always stay at the blue line since leaving the line is too risky regardless of how much possession your team has.
Yes — both D should jump into the play since you have full possession and there's no risk of a breakout when your team controls.
No — the D should pass the puck between each other at the blue line until a shooting lane opens up and then take a long-range.
U15 / Bantam.why: With full possession against a passive box, one D jumping down creates an overload. The key is one stays back as safety. Both going creates transition risk.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Passive box? One D steps down for the overload. Other holds the line.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're taking a defensive zone faceoff on the PK. Your team's faceoff play is to win it back to the D and clear it. You lose the draw cleanly to the PP center. What's the immediate priority?
opts: Sprint to cover the shooting lane and get in front of any shot — the PP will look to shoot immediately after a won O-zone faceoff draw.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Chase the center who won the draw and try to take the puck back from them before they can pass it to one of their power play instead.
Skate to the bench for a change since you lost the faceoff and a fresh penalty killer will be more effective than you right now.
U15 / Bantam.why: After losing a PK faceoff, the PP often looks for an immediate shot. Getting into the shooting lane quickly prevents the most dangerous opportunity.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Lost the PK faceoff? Sprint to the lane. Block the immediate shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: An attacker has beaten you wide on a 1-on-1. They're now inside your zone with a step on you. Your goalie is set. What do you do to recover?
opts: Angle your recovery to take away the pass option while your goalie plays the shot — don't chase directly behind the puck carrier.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
Give up on the play since they've already beaten you and you'll only make things worse by being out of position chasing from behind.
Dive at their feet to try to trip them or knock the puck away since that's the only option left once you've been beaten on the rush.
U15 / Bantam.why: Recovery angle is critical. Don't chase directly behind — angle to take away the pass while your goalie plays the shot. Your goalie can handle the shooter; the pass is the danger.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Beaten wide? Angle your recovery to the pass lane. Goalie has the shot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're backchecking and an opposing forward is receiving a pass in the neutral zone at full speed. You're at an angle where you can deliver a legal body check. The puck is arriving in one second. What do you do?
opts: Time the hit to arrive just as the puck does — a clean check at the moment of reception separates them from the puck and kills the rush dead.
Use your body aggressively rather than positioning smartly.
Skate past them and get ahead to set up your defensive positioning since the hit might not connect and then you'd be completely out of the play.
Wait until they have full possession and have made their first move before deciding whether to hit or play the puck on the defensive.
U15 / Bantam.why: Timing a hit to arrive with the puck is legal and devastating. It kills the rush and sends a message. The key is timing — too early is interference, too late is ineffective.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Hit timed with the puck arrival: legal, devastating, rush-killing. Timing is everything.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're killing a penalty. The opponent's PP runs a 1-3-1 with a one-timer option from the left circle. You've seen this three times. How do you adjust your PK?
opts: Overplay the one-timer side slightly and have the high PK player pressure the passer when the puck goes to the set-up spot on the left side.
Keep the box exactly the same since adjusting to one option will open up other options and the standard PK box is designed to handle.
Switch to a diamond PK formation since the 1-3-1 PP always beats the box and the diamond is designed specifically to counter the 1-3-1 offense.
Improvise instead of following the system.
U15 / Bantam.why: Recognizing the PP's go-to play and overplaying it is elite PK hockey IQ. You don't abandon the box — you adjust within it to take away their best option.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Seen their PP play three times? Overplay it. Adjust within your structure.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a D with the puck in your zone. The forecheck is closing hard from both sides. Your outlets are covered. You have a split second. What creative option do you have?
opts: Use the back of the net as a shield to buy time — skate behind it and wait for the forecheck to commit before choosing your outlet play.
Try to split both forecheckers by skating between them since skilled D should be able to handle the puck under pressure in their own.
Shoot the puck directly at the forechecker and hope it bounces off them and goes to one of your teammates who can break out.
Dump it in and change immediately instead of pushing the transition.
U15 / Bantam.why: The back of the net is a tool. Using it as a shield buys time for outlets to open and forces the forecheck to commit to a side.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Outlets covered? Use the net. Shield the puck. Wait for the forecheck to commit.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The opposing team dumps the puck in on every entry. They're fast and aggressive on the forecheck after the dump. How do you adjust your retrievals as a D?
opts: Communicate with your partner before each dump — one retrieves, one covers the net front. Move the puck quickly before their forecheckers arrive.
Both D go after every dump-in together since having two defensemen on the puck means you'll always win the race to the puck in the corner area.
Ignore the dump-in and let your goalie play the puck every single time since they're better at handling pucks behind the net than the D are at.
Commit aggressively to the puck and close the gap immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a dump-and-chase team, D communication on retrievals is critical. One goes, one covers. Quick puck movement before their speed arrives prevents the forecheck from working.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Dump-and-chase team? One D retrieves, one covers. Move the puck fast.

U15 / Bantam.concept: PK in the last minute of a penalty
U15 / Bantam.sit: There are 15 seconds left in your penalty kill. The PP is set up and looking dangerous. They're cycling the puck looking for the perfect play. What's the PK mindset?
opts: Stay disciplined in the box for 15 more seconds — don't chase, don't gamble, deny the high-danger shot and let the clock run out on the penalty.
Get aggressive and try to steal the puck since with only 15 seconds left you can take a risk to end the penalty kill early with a clear.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Pull one PK player off and play more passively since with only 15 seconds left the PP probably won't score and you can start transitioning to.
U15 / Bantam.why: 15 seconds of discipline ends the PK. Gambling or chasing with that little time left is unnecessary. Stay in structure, deny the dangerous play, and run the clock.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 15 seconds left on the PK? Stay disciplined. Don't chase. Run the clock.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just lost a tough game. The room is quiet. Some players are visibly upset. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Acknowledge the tough loss, point out something specific the team did well, and remind them of the next opportunity to compete and improve as a group.
Give a long speech about everything that went wrong so the team knows exactly what to fix before the next game and doesn't repeat the same.
Stay silent and let the coach handle everything since it's not your job to say anything after a loss the coaching staff's responsibility to.
Blame specific players for the loss so the team knows who was responsible and can hold them accountable for the mistakes that cost the game that instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: Post-loss leadership is about honesty and perspective. Acknowledging the loss, finding a positive, and looking forward gives the team something to build on.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tough loss? Acknowledge it. Find one positive. Point to the next one. That's leadership.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team just got a new coach mid-season who runs a completely different system than you're used to. You're struggling to execute it. What's the right approach?
opts: Commit fully to learning the new system even if it feels uncomfortable — watch video, ask questions, and practice the details every single rep.
Keep playing the old system since it worked and the new coach should adapt to what the team already knows how to do instead of changing.
Only run the new system when the coach is watching and go back to the old one during games since the old system is what the team is.
Refuse to buy into the new system and tell your teammates it won't work since the old coach's system was better and the new one doesn't fit.
U15 / Bantam.why: Buying into a new system is a character test. Even when it's uncomfortable, committing fully — video, questions, practice — is how good players adapt.
U15 / Bantam.tip: New system? Commit fully. Watch video. Ask questions. Discomfort is growth.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck is below the goal line on your right side. The attacker is tight to the post. What's the correct post integration?
opts: RVH — pad stacked against the post, blocker and glove covering high to seal the entire post.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Overslide to the backside expecting a cross-crease pass.
VH with your lead leg up since that gives you more mobility to push across if they go.
U15 / Bantam.why: Below the goal line, RVH is the correct integration. It seals the bottom against the post while keeping blocker and glove covering high.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Below the goal line? RVH. Seal the bottom, cover the top.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The puck is dumped in behind your net. You leave to play it. A forechecker is closing. Your D is on the boards. What do you communicate?
opts: Call the play — 'Leave it!' if you have time, or 'Rim it!' to your D if pressure is too close.
Stay silent and make the play yourself since talking behind the net can distract your defensemen.
Yell 'Help!' to get all your teammates to come back behind the net and assist with the puck.
Don't communicate anything specific and let your D figure out on their own what the right call is.
U15 / Bantam.why: Goalie communication behind the net directs traffic. 'Leave it' tells the D you've got it. 'Rim' tells them you need them to take it. Clear calls prevent confusion.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Behind the net: direct traffic. 'Leave it' or 'Rim.' Be specific.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A point shot is coming and you see a forward in front with their stick on the ice, ready to tip. What do you prepare for?
opts: Expect the deflection — stay low in your stance with your stick on the ice and track the puck through the screen and forward's deflection.
Ignore the forward and focus only on the original shot from the point since the deflection might not happen and you can't prepare.
Stand up taller to see over the forward since a higher stance gives you a better view of the point shot as it comes through the traffic.
Drop into a full butterfly before the shot even arrives to cover the bottom of the net since deflections usually go low and you need to.
U15 / Bantam.why: A forward with their stick on the ice is a deflection setup. Stay low, track through, and expect the direction change. Deflections change angle and elevation.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Stick on the ice in front? Expect the tip. Stay low. Track through.

U15 / Bantam.sit: It's a 2-on-1. Your D is taking away the pass. The puck carrier is on the right side driving to the net. What's your positioning?
opts: Square to the shooter on the right side with good depth — trust your D on the pass and play the shot.
Cheat slightly to the far post since if the pass gets through you need to be ready for the instead.
Stay in the exact center of the net since you don't know if it's a shot or pass until it.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U15 / Bantam.why: When your D takes the pass, you play the shot. Square to the shooter with good depth — trust the D's job. If the pass gets through, that's a breakdown, not your read.
U15 / Bantam.tip: D takes the pass on 2-on-1? You play the shot. Square. Good depth. Trust.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Shooters in your league have started targeting your glove side high consistently. You've given up three glove-side goals in the last two games. How do you adjust?
opts: Work on your glove positioning in practice — start with the glove slightly higher and more visible to shooters to take away that visual cue for targeting.
Switch to catching with your blocker hand since your glove side is clearly a weakness and changing hands will confuse the shooters who have been instead.
Don't change anything since three goals is a small sample size and adjusting your technique based on a few goals will create new problems in other areas.
Tell your D to never let anyone shoot from your glove side since it's their job to take away that angle and any goal scored glove side high is a instead.
U15 / Bantam.why: Adjusting glove positioning is the right response. Starting slightly higher takes away the visual cue shooters are using. Address the weakness in practice.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Getting beaten glove side? Adjust the starting position. Work it in practice.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is losing 1-0 with five minutes left. You've been solid all game. An attacker is coming on a breakaway. Do you change your approach?
opts: Stay patient and play your game — the same technique that's kept you in the game all night will give you the best chance to stop this breakaway.
Be way more aggressive than normal since you need to stop this at all costs and coming out past the circles is the only way to guarantee a.
Play more conservatively and back up deeper into the net since you absolutely cannot give up another goal and depth gives you more reaction.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U15 / Bantam.why: Game state doesn't change breakaway technique. The same patience and fundamentals that kept you in the game give you the best chance on any single save.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Losing and facing a breakaway? Same technique. Trust your game.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You gave up two goals in the second period — both on plays you'd normally save. The team is still in the game. What's your intermission routine?
opts: Reset mentally — review what went wrong briefly, then visualize making saves in the third. Focus on the next period, not the mistakes that happened.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Tell the team it was your fault and apologize repeatedly since your teammates need to know that you know the goals were soft and that you feel bad.
Don't think about hockey at all during the intermission — completely clear your mind and think about other things so you come out fresh.
U15 / Bantam.why: Brief review, then visualization of success. Dwelling causes doubt. Ignoring prevents learning. The balance is quick acknowledgment, then forward focus.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Bad period? Brief review. Visualize saves. Forward focus. Next period is new.

U15 / Bantam.sit: When your team is in the defensive zone with the puck, the first forward should immediately pinch the boards on every play.
U15 / Bantam.why: Pinching should be situational — only when you're sure you'll win the puck.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Pinch smart, not automatic.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Against a forward attacking at full speed, you should try to match their speed skating backwards.
U15 / Bantam.why: Backing up maintains gap and forces their decision.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Control the gap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On the penalty kill, the two forwards should focus on preventing zone entry, not creating counter-attacks.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK success depends on preventing entries and maintaining structure.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK first: prevent zone entry.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A player should only demand accountability from teammates if they're having a great game themselves.
U15 / Bantam.why: Leadership is about effort and attitude, not perfection.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Lead by attitude and effort.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In transition, moving the puck quickly up ice is more important than finding the perfect pass.
U15 / Bantam.why: Quick transitions create odd-man advantages. Speed matters more than perfection.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition speed beats perfection.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You should avoid using your body on a defenseman because you'll be called for a penalty.
U15 / Bantam.why: Using your body properly is legal and encouraged. Penalties come from illegal contact.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Physical play is legal if positioned correctly.

U15 / Bantam.sit: When up 3-1 late, you should play defensively and avoid risks with the puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Protecting a lead means controlled possession and smart puck management.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Protecting lead = smart puck management.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of a controlled breakout in order.
items: Defenseman secures puck behind net
D creates space with lateral movement
Forward support arrives at hash marks
D makes crisp pass to forward in stride
Forward exits cleanly up board or middle
U15 / Bantam.why: Breakout requires D to secure, create space, pass when support ready, then exit cleanly.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakout: secure, move, support, pass, exit.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of penalty kill entry prevention.
items: First forward pressures puck carrier
Second forward angles to boards
Together prevent the zone entry
Transition to short-handed counter if available
U15 / Bantam.why: PK requires engagement, angling, prevention, then counter opportunity.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: engage, angle, prevent, counter.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of setting up a 1-3-1 neutral zone trap in order.
items: F1 engages puck carrier
D establish blue line structure
F2/F3 move to passing lanes
If turnover, transition to offense
U15 / Bantam.why: Trap requires F1 engage first, then D set, then wings block lanes.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-3-1: F1 engages, D set, wings cover lanes.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Put the steps of defending a 1-on-1 situation in order.
items: Read the attacker approach and speed
Maintain gap and body position
Force them to outside if possible
Stay on feet and make the play
U15 / Bantam.why: 1-on-1 requires reading, maintaining gap, forcing issue, then reacting.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-on-1: read, gap, force outside, stay up.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of executing a fast break in transition.
items: First pass breaks first line of defense
Second pass creates odd-man advantage
Final pass sets up scoring opportunity
Take the shot or create best play
U15 / Bantam.why: Fast breaks build in rhythm: break line, create advantage, position, score.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Fast break: break line, advantage, position, finish.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of a proper slot attack sequence.
items: Recognize the open slot area
Move into slot with purpose
Receive pass in sweet spot
Shoot immediately without hesitation
U15 / Bantam.why: Slot play requires reading area, getting in, receiving clean, then shooting fast.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Slot: read, move, receive, shoot.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the communication steps during defensive play.
items: Call out opponent assignments
Confirm coverage with partner
Make callouts as play develops
Reset communication after play
U15 / Bantam.why: Communication starts with clear assignments, confirms coverage, continues, then resets.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Defense talk: assign, confirm, continue, reset.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Order the steps of executing a zone cycle.
items: Enter zone with support arriving
Cycle puck around perimeter
Create rebound opportunity in slot
Maintain possession or create scoring chance
U15 / Bantam.why: Cycling builds time and opportunity: enter, move, create, finish.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Cycle: enter, move, create, finish.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You skate forward to challenge early instead of backing up. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have stayed deeper.
You overcommitted — closing gap early gives speed advantage and scoring chance.
You didn't use your stick effectively.
You angled them wrong.
U15 / Bantam.why: Overcommitting closes gap and removes ability to react.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Maintain gap first. Challenge when sure.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In transition at hash marks with no support, you try to deke through two defenders. What's the mistake?
opts: Deking is never right.
You forced play instead of letting transition develop — support arrives if you buy time.
You should have shot.
You didn't protect puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Forcing plays in transition kills momentum. Support arrives if you buy time.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition: buy time, let support arrive.

U15 / Bantam.sit: On PK, you chase the puck instead of maintaining coverage, leaving your player open. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have predicted the pass.
You left your check open — coverage matters more than chasing puck.
You moved too slowly.
You should have blocked the pass.
U15 / Bantam.why: PK is about structure and coverage. Chasing pucks breaks assignments.
U15 / Bantam.tip: PK: maintain coverage assignment.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You receive pass in slot and hesitate to adjust puck. Goalie sets and saves. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have passed.
You hesitated — in slot with open lane, shoot immediately.
The pass was late.
The goalie got lucky.
U15 / Bantam.why: Slot opportunities are high-percentage only if you shoot fast.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Slot: receive and shoot immediately.

U15 / Bantam.sit: In a 1-3-1 trap, F1 leaves the puck carrier to help F2 block a passing lane. What's the mistake?
opts: F1 should support behind the play.
F1 abandoned their primary responsibility — engaging the puck carrier is the foundation of the trap.
F2 should handle the puck carrier.
The trap setup was wrong.
U15 / Bantam.why: Trap fails if F1 doesn't maintain pressure on puck carrier.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Trap: F1 engagement is foundation.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You see a gap opening but don't call it out to your partner, who gets caught out of position. What's the mistake?
opts: Your partner should have seen it.
You didn't communicate — calling gaps keeps partner informed and alert.
Your partner should have moved.
The play was too fast.
U15 / Bantam.why: Defense communication prevents coverage breakdowns.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Talk constantly on defense.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Against a rushing attacker, you use your body too aggressively and get called for a check from behind. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have let them score.
You overcommitted with your body — positioning and angling work better than aggressive contact.
The ref was wrong.
You should have pushed harder.
U15 / Bantam.why: Effective physical play uses positioning and angle, not aggression.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Positioning beats aggression.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Down 2-1 with 30 seconds left, you throw the puck away trying a risky pass instead of shooting. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have passed.
You were reckless — in desperation, shot > risky pass. You need a goal.
The goalie made a save.
You needed more skill.
U15 / Bantam.why: Late-game deficit requires goal-scoring focus, not playmaking.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Down late: create shots, not chances for others.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Breaking out at hash marks, opposing forward approaches. What next?
opts: Dump it in deep before defender closes.
Cut toward middle and look for support threading through.
Protect puck and continue up board.
Pass back to trailing defenseman.
U15 / Bantam.why: Support arriving through middle creates odd-man advantage.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Transition: use support in middle.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Backing up maintaining gap, attacker cuts toward middle. What next?
opts: Stay in middle to cut pass.
Follow them — stay tight.
Shuffle across staying between them and net.
Keep backing straight — let partner help.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shuffle across (not turn back). Stay gap-conscious.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Shuffle with them. Stay gap-conscious.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You shoot from the point. Goalie makes the save and rebound bounces loose. What next?
opts: Back off — give the goalie space.
Push hard to first rebound and follow up with another shot.
Pass to open teammate.
Let the play develop.
U15 / Bantam.why: Loose rebounds are second chances. Push hard and be ready to shoot again.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Rebound: push hard and be ready.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're on PK and gain possession. You have a 3-on-2 short-handed break. What next?
opts: Conservative pass and reset.
Attack with speed — convert to scoring chance.
Dump it in and retreat.
Wait for teammates to get back.
U15 / Bantam.why: Short-handed breaks are premium opportunities. Attack with speed.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Short-handed break: attack with speed.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Applying a hard forecheck, the D passes it to a forechecker's wing. What next?
opts: Back off the pressure.
Immediately transition to defense — they may break through.
Keep pressing.
Let the wing figure it out.
U15 / Bantam.why: When forecheck backfires, transition quickly to defense.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Forecheck failure = quick transition.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is up 2-1 with 5 minutes left. Opponent scores to tie 2-2. What next?
opts: Play more aggressive to retake lead.
Play controlled and create even-strength chances.
Go into full defensive mode.
Push for overtime win.
U15 / Bantam.why: Tied late, controlled play creates chances without risk of odd-man rush.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tied late: controlled possession.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your defensive partner is beat on their side and the forward is heading to the net. What next?
opts: Go back to check on them.
Be ready to support and cover the assignment.
Let them handle it.
Change immediately.
U15 / Bantam.why: Supporting your partner's mistake prevents the goal. Communication and position matter.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Back up your partner on D.

U15 / Bantam.q: Set up the 1-2-2 forecheck. Drag each chip to their starting spot.
U15 / Bantam.tip: 1-2-2: F1 pressures the puck, F2 and F3 cover the strong-side wall + weak-side outlet, D1 and D2 hold the blue line.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The forecheck is applying heavy pressure on your D behind the net. You're the weak-side winger. Best read?
opts: Come lower and offer a reverse support.
Stay high on your wall.
Skate straight to the bench.
Crash the net.
U15 / Bantam.why: Against a heavy forecheck, wingers sometimes need to come down to give the D a second option — a low reverse outlet.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Heavy pressure? Come low.

U15 / Bantam.sit: A good breakout adjusts based on how the other team forechecks — it's not one fixed pattern.
U15 / Bantam.why: Elite teams read the forecheck and flex. 1-2-2, 2-1-2, trap — each demands a different breakout option.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Read the forecheck, pick your out.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You retrieve a dump-in behind the net. Two forecheckers are coming hard. What's the highest-percentage play?
opts: Rim the puck hard off the boards to the strong-side winger.
Skate it up the middle yourself.
Throw it high through the slot.
Freeze it and take an icing.
U15 / Bantam.why: Rimming is a staple breakout escape — fast, hard, predictable for your winger. Middle-ice carries into heavy forecheck = turnovers.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Under pressure? Rim it.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're in your own end with a questionable pass available through the middle. Right call?
opts: Glass-and-out — don't force it.
Force the middle pass.
Skate through two defenders.
Reverse to your goalie.
U15 / Bantam.why: In your own end, the middle is sacred. If you aren't 100% sure, glass it out and regroup in the neutral zone.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Own end middle = only if certain.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Good puck management means taking fewer low-percentage shots from bad angles and saving possession.
U15 / Bantam.why: Shooting from zero-angle just gives the puck back. Keeping possession = more chances later.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Manage the puck. Don't donate it.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Late in the game, up by one, you gain the offensive zone. A risky cross-ice pass is open. What's the smart play?
opts: Cycle the puck low and kill time.
Force the cross-ice pass.
Shoot from the blue line.
Skate into the defender.
U15 / Bantam.why: Up a goal late = protect possession. Cycle low, eat clock, force them to chase.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Up late? Cycle, don't gamble.

U15 / Bantam.sit: It's your third period, score tied, 40 seconds left in your shift. You get a 50/50 chance to pinch as a D. Right call?
opts: Stay back — your legs are fried and the consequences are too high.
Pinch hard — go for it.
Skate to the bench right now.
Yell for a change.
U15 / Bantam.why: Decision-making is situational. Tired legs + tight game + high consequence = stay. Pinch on clean reads only.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Tired + tight game = safe.

U15 / Bantam.sit: The right decision at 0-0 in the first period is sometimes different from the right decision with a 3-goal lead in the third.
U15 / Bantam.why: Game state matters. What's great in one scenario can be reckless in another. Elite players constantly adjust.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Score + time = context.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Down a goal late, you receive a puck at center with a clean look at a stretch pass through the middle. Right call?
opts: Go for it — the risk is worth the reward.
Chip it deep safely.
Reverse to your D.
Freeze the puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Down late = take calculated risks. Forcing chances is worth it when the alternative is losing. Flip in the lead situation, and it's the opposite.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Down late? Take smart risks.

U15 / Bantam.sit: 3rd period, your team is up by 1, 4 minutes to go. Best line philosophy?
opts: Simple plays, short shifts, get pucks out clean.
Force the extra goal with risky plays.
Hold the puck as long as possible.
Take more shots from bad angles.
U15 / Bantam.why: Late with a lead = short shifts, simple plays, clean exits. Don't give them oxygen.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Lead late = simple + short.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're down by a goal with 90 seconds left. You have the puck in your zone. Best play?
opts: Join the rush — calculated risk is worth it.
Play it safe and cycle.
Glass-and-out.
Freeze the puck.
U15 / Bantam.why: Down late = calculated risk > safe. Join the attack, create numbers, force a chance.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Down late = push, don't coast.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team should play the same way in minute 1 as minute 59 — score + time don't matter.
U15 / Bantam.why: Elite teams adjust constantly. Tight playoff game ≠ 6-1 blowout ≠ early-period feel-out. Read the state.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Score + time = adjust.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You break in alone on the goalie. What's the highest-percentage move?
opts: Deke to your forehand and elevate.
Shoot from the blue line.
Pass back to no one.
Dump it in.
U15 / Bantam.why: Breakaways favor the attacker. Get to your forehand, pick the corner, elevate. Quick eyes on the goalie's pads.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Breakaway = forehand, elevate.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You receive a pass backdoor in the slot. What's the play?
opts: One-timer into the open net.
Stop and stickhandle.
Look for another pass.
Hesitate.
U15 / Bantam.why: Backdoor feeds die when the goalie recovers. One-time it into the open side — speed beats stickhandling here.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Backdoor = one-time it.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Great finishers look at the goalie, not the puck, right before they shoot.
U15 / Bantam.why: Top finishers scan the goalie's position AND what the goalie is giving up. Their hands find the puck by feel.
U15 / Bantam.tip: Finisher's eyes = goalie, not puck.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're a defenseman, and the opposing forward is carrying the puck wide on your weak side along the boards.
U15 / Bantam.q: Which of these reads ALL apply to good gap control here?
opts: Match the attacker's speed so the gap stays the same as they cross the blue line
Open your hips toward the boards so you can pivot if they cut inside
Skate backward in a straight line at full speed regardless of the puck
Keep your stick on the ice in their puck-side passing lane
U15 / Bantam.tip: Gap control is about matching speed AND keeping the inside route closed.
U15 / Bantam.why: Skating full speed backward without reading the puck takes you out of position. The other three are all part of holding a tight, smart gap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your D-partner just retrieved the puck behind your net and is starting a breakout up the strong-side wall.
U15 / Bantam.q: Which of these are YOUR responsibilities as the other defenseman?
opts: Skate to the slot to be a high screen for the goalie
Provide a low support option behind the net or across to the weak side
Read pressure — if F1 forechecks hard, be ready for a reverse
Communicate with a clear call so your partner knows where you are
U15 / Bantam.tip: On a wall breakout you're the safety valve, not the goalie's screen.
U15 / Bantam.why: Going to the slot puts you in the wrong place AND screens your own goalie. Low support, reading pressure, and clear calls are the D-partner's job on a wall breakout.

U15 / Bantam.sit: You're F1 in a 1-2-2 forecheck. The opposing D retrieves the puck behind their net.
U15 / Bantam.q: Which of these are correct F1 reads?
opts: Take an angle that forces the puck to one side of the rink
Charge straight at the puck-carrier from the middle of the ice
Stick on the ice to take away the D-to-D pass
Communicate which side you're forcing to F2 and F3
U15 / Bantam.tip: F1's job is to FORCE a side — never charge straight at the middle.
U15 / Bantam.why: Charging straight at the carrier opens both sides of the ice and lets them go either way. Angling, stick-on-ice, and clear calls let F2 and F3 set the trap.

U15 / Bantam.sit: Your team is defending a 5-on-5 in your own zone. The puck cycles to the half-wall on your side.
U15 / Bantam.q: Which of these are correct reads if you're the strong-side D?
opts: Stay at the net front to protect the slot
Pressure the puck-carrier on the half-wall hard
Stay tight to the carrier — close the time and space without overcommitting
Keep your stick in the passing lane to the slot
U15 / Bantam.tip: Strong-side D pressures the wall — the F (winger) does NOT leave the net front.
U15 / Bantam.why: Charging hard at the wall leaves the slot open. Standing flat-footed at the net front while the wall is uncontested is the strong-side wing's job, not yours. Smart pressure plus a stick in the lane closes the play.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your coach shows video of the opposing team's breakout. Their D always goes behind the net and passes to the winger on the left wall. How do you use this in the game?
opts: Position your forecheck to take away the left wall pass and force the D into an unfamiliar play.
Ignore the scouting since video from a previous game doesn't predict what they'll do.
Tell your teammates about the tendency but don't adjust your forecheck system based on instead.
Watch for it in the game but don't change anything until you see them actually do it multiple.
U18 / Midget.why: Pre-scout information is most valuable when it directly changes your positioning. Taking away their go-to play forces them into uncomfortable decisions.
U18 / Midget.tip: Scouting shows a tendency? Position to take it away. Force them into Plan B.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team is trying to carry the puck through the neutral zone. Your team is in a 1-2-2. You're one of the two forwards at the red line. What's your job?
opts: Clog the middle of the ice and force the puck carrier to the outside where they have less options.
Chase the puck carrier aggressively regardless of where they are since speed solves every.
Back up to your own blue line and let them enter the zone since the neutral zone doesn't instead.
Move to the same side as the other forward at the red line so you can double-team the puck.
U18 / Midget.why: The two forwards in a 1-2-2 clog the middle. Forcing the carrier outside reduces their options — wide entries are lower percentage than middle-lane attacks.
U18 / Midget.tip: 1-2-2 middle forward: clog the center. Force them wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's game day against the first-place team. Your team has lost to them twice this season. As a leader, how do you approach the pre-game room?
opts: Bring calm confidence — acknowledge the challenge but focus on specific things your team does well that can win this game tonight against them.
Give an emotional speech about how important this game is and how everyone needs to play their best game of the season or the season is.
Don't say anything since the coach handles pre-game talks and players should just focus on their own individual preparation without at this level.
Talk about how the other team isn't that good and you should have beaten them both times since overconfidence will motivate your team to play.
U18 / Midget.why: Pre-game leadership is about calm confidence. Acknowledge the challenge, focus on specific strengths, and set a tone of preparation, not panic.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big game? Calm confidence. Specific strengths. Set the tone.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D has the puck in the corner and the forecheck is closing. You're the winger. Where do you need to be?
opts: Up the wall on your side — be the outlet and give the D a clear, safe passing option up the ice.
In the slot area in front of the net since that's always the most dangerous area to receive.
At the far blue line waiting for a stretch pass since that creates the biggest transition.
Behind the D to provide close physical support in case they need help winning the board.
U18 / Midget.why: The winger's wall position is the primary breakout outlet. Getting up the wall fast and being ready for the pass is your most important job on the breakout.
U18 / Midget.tip: D in the corner? Get up the wall. Be the outlet. Fast.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team leads by one with five minutes left. The other team is pressing. What's the team-wide approach?
opts: Smart clears, no turnovers in the neutral zone, and take what the defense gives you without risk.
Turtle completely — dump the puck out every time you touch it and don't try to create any.
Play the exact same way you've played all game since changing your approach shows you're scared.
Get ultra-aggressive and try to score another goal to put the game away with an insurance tally.
U18 / Midget.why: Protecting a lead late means smart hockey — clean exits, no neutral zone turnovers, and taking what's given without unnecessary risk. It's not turtling, it's managing.
U18 / Midget.tip: Lead with five left? Smart clears. No turnovers. Take what they give you.

U18 / Midget.sit: You notice the opposing D keeps pinching aggressively on your breakouts. There's open ice behind them every time they pinch. How do you exploit it?
opts: Use stretch passes behind the pinching D to create breakaway chances for your fastest forwards.
Ignore it since taking advantage of other teams' mistakes is unsportsmanlike and not how you play.
Tell the other team's coach about their D's pinching habit so the game stays fair for both.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing and exploiting defensive mistakes is not unsportsmanlike hockey IQ. Stretch passes behind an over-aggressive D create the highest-danger chances.
U18 / Midget.tip: Their D pinching too much? Stretch behind them. Exploit the mistake.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team is running a neutral zone trap. Your team keeps turning the puck over trying to carry through it. What's the adjustment?
opts: Use D-to-D movement and east-west passes to pull the trap apart before trying to carry through it.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Dump the puck every single time and never try to carry through any trap formation.
Have your best player try to beat the trap alone since they're the only one skilled enough.
U18 / Midget.why: Traps are beaten by east-west movement. D-to-D passes and lateral puck movement pull the trap apart and create lanes.
U18 / Midget.tip: Facing a trap? East-west. D-to-D. Pull it apart. Don't skate into it.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your D is behind the net with the puck. The forecheck has overloaded the strong side — both forecheckers went to the same side. What breakout option does this create?
opts: Reverse the puck to the weak side where the ice is wide open since the forecheck left it vacant.
Pass up the strong side anyway since that's the standard breakout and you should stick to.
Hold the puck behind the net and wait for the forecheck to back off before making any breakout.
Shoot the puck off the glass on the strong side to get it past the forecheckers and out.
U18 / Midget.why: An overloaded forecheck leaves the weak side open. Reversing the puck behind the net exploits their aggressiveness and creates a clean exit.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forecheck overloaded one side? Reverse. The weak side is open.

U18 / Midget.sit: You've taken two penalties this game. The coach benched you for a shift as a message. How do you respond when you go back out?
opts: Play disciplined but don't change your compete level — show the coach you can be physical and smart.
Play the same way regardless of the game score or time.
Play the exact same way that got you the penalties since you were just unlucky with the officials.
Go out hunting big hits to swing momentum back and prove a point to the bench.
U18 / Midget.why: The right response to being benched for penalties is disciplined compete. Physical and smart. Not timid, not reckless — the balance in between.
U18 / Midget.tip: Benched for penalties? Come back disciplined but competitive. Show you learned.

U18 / Midget.sit: You have the puck at the top of the left circle. The goalie is set and square to you. There's traffic in front. What's the best shot selection?
opts: Low shot through traffic — the goalie can't see through the screen and a low shot creates a rebound chance off pads or deflects off traffic in front.
High shot over the traffic since going over the screen eliminates the chance of the puck being blocked by bodies and gives you a clear look at the net.
Hold the shot and try to walk to the middle of the ice for a better angle since the left circle isn't a high enough percentage area to shoot.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
U18 / Midget.why: With traffic and a set goalie, low through traffic creates rebounds and deflections. The screen makes the goalie's job harder. High shots over the screen are often caught.
U18 / Midget.tip: Traffic in front, goalie set? Shoot low through it. Screen + rebound = goals.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has lost four games in a row. Morale is low. Players are pointing fingers. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Refocus the team on process — work ethic, preparation, and executing the details in the next game.
Call out the specific players who have been making the most mistakes so they can be held accountable.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Have a team party to boost morale since the problem is clearly that the team isn't having enough.
U18 / Midget.why: Losing streaks are broken by refocusing on process, not results. Work ethic, preparation, and details. Teams that focus on the next game instead of the last four recover.
U18 / Midget.tip: Losing streak? Process over results. Work ethic. Preparation. Next game.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're entering the offensive zone with possession. The D is backing up and giving you the blue line. What type of entry should you make?
opts: Carry the puck in with speed and possession since the D is conceding the blue line to you.
Dump it in hard to the corner since every zone entry should be a dump to protect against turnovers.
Stop at the blue line and wait for your entire team to catch up before you enter the zone.
