Pass the puck backward to your D and let them carry it in since D entries are more effective.
U18 / Midget.why: When the D gives you the blue line, carry the puck in. A possession entry against a retreating D creates more offense than a dump-in.
U18 / Midget.tip: D backing up? Carry it in. Possession entry. Don't give up the puck for free.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're a forward sprinting up ice on a stretch play. Your D has the puck. The opposing D is flat-footed at the blue line. When should the stretch pass arrive?
opts: Timed so you receive it in stride at the blue line — not too early to be offside, not too late to stop.
As early as possible so you have maximum time with the puck before you reach the defensive zone.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
It doesn't matter when it arrives as long as it's in the general area and you can skate to go get.
U18 / Midget.why: Stretch pass timing is critical. In stride at the blue line maximizes speed and minimizes offside risk. Too early forces offside; too late kills momentum.
U18 / Midget.tip: Stretch pass: in stride at the blue line. Timing is everything.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one goal entering the third period. The other team plays a tight defensive system. What's the overall approach?
opts: Increase offensive zone time through sustained pressure and cycling — force turnovers in their zone to create chances rather than rushing the attack up the ice.
Pull the goalie immediately at the start of the third to get an extra attacker for the full twenty minutes since you need to score and this maximizes your.
Keep playing the same way a lucky bounce since changing your approach when you're down usually makes things worse and creates more turnovers.
Play ultra-aggressive with no regard for defense since you need a goal and the best defense when trailing is a good offense that puts constant pressure on.
U18 / Midget.why: Trailing by one requires sustained offensive zone pressure, not desperation. Cycling, winning puck battles, and creating chances through possession is more effective than rush attacks against a tight system.
U18 / Midget.tip: Down one in the third? Sustained pressure. Cycle. Win battles. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: You've noticed the opposing center always cheats to the right on faceoffs. They win draws to their forehand consistently. You're taking the next draw against them. What do you do?
opts: Counter their forehand by positioning your stick to block the right-side win and use your body to gain inside leverage for the draw at the dot against them.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Let the opposing center win the draw and focus on the puck battle after since faceoffs aren't important enough to spend time strategizing about before.
Ask your winger to line up where the puck usually goes on their forehand win so at least you'll have the puck battle covered if they win it to their preferred.
U18 / Midget.why: Reading an opponent's faceoff tendency and countering it is a competitive advantage. Blocking their forehand win and using body leverage directly addresses their strength.
U18 / Midget.tip: Know their faceoff tendency? Counter it. Block the forehand. Use leverage.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your coach wants to run a 1-3-1 trap in the neutral zone to slow down a fast team. You're the center in the middle of the three. What's your specific role?
opts: Control the middle of the ice — take away the center lane and force everything wide where your D can close on the puck carrier as they approach your blue line.
Chase the puck carrier wherever they go since the center in the 1-3-1 should always be closest to the puck regardless of where it is on the ice at all.
Stand at center ice and don't move since the 1-3-1 is a passive system where everyone stays in their spot and waits for the opponent to make a mistake first.
Drop back to the blue line and play as a third defenseman since the neutral zone isn't where the trap works and the real defense starts at your own blue line.
U18 / Midget.why: The center in a 1-3-1 controls the middle lane. You're the key to the trap — take away the center and funnel everything wide where the structure has advantages.
U18 / Midget.tip: Center in the 1-3-1: own the middle lane. Force everything wide.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team just iced the puck. You can't change lines. The other team sends out their top line for the offensive zone faceoff. What do you focus on?
opts: Win the draw and get a clean zone exit on the first touch since a tired team on an icing can not afford to spend time in the D-zone against a fresh top line.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Don't worry about it since icings happen and whatever happens on the faceoff is fine since you can't control whether you're tired or not on this particular.
Argue with the referee about the icing call to try to get a few extra seconds of rest before the faceoff is taken since every second of rest counts when you're.
U18 / Midget.why: After an icing, the priority is a clean zone exit on the first touch. Your team is tired and can't change — spending time in the D-zone against their fresh top line is dangerous.
U18 / Midget.tip: Icing, tired team: win the draw. Get out clean. First touch matters most.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has a new player who's talented but hasn't bought into the team system. They freelance on the ice and it's creating problems. As a veteran leader, what do you do?
opts: Have a private conversation explaining how the system works and why buying in makes everyone better including them personally — welcome them and show them the culture.
Ignore it since they'll either figure it out or the coach will bench them and it's not your responsibility to integrate new players into the team culture and system.
Don't say anything — let your play speak for itself.
Tell the coach to bench them until they agree to play within the system since that's the most effective way to force compliance from talented players who won't.
U18 / Midget.why: Integrating new players through private conversation is leadership. Welcoming them, explaining the culture, and showing why the system helps everyone — including them — builds buy-in.
U18 / Midget.tip: New player not buying in? Private conversation. Welcome them. Show the 'why.'

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in a tight game. The coach calls a timeout after a goal against. You have 30 seconds. What's the most productive way to use this time as a player?
opts: Listen to the coach, catch your breath, and focus on one specific adjustment for the next shift to execute — don't try to process everything at once or change too many things.
Drink water and zone out since the timeout is for the coach to talk and you just need physical rest not tactical information during these short breaks in the game flow.
Talk over the coach to share what you've been seeing on the ice since your perspective is more important than theirs and the team needs to hear your observations.
Use the time to argue about the goal that was just scored and assign blame for the breakdown since accountability during timeouts prevents the same mistake from happening.
U18 / Midget.why: Timeout productivity: listen, breathe, focus on one adjustment. Trying to fix everything at once during a 30-second break creates confusion. One focus, one fix.
U18 / Midget.tip: Timeout: listen, breathe, one focus. Don't overload. One adjustment at a time.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is down by one with 90 seconds left. You have a faceoff in the offensive zone. The coach signals to pull the goalie for the extra attacker. What's the team's approach?
opts: Set up the 6-on-5 with the extra player at the net front or point — use the overload to create a shot through traffic or a cross-ice play with the numerical advantage.
Panic and shoot the puck every time you touch it since you need a goal and shot volume is the only thing that matters when the goalie is pulled and time is.
Have the extra player roam around the zone without a specific assignment since having an extra body creates natural chaos that the defending team can not handle.
Don't pull the goalie since an empty-net goal against ends the game and the risk outweighs the reward of having six attackers when there's still 90 seconds left to.
U18 / Midget.why: 6-on-5 requires structure. The extra player takes a specific position — net front or point — to create the overload. Random movement wastes the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: 6-on-5: extra player takes a specific spot. Net front or point. Structure wins.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your teammate turns the puck over in the neutral zone. The other team has a 3-on-2 rush coming at your defense. You're the forward closest to the play. What's your job?
opts: Backcheck hard and take away the trailing player — reduce the 3-on-2 to a 2-on-2 by picking up the third attacker coming late with speed from behind the play.
Chase the puck carrier since taking away the puck is the most direct way to stop the rush and your speed should be enough to catch them from behind on this play.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Stay in the neutral zone in case the rush fails and the puck comes back since being in position for a counter-attack is more valuable than backchecking.
U18 / Midget.why: Backchecking to take away the trailer turns a 3-on-2 into a 2-on-2. Your D handle two — you eliminate the third. That's the highest-value backcheck play.
U18 / Midget.tip: 3-on-2 against? Backcheck and take the trailer. Turn it into 2-on-2.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're F1 on the backcheck. Your team just turned the puck over. The opposing D has the puck and their center is open in the middle. What's your assignment?
opts: Take the center away — get in the middle lane and prevent the pass to the most dangerous player in their breakout since the center pass starts their transition cleanly.
Chase the D who has the puck since taking the puck away directly is always better than covering a passing lane and your speed should be enough to catch them from here.
Skate to the far winger's side since they're the most open player on the ice and a long pass to the winger creates a bigger offensive threat than the center in the middle.
Go to the bench for a change since backchecking F1 should be a fresh player and you've been on the ice too long to be effective in a footrace with the opposing center.
U18 / Midget.why: F1 backcheck takes away the center. The middle lane is the most dangerous breakout pass — eliminating it forces the D to use the boards, which is slower and easier to defend.
U18 / Midget.tip: F1 backcheck: take the center away. Eliminate the middle lane.

U18 / Midget.sit: In the defensive zone, the opposing team passes the puck from the corner to the point. Your winger was covering the point but got caught low. You're the center. What do you do?
opts: Rotate up to cover the point player since your winger can't get there — communicate so the coverage gap is filled before the point player can shoot or pass it down to the slot.
Stay in the slot since that's always the center's responsibility and if the winger got caught low that's their mistake to fix and not your responsibility to cover for them.
Follow the puck to the corner since the puck is what scores goals and being near the puck is always the right decision regardless of where your coverage assignment is.
Don't rotate and let the point player have the shot since shots from the point are low percentage and your goalie can handle a point shot without any defensive help from you.
U18 / Midget.why: Defensive coverage is fluid. When a winger gets caught, the center rotates to fill the gap. Communication prevents confusion. Rigid assignments without rotation create open shooters.
U18 / Midget.tip: Winger caught low? Rotate to the point. Fill the gap. Communicate.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has a PP offensive zone faceoff with a set play designed: draw back to the point for a one-timer. The PK knows this play — they've seen it. What adjustment do you make to the play?
opts: Run the same draw but add a secondary option — if the PK overplays the point shot, have the center fake the draw-back and go directly to the net with the puck instead.
Run the exact same play without any adjustment since execution beats scouting and if you win the draw cleanly the PK can't stop a clean point shot regardless of what.
Abandon the set play entirely and just try to win the draw forward aggressively for a quick shot since set plays don't work when the other team knows what you're instead.
Skip the faceoff entirely — don't even try to win it and focus all your energy on defending the PK's counter-attack since their scouting has made your PP faceoff.
U18 / Midget.why: Adding a secondary option to a scouted play keeps the PK guessing. If they overplay the point shot, the center going direct to the net exploits their adjustment.
U18 / Midget.tip: PK knows your play? Add a secondary option. Keep them guessing.

U18 / Midget.sit: There are 10 seconds left in the second period. Your team has the puck in the neutral zone. What's the smart play?
opts: Get the puck deep into the offensive zone and create a scoring chance — a late-period goal changes momentum going into the intermission break between periods.
Hold the puck at center ice and let the clock run out since there isn't enough time to create anything meaningful and risking a turnover at the buzzer could.
Dump the puck in and go for a change since the period is essentially over and there's no point in trying to create a scoring chance with only 10 seconds.
Shoot the puck on net from center ice since any shot from anywhere is a good shot when time is running out and you might score a lucky goal from long distance at.
U18 / Midget.why: 10 seconds is enough to get a shot. Getting the puck deep and creating a late-period chance can shift momentum. Wasting the opportunity is a missed chance.
U18 / Midget.tip: 10 seconds left with possession? Get it deep. Create a chance. Momentum matters.

U18 / Midget.concept: Supporting the rush through the NZ
U18 / Midget.sit: Your D is carrying the puck through the neutral zone. You're a forward and there's open ice on the right side. But another forward is already filling that lane. Where do you go?
opts: Fill the middle or left lane — don't double up in the same lane as your teammate and give the rush three distinct options across the ice to attack with maximum width and numbers.
Go to the right side anyway since being close to the puck carrier is always better than being in a different lane and double coverage on one side creates an overload the D.
Stop skating and let the rush develop without you since three forwards in the rush is too many and one forward should always stay back as a safety valve in case of a after that.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
U18 / Midget.why: Rush support requires lane discipline. Three distinct lanes (left, middle, right) give the puck carrier maximum options. Doubling up in a lane wastes a player.
U18 / Midget.tip: Teammate already in a lane? Take a different one. Three lanes, three options.

U18 / Midget.concept: Penalty kill strategy late in game
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one with four minutes left and takes a penalty. The opposing PP is strong. What's the PK strategy?
opts: Ultra-disciplined box — collapse to the net, block shots, and clear every puck. Deny the high-danger shots and force them to score from the perimeter if they can at all.
Aggressive PK — pressure the puck carrier at every opportunity since taking the puck away ends the power play instantly and that's better than sitting back and.
Play a normal PK without any adjustment for the game situation since your PK should be the same regardless of the score or the time remaining on the clock in the game.
Send a player after the PP puck carrier every time to try to force a shorthanded chance since the best PK is one that scores a goal and that would seal the game.
U18 / Midget.why: Late-game PK with a lead: ultra-disciplined. Collapse, block, clear. The goal is to survive the two minutes — not create offense. Force them to score from low-percentage areas.
U18 / Midget.tip: Leading late, PK? Ultra-disciplined. Collapse. Block. Clear. Survive the two minutes.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your PP has been in the zone for a full minute. The PK is tired but still in structure. You have the puck at the point. The PK is sagging to block shots. What's the next-level read?
opts: Walk the puck lower to change the shooting angle and draw the PK out of their collapsing position, creating a lane for a pass to the slot or a higher-percentage shot from a new spot.
Play defensively and focus on preventing the scoring chance.
Pass the puck back and restart the PP setup since the current setup isn't working and cycling back will give the PK a chance to reset and you can try a completely new approach this.
Hold the puck at the point for as long as possible since the PK is tired and if you just wait long enough they'll eventually break down and give you a wide open look at the net.
U18 / Midget.why: Walking the puck lower changes the geometry. A sagging PK is beaten by changing the angle — it draws them out and creates passing lanes that didn't exist from the point.
U18 / Midget.tip: PK sagging at the point? Walk lower. Change the angle. Draw them out.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your forward has the puck in the neutral zone but is getting pressured. You're the D behind them. They're about to lose the puck. What do you do?
opts: Get close enough to be an outlet for a drop pass — give them an escape option before the pressure forces a turnover in a dangerous area of the neutral zone on the play.
Stay back at your own blue line since if they lose the puck you need to be in defensive position already and moving forward would leave the back end completely.
Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Pinch aggressively to create a 2-on-1 up ice and ask your partner to cover behind you.
U18 / Midget.why: Being an outlet for a drop pass when your forward is pressured prevents the turnover. Close support gives them an escape option in a dangerous area.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forward under pressure in NZ? Get close. Be the drop-pass outlet.

U18 / Midget.sit: The other team has been forechecking aggressively for the last three shifts. Your team has been pinned in the D-zone. You finally get the puck. What's the priority?
opts: Make a quick, smart pass to exit the zone — don't try to be a hero with the puck since one clean exit can change the momentum of the entire game for your team.
Hold the puck and try to deke through the forecheck since after three shifts of pressure your team needs a highlight play to change the momentum and fire up the bench.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
Pass the puck back to the goalie and have them freeze it for a faceoff every single time since faceoffs are the only reliable way to reset when you're under after that.
U18 / Midget.why: After sustained pressure, one clean exit changes everything. Quick, smart pass — don't try to be a hero. Clean exits relieve pressure and shift momentum.
U18 / Midget.tip: Pinned for three shifts? One clean exit changes everything. Quick, smart pass.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in 3-on-3 overtime. You have possession. There's a ton of open ice. What's the strategic approach?
opts: Control the puck and wait for the right moment to attack — don't force anything since a turnover in 3-on-3 creates a high-danger chance the other way with open ice everywhere.
Attack immediately every time you get the puck since 3-on-3 is about scoring and hesitation gives the defense time to set up and take away the open ice that overtime creates.
Pass the puck between your three players at center ice without attacking since the other team will eventually make a mistake and give you a perfect 2-on-0 or breakaway.
Dump the puck in every time and forecheck since dump-and-chase works in 3-on-3 just like it works in 5-on-5 and it's the safest way to generate offensive zone time in after that.
U18 / Midget.why: 3-on-3 is about controlled aggression. Control the puck, read the play, and attack when the opening appears. Forcing the play creates turnovers — and turnovers in OT are breakaways.
U18 / Midget.tip: 3-on-3 OT: controlled aggression. Wait for the moment. Don't force it.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing coach calls a timeout. When play resumes, you notice they've changed their forecheck from a 1-2-2 to a more aggressive 2-1-2. What do you do with this recognition?
opts: Adjust your breakout immediately — use the weak side and quick D-to-D passes since a 2-1-2 overloads one side and leaves the other side more open for a clean breakout exit.
Ignore it since trying to identify and adjust to mid-game tactical changes is overthinking and you should just run your standard breakout regardless of what they're.
Panic and call your own timeout since the opponent has changed tactics and your team needs time to discuss and plan a completely new strategy to counter their aggressive.
Slow the game down and hold the puck in your own zone to waste time and prevent the new forecheck from being effective since if you don't have the puck they can't.
U18 / Midget.why: Recognizing a mid-game tactical change and adjusting immediately is elite hockey IQ. A 2-1-2 overloads one side — the weak side is the outlet.
U18 / Midget.tip: They changed their forecheck after a timeout? Recognize it. Adjust. Use the weak side.

U18 / Midget.concept: NZ faceoff with game on the line
U18 / Midget.sit: Neutral zone faceoff with 10 seconds left. Game is tied. Your coach wants you to win the draw and get a shot. The opposing center is one of the best faceoff players in the league. What's your approach?
opts: Study their stance and hand position — adjust your technique to counter their strength. Have your wingers ready to jump on a loose puck if the draw isn't won cleanly by either side.
Take the draw the same way you always do since adjusting your technique at the last second is more likely to cause you to lose the draw than to win it against a strong faceoff.
Let them win the draw and focus on defense since with 10 seconds left it's better to play for overtime than to risk a turnover that gives them a chance to win it in regulation.
Cheat on the draw and try to get the puck before it hits the ice since the referee probably won't kick you out with 10 seconds left and the advantage of getting the puck first.
U18 / Midget.why: Big faceoffs require preparation and support. Study the opposing center, counter their technique, and have your wingers ready for a loose puck. It's not just the center's battle.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big faceoff: study them. Counter their technique. Wingers ready for the loose puck.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's the championship game. Pre-game nerves are high. Some teammates look tight. As a leader, what do you do?
opts: Keep the room loose but focused — remind the team to play their game, trust the preparation, and enjoy the moment since this is what you've worked for the entire season.
Give an intense, emotional speech about how this is the most important game of everyone's life and anyone who doesn't perform at their best will regret it for the rest.
Don't say anything since every player handles pressure differently and any pre-game talk might make the nervous players feel worse and the confident players feel.
Tell everyone to pretend it's a regular game since acknowledging the significance of the championship will add to the pressure and the team should treat it like any.
U18 / Midget.why: Championship leadership: loose but focused. Trust the preparation, play your game, enjoy the moment. Over-intensity creates tightness. Dismissing the moment loses its power.
U18 / Midget.tip: Championship game? Loose but focused. Trust preparation. Enjoy the moment.

U18 / Midget.concept: Managing emotions after a bad hit
U18 / Midget.sit: An opponent delivers a questionable hit on your linemate. No penalty is called. Your linemate is shaken up. The bench is furious. What do you do on the next shift?
opts: Play hard and physical but within the rules — channel the emotion into compete level, not retaliation, since a penalty hurts your team more than it helps your linemate.
Go after the player who delivered the hit and fight them since your teammate needs to know you have their back and physical retaliation is the only language some players.
Take a run at the nearest opposing player to send a message to the other team that they can't get away with hits like that without consequences from your team in the next shift.
Ignore it completely and play passively since getting emotional after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants and the best response is to pretend nothing happened at all.
U18 / Midget.why: Channeling emotion into compete, not retaliation, is elite emotional management. A penalty after a bad hit is exactly what the other team wants. Play harder, not dirtier.
U18 / Midget.tip: Bad hit on a teammate? Channel the emotion into compete. Don't give them the penalty.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your PK just killed a big penalty and your bench is energized. You're the first player on the ice after the penalty expires. What do you do with the momentum?
opts: Channel the energy into a strong, aggressive shift — the PK momentum carry-over often leads to scoring chances if the team capitalizes on the emotional shift immediately.
Calm down and play conservatively since the PK was tiring and the team needs to recover their energy before pushing the pace again so you should ease into the next.
Call a timeout to celebrate the kill and let the momentum sink in since stopping play after a big PK allows the team to savor the moment and build even more emotional.
Ignore the momentum since hockey is played in individual shifts and what happened on the PK has no bearing on what happens in the next five-on-five shift for your.
U18 / Midget.why: PK momentum is real. The first shift after a big kill is often the best chance to capitalize — the team is energized and the opponent is frustrated their PP failed.
U18 / Midget.tip: Big PK? First shift after: channel the energy. Momentum is real. Capitalize.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're a D with the puck behind your net. The forecheck is closing hard from both sides. You have maybe one second. Your outlets are: winger on the wall (covered with pressure coming), center at the hashmarks (partially open), or glass. What do you choose?
opts: Quick pass to the center at the hashmarks since it's partially open and the center can handle some pressure — it starts the breakout while the winger option risks a board turnover.
Pass to the winger on the wall even though they're covered since at U18 your winger should be skilled enough to handle a pass under pressure and fight through the coverage easily.
Use the glass every time you're under pressure since it's the safest option and you should never attempt a pass when the forecheck is closing hard from both sides during the game.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
U18 / Midget.why: The partially open center at the hashmarks is the best read. It starts the breakout while the winger option risks a board turnover. The glass is last resort. Quick decision, smart pass.
U18 / Midget.tip: One second, outlets limited? Read the best option. Center partially open = go there.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is in the offensive zone for a faceoff late in the game while tied. The coach gives you a specific play. What's the difference between an O-zone and D-zone faceoff mentality?
opts: O-zone: aggressive, go for the scoring chance. D-zone: protect first, get the puck out clean. The zone changes the risk-reward calculation of every decision after the draw.
There's no difference — faceoffs should be approached the same way regardless of which zone they're in since the technique for winning the draw is the same everywhere.
Play more conservatively and take fewer risks.
The zone doesn't matter at all since faceoffs are 50-50 by nature and you can't control which way the puck goes after it's dropped so preparing specific plays is mostly.
U18 / Midget.why: Zone changes everything. O-zone faceoffs are about creating chances — aggressive. D-zone faceoffs are about getting the puck out — protective. The risk-reward shifts with location.
U18 / Midget.tip: O-zone: aggressive, create chances. D-zone: protect, get it out. Zone changes everything.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're on the PK and the PP is cycling. The puck goes below the goal line to the player behind the net. Your PK has been passive. Should you pressure behind the net?
opts: Yes — controlled pressure behind the net disrupts the cycle setup and can force a turnover before the PP can get organized in the dangerous areas of the offensive zone above the dots.
No — stay in the box and let the goalie handle anything from behind the net since leaving the box creates gaps that the PP will exploit with quick passes to open players in the slot.
Yes — send two PK players behind the net aggressively to trap the puck carrier and force a turnover since a shorthanded 2-on-1 behind the net will overwhelm the PP puck carrier.
No — the PK should never pressure above or below the goal line since the box formation is designed to work without any players leaving their assigned positions at any point.
U18 / Midget.why: Controlled pressure behind the net disrupts the cycle. The key word is controlled — one player pressures, the box adjusts. You're not trapping, you're disrupting.
U18 / Midget.tip: Puck behind the net on PP? Controlled pressure. Disrupt, don't trap.

U18 / Midget.concept: NZ play with the lead in the third
U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one in the third. The other team is pressing. Your neutral zone play has been loose — they're getting clean entries. What do you tighten up?
opts: Clog the middle lane, backcheck harder to the puck side, and force everything wide — clean entries are coming through the middle so take it away systematically with your positioning.
Play the same NZ system since you're winning and changing anything in the third period shows the other team you're afraid and gives them confidence that their pressure is.
Open up the NZ and let them enter since trying to prevent entries is futile when a team is pressing and you should focus all your defensive energy on the zone defense after.
Have all five players back behind the blue line before the opposing team reaches the neutral zone so there's no chance of a clean entry through the NZ since depth is more.
U18 / Midget.why: Clean entries through the middle mean the NZ is too loose. Clogging the middle and forcing wide entries reduces the quality of their zone possessions.
U18 / Midget.tip: Clean entries through the middle? Clog it. Force wide. Reduce quality.

U18 / Midget.sit: The season is over. Your team didn't make it as far as you hoped. As a leader, what's the most important message in the final team meeting?
opts: Acknowledge the disappointment, celebrate what the team accomplished and grew through, and challenge everyone to use the offseason to come back better for next year together.
Focus entirely on what went wrong so the team understands their failures and doesn't repeat the same mistakes next year since accountability after a disappointing season is.
Don't say anything emotional since the season is over and there's nothing anyone can do about it now — everyone should just go home and move on to whatever comes next in their lives.
Blame the coaching staff for the team's failures since the players did their best and the results were a reflection of poor coaching decisions and strategy throughout the.
U18 / Midget.why: End-of-season leadership: acknowledge, celebrate, challenge. The balance between processing disappointment and finding forward momentum defines how a team handles adversity.
U18 / Midget.tip: Season over, fell short? Acknowledge, celebrate, challenge. That's how leaders close a season.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team makes the playoffs. The games are immediately more physical and structured than the regular season. What adjustments do you make to your game?
opts: Protect the puck more, finish every check, and be disciplined with penalties — playoff hockey punishes mistakes and rewards structure and compete level above all else in every shift.
Play the exact same way you played in the regular season since changing your style for the playoffs means your regular season preparation was flawed and you can not change now at.
Play the same way regardless of the game score or time.
Focus entirely on individual stats since playoff performance determines your future and personal numbers are what scouts and coaches remember when they evaluate players after.
U18 / Midget.why: Playoff hockey rewards discipline, physicality, and puck protection. Mistakes are amplified. Adjusting your game to protect the puck and finish checks is the playoff formula.
U18 / Midget.tip: Playoffs? Protect the puck. Finish checks. Discipline. Mistakes are amplified.

U18 / Midget.concept: D reading the forecheck in real time
U18 / Midget.sit: You're a D retrieving a dump-in. As you pick up the puck, you scan and see: F1 closing hard from the right, F2 drifting to the middle, F3 high in the NZ. Your winger is on the left wall. What do you do?
opts: Quick pass to the left winger on the wall — F1 is coming from the right so the left side is the safe outlet. The scan told you everything you needed in one second to make the play.
Hold the puck and wait to see what happens since making decisions in one second is too fast and you need more time to process all the information before committing to.
Carry the puck up the right side directly at F1 since the best way to beat a forecheck is to attack it head-on and your puck skills should be good enough to beat one in this case.
Shoot the puck off the glass and out of the zone since any time you're under forecheck pressure the safest play is to clear the zone and let the team reset on the next possession.
U18 / Midget.why: One-second scans dictate breakout decisions. F1 from the right means the left is open. Quick processing and execution is the difference between a clean breakout and a turnover.
U18 / Midget.tip: Scan: one second. F1 right = outlet left. Quick process, quick execute.

U18 / Midget.sit: A shooter is approaching from the right wing on an angle. You're set at the top of the crease. How do you maximize your coverage?
opts: Square your body to the puck and adjust your depth to cut the angle — telescope out slightly to reduce the visible net from the shooter's perspective.
Stay deep in the net to give yourself maximum reaction time since advanced shooters have such quick releases that depth is more valuable than.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Lean toward the short side since most shooters go short side from that angle and cheating there gives you the best chance of making the save on.
U18 / Midget.why: Squaring to the puck and telescoping out slightly maximizes coverage while maintaining reaction space. The goal is to minimize visible net from the shooter's angle.
U18 / Midget.tip: Angle play: square to the puck. Telescope out. Minimize visible net.

U18 / Midget.sit: You make a save and the puck drops in front of you. There are three players from each team battling in the crease. You can see the puck. What's your priority?
opts: Cover the puck immediately if possible — in heavy traffic, freezing the puck eliminates the chaos and gives your team a defensive zone faceoff reset to clear the zone.
Try to push the puck to a teammate since keeping the play alive is better than a faceoff and your team might be able to transition to offense if you make a clean.
Leave the puck and let the players battle for it since getting involved in the scrum as a goalie is dangerous and you might get injured or accidentally push it into your.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
U18 / Midget.why: In heavy traffic, covering the puck is the priority. It eliminates chaos, prevents second chances, and gives your team a reset. Trying to play it in traffic risks an own goal.
U18 / Midget.tip: Traffic in front, puck visible? Cover it. Eliminate the chaos. Reset.

U18 / Midget.concept: Reading the shooter on a breakaway
U18 / Midget.sit: A breakaway is coming. The shooter is approaching at moderate speed — they're not rushing. They're a known deker. At what point do you commit to your save?
opts: Wait as long as possible — let them show their hand first, then react. Committing early against a deker gives them the advantage since they can adjust to your commitment easily.
Commit early and aggressively since the best way to stop a deker is to take away as much net as possible before they start their move and force them to go around you.
Drop to butterfly immediately when they cross the blue line since most breakaway goals go low and covering the bottom of the net early gives you the best chance against.
Poke check from the top of the circles since stopping the breakaway before they get a shot is always better than trying to make a save and the poke check eliminates.
U18 / Midget.why: Against a known deker, patience is your weapon. Let them show their move, then react. Committing early gives them the read and the advantage.
U18 / Midget.tip: Known deker? Be patient. Let them show you. Then commit. Patience wins.

U18 / Midget.concept: Playing against screens at the U18 level
U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing team consistently sets screens on every point shot. You've been getting beat through screens all game. What adjustment do you make?
opts: Be more active with your positioning — move laterally to find sightlines around screens.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Tell your D to clear every screen before the shot comes since it's their job to keep the front of the net clean and any goal scored through a screen is a defensive breakdown.
Drop deeper in the net to give yourself more time to react to shots through screens since depth is the best counter to screened shots and being further back gives you more.
U18 / Midget.why: Active positioning around screens is the adjustment. Move laterally to find sightlines, communicate for clears, and track the puck through gaps. Passive goalies get beaten by screens.
U18 / Midget.tip: Getting beat through screens? Be active. Move to find sightlines. Communicate for clears.

U18 / Midget.concept: Handling the wraparound from both sides
U18 / Midget.sit: An attacker is behind the net and you don't know which side they'll attack. They've used both sides effectively. How do you position yourself?
opts: Stay centered and patient — read their body language and commit to the side they choose at the last possible moment. Moving too early gives them the other side of the net for free.
Pick a side and commit early since being aggressive on one side gives you the best chance of making the save and if they go the other way it was just a good play by them.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Drop into a full butterfly with your pads flat to the crease to block any short-side wraparound.
U18 / Midget.why: Patience and centering against a dual-threat wraparound forces the attacker to commit first. Moving early gives up the other side. Read their body language and react.
U18 / Midget.tip: Wraparound, both sides dangerous? Stay centered. Be patient. Let them commit first.

U18 / Midget.sit: An attacker receives a pass in the slot and is coming at you with a deke move. They're known for a forehand-to-backhand roof deke. You're at the top of the crease. What's your approach?
opts: Stay patient on your feet as long as possible — respect their move but don't bail early. When they commit to the forehand, track the puck and push across if they go backhand side.
Drop to butterfly immediately since most slot dekes require the puck to go low at some point and being in butterfly covers the bottom of the net where the majority of deke goals.
Poke check aggressively since taking the puck off their stick before they can start the deke is the only reliable way to stop a skilled deker in the slot at the U18 level.
Back up deeper into the net to give yourself more reaction time since the closer you are to the goal line the more time you have to react to their deke move and make the save on.
U18 / Midget.why: Patience on your feet against a slot deke forces the attacker to commit first. Bailing into butterfly early gives them the top of the net. Stay up, track the puck, then react.
U18 / Midget.tip: Slot deke? Stay on your feet. Be patient. Let them commit. Then react.

U18 / Midget.sit: The opposing PP runs a set play: point shot with a forward at the crease specifically positioned to tip the puck. You know it's coming. How do you prepare?
opts: Get compact and low — when the tip comes, the puck changes direction unpredictably. Being compact maximizes coverage for any angle the tip might create. Keep your stick on the ice firmly.
Focus only on the point shot and ignore the tipper since you can't predict where a tip will go and the best strategy is to play the original shot and hope the tip goes wide.
Stay centered and react — don't anticipate the play.
Move to one side of the net to avoid the tipper entirely since getting a clear view of the shot is worth leaving some net exposed and you'll make up for it with better reaction time.
U18 / Midget.why: Against a known tip play: compact, low, stick on ice. Tips are unpredictable — being compact maximizes coverage for whatever angle the deflection creates.
U18 / Midget.tip: Known tip play? Get compact. Low. Stick on ice. Cover maximum net.

U18 / Midget.sit: When down by one goal with two minutes left, you should take more risks with the puck and try more aggressive plays.
U18 / Midget.why: Risk-reward changes when chasing a goal. Aggressive play creates chances.
U18 / Midget.tip: Down late = controlled aggression.

U18 / Midget.sit: A defenseman should rarely join a rush because it leaves the zone vulnerable.
U18 / Midget.why: Defensemen can join rushes when there's clear advantage and partner has back coverage.
U18 / Midget.tip: Join rush when you have advantage.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the steps of an attack-mode breakout against aggressive forecheck.
items: Read forecheck pressure and identify weak side
Quick lateral pass to weak side winger
Winger accelerates up board with space
Forward support fills middle for transition
U18 / Midget.why: Attack breakout exploits forecheck by reading pressure and hitting weak side early.
U18 / Midget.tip: Attack breakout: read, weak-side pass, accelerate, middle support.

U18 / Midget.sit: Up 2-0 in first period, you get a 2-on-1 but pass instead of shooting. Pass is intercepted. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have shot immediately.
You didn't account for score — up 2-0, you take percentage plays.
The pass was bad.
You should have dumped it.
U18 / Midget.why: Score awareness changes decision-making. Up 2-0, take percentage plays.
U18 / Midget.tip: Score changes strategy.

U18 / Midget.sit: In neutral zone trap, opponent breaks through first line. What next?
opts: Full retreat to defensive zone.
Transition to aggressive forecheck.
Regroup at blue line and reset trap.
Let them in cleanly.
U18 / Midget.why: If trap breaks, reset at blue line and prepare to defend or trap again.
U18 / Midget.tip: Trap breaks = reset and defend.

U18 / Midget.sit: In a playoff game tied in overtime, taking a high-risk pass through the middle to create a chance is justified if it could lead to a goal.
U18 / Midget.why: In overtime playoff, risk-reward favors creating scoring chances. One mistake ends the game, but so does playing safe.
U18 / Midget.tip: OT hockey: controlled risk for chances.

U18 / Midget.sit: Only the team captain should hold teammates accountable because it's a position of authority.
U18 / Midget.why: Any player can hold others accountable through their actions, words, and attitude. Leadership is earned through effort, not title.
U18 / Midget.tip: Leadership is action, not title.

U18 / Midget.sit: Using a stretch pass in neutral zone is very risky and should be avoided in favor of shorter, safer options.
U18 / Midget.why: Stretch passes, when executed properly with support, are high-percentage transition tools. Risk depends on read and execution.
U18 / Midget.tip: Stretch pass: read, execute, support.

U18 / Midget.sit: When up 4-2 with 5 minutes left, you should focus on not making mistakes rather than creating new chances.
U18 / Midget.why: Late lead protection is about controlled possession and smart decisions, not offensive creation.
U18 / Midget.tip: Late lead: control possession.

U18 / Midget.sit: In the modern game, defensemen should pass the puck immediately rather than handle it because the pressure is too intense.
U18 / Midget.why: Skilled D create advantages by handling the puck and changing angles. This is a critical modern skill at high levels.
U18 / Midget.tip: Modern D: handle, create, pass.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the steps of deciding how aggressive to forecheck based on game situation.
items: Assess game score and time remaining
Determine risk-reward of aggressive forecheck
Adjust pressure intensity accordingly
Be ready to transition if forecheck fails
U18 / Midget.why: Forecheck intensity depends on game situation. Up 3-1? Conservative. Down 0-1? Aggressive.
U18 / Midget.tip: Forecheck: situational intensity.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the priorities for managing the final 2 minutes of a game up by 1.
items: Control the puck in your zone
Make crisp passes to clear zone
Avoid giving opponent scoring chances
Run time off clock with possession
U18 / Midget.why: Final minutes require possession control and smart clearing to run time.
U18 / Midget.tip: Final minutes: possession, clear, run time.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the steps of reading a neutral zone transition situation.
items: Identify numerical advantage or disadvantage
Read defenseman positioning and depth
Decide entry strategy (carry, chip, pass)
Execute with support timing
U18 / Midget.why: Transition reads require first assessing numbers, then D positioning, then choosing entry.
U18 / Midget.tip: Transition read: numbers, D depth, entry choice.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the steps of building momentum after a negative play or mistake.
items: Acknowledge the mistake without dwelling on it
Make the next positive play with intensity
Communicate confidence to teammates
Build on the play with continued effort
U18 / Midget.why: Momentum recovery requires acknowledgment, immediate positive action, communication, and sustained effort.
U18 / Midget.tip: Momentum: reset, attack, talk, sustain.

U18 / Midget.sit: Order the steps of deciding whether to pinch as the O-zone D.
items: Assess puck position and rim direction
Check if your partner has back coverage
Evaluate whether you can win the puck
Commit to the pinch or hold the line
U18 / Midget.why: Pinch decisions require puck awareness, partner support coverage, win probability, then commitment.
U18 / Midget.tip: Pinch: puck, coverage, win %, commit.

U18 / Midget.sit: After a goal against, you blame your defenseman publicly instead of discussing it privately after the game. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have prevented the goal yourself.
Public blame erodes team culture — address mistakes privately and move forward together.
You should have kept it to yourself.
The defenseman made the mistake, so it's their fault.
U18 / Midget.why: Great teams address mistakes privately and move forward. Public blame creates division.
U18 / Midget.tip: Mistakes: address privately, move forward.

U18 / Midget.sit: After a controversial call against you, you retaliate with an obvious penalty. What's the mistake?
opts: You were justified.
You let emotion override judgment — responding to the ref's call gives them the final say.
The call was wrong.
You should have hit them harder.
U18 / Midget.why: Great players channel emotion into good play, not retaliation. Emotional play = penalties.
U18 / Midget.tip: Channel emotion into smart play.

U18 / Midget.sit: With your partner still in defensive zone coverage behind the net, you pinch aggressively on the wing. Rim-out breaks behind you. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have stayed home.
You pinched without confirming partner coverage — break goes behind you for odd-man rush.
Your partner should have helped.
The play was too fast.
U18 / Midget.why: Pinching requires confirmed back coverage. Without it, you create breakaways.
U18 / Midget.tip: Pinch: confirm back coverage first.

U18 / Midget.sit: In neutral zone with the puck, you try a showboat deke against pressure and lose the puck. Immediate odd-man against you. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have tried harder.
You took an unnecessary risk in a high-risk area — possession matters in neutral zone.
The defender was lucky.
You should have passed earlier.
U18 / Midget.why: Neutral zone turnovers create odd-man against you. Smart play > style points.
U18 / Midget.tip: Neutral zone: possession over style.

U18 / Midget.sit: Facing aggressive forecheck, you try to stickhandle through traffic instead of making a quick first pass. You're turned over deep. What's the mistake?
opts: You were too fancy.
You held puck too long under heavy forecheck — quick first pass beats pressure.
The forecheck was too good.
You should have dumped it.
U18 / Midget.why: Under forecheck, quick decision and pass beats fancy handling.
U18 / Midget.tip: Heavy forecheck: quick pass.

U18 / Midget.sit: On power play with a skater advantage, you pass the puck 5 times without taking a shot, and the PK kills it. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have shot immediately.
You overplayed — a skater advantage is for scoring, not just passing. Take high-percentage shots.
The PK was good.
Someone should have scored.
U18 / Midget.why: Power play success requires shot volume. Passing only kills time.
U18 / Midget.tip: Power play: take shots, create chances.

U18 / Midget.sit: Down 3-2 with 2 minutes left, instead of trusting your linemates to create chances, you try to deke through three defenders. Turnover ends the game. What's the mistake?
opts: You should have passed earlier.
You became selfish under pressure — trust your teammates and make smart plays.
Your linemates weren't in position.
You tried too hard.
U18 / Midget.why: Pressure situations require trust in teammates and smart play, not individual heroics.
U18 / Midget.tip: Late pressure: trust, execute, support.

U18 / Midget.sit: You score to tie the game 2-2 in the third period. The other team immediately calls timeout. What happens next?
opts: You celebrate aggressively.
You stay focused and aggressive — momentum is yours, don't let timeout reset it.
You defend and be conservative.
You prepare for overtime.
U18 / Midget.why: After scoring to tie, maintain aggressive play. Timeout is opponent's reset, not yours.
U18 / Midget.tip: Keep momentum: stay aggressive after scoring.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team gives up two goals in 30 seconds. As a leader, what happens next?
opts: Stay silent and let emotions settle.
Call teammates together, reset focus, and demand immediate response with intensity.
Request to sit out.
Wait for coach to address it.
U18 / Midget.why: Leaders respond immediately to adversity and call team to action. Silence enables panic.
U18 / Midget.tip: Adversity: leaders call reset.

U18 / Midget.sit: You complete a difficult transition pass that creates a 2-on-1 breakaway. What happens next?
opts: Play defensively to cover risk you took.
Stay up ice and support the play — you earned the aggressive position with a good read.
Back off and regroup.
Head to bench to rest.
U18 / Midget.why: Good high-risk plays earn the right to support the opportunity. Stay engaged.
U18 / Midget.tip: Good risk: stay involved and support.

U18 / Midget.sit: You gain possession in neutral zone and realize you have a 3-on-2 advantage. What next?
opts: Dump and change instead of managing the puck in transition.
Push with speed immediately — 3-on-2 goes downhill fast if you attack with pace.
Wait for support.
Make a safe pass.
U18 / Midget.why: 3-on-2 advantages diminish with time. Push with speed immediately.
U18 / Midget.tip: Numerical advantage: attack with speed.

U18 / Midget.sit: You complete a clean breakout and your forwards have a 2-on-1 rush. What next?
opts: Return to defense immediately.
Push up ice in support of the rush — if play comes back, you're in position.
Stay deep in zone.
Change immediately.
U18 / Midget.why: After a clean breakout supporting the rush keeps pressure and prevents backhand entries.
U18 / Midget.tip: Breakout success: support the rush.

U18 / Midget.q: You just received the puck at the offensive blue line with pressure arriving. Which lane gets the puck to a teammate cleanly?
U18 / Midget.tip: Read the defenders' sticks. The open lane is the one that doesn't cross a stick AND leads to a dangerous spot.

lanes.msg: ✓ Clean lane to LW — defender's stick is on the other side.
Blocked — the second defender is in that lane.

U18 / Midget.q: You're the D on a 1-on-1. The attacker is carrying wide. Drag to where your gap should close.
U18 / Midget.tip: Angle them to the boards — your stick in the passing lane, body between puck and center ice. Don't get beat wide.

targets.feedback: ✓ Gap closed on the boards, stick in the lane. Textbook.
Stayed flat-footed. The attacker's gone wide — you lost the gap.

U18 / Midget.q: Rebuild the fastest breakout on this regroup. Tap players in order 1 → 4.
U18 / Midget.tip: D retrieves (1) → D-to-D reverse (2) → strong-side winger (3) → stretch to the F3 high (4).

U18 / Midget.sit: You're on the power play running a 1-3-1. The puck is at the half-wall on your strong side.
U18 / Midget.q: Which options are GOOD looks for the half-wall player?
opts: One-touch pass to the bumper in the high slot
Pass cross-ice to the weak-side flank for a one-timer
Hard backhand pass to the point
Force a low-percentage shot through three sticks
U18 / Midget.tip: The 1-3-1 lives off three options — bumper, weak-side, and point. Forcing a contested shot kills a power-play possession.
U18 / Midget.why: Half-wall has three release valves built into the formation. Forcing a shot through traffic gives the puck back. The first three are all designed reads.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're killing a penalty in a passive box. The other team has the puck at the point.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are correct PK reads?
opts: Allow the point shot if the lane is clean — block it with your body if you can
Both forwards charge the points hard and chase the puck
Keep sticks in passing lanes to take away cross-ice seams
Box collapses when the puck goes below the goal line
U18 / Midget.tip: A passive box concedes the perimeter shot and protects the middle.
U18 / Midget.why: Charging both points opens the slot and leaves the box broken. Passive box logic: take away the middle, allow low-percentage looks, collapse below the dots.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team just won a defensive-zone faceoff cleanly back to your D-partner.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are good first-pass options for the D?
opts: Quick D-to-D pass to swing the puck away from pressure
Tape-to-tape outlet to the strong-side winger breaking up the wall
Wrist a soft chip into the neutral zone with no skater going to it
Stretch pass to a forward who has timed an attack with speed through center
U18 / Midget.tip: Three reads — D-to-D, wing on the wall, stretch with timing. Soft chips with no support give it back.
U18 / Midget.why: Chipping it out without a skater chasing is a turnover. Each of the other three is a designed first-pass option after a clean win.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're on a 2-on-1 rush. You have the puck on your forehand; the defender is square and lined up on you.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are GOOD reads for the puck-carrier?
opts: Drive wide and shoot for a rebound your linemate can chase
Attempt a saucer pass through the defender's stick to your linemate's blade
Slide the puck early before the defender commits, while there's still a clean lane
Take a hard slap shot from the blue line through the defender's body
U18 / Midget.tip: On a 2-on-1, force the defender to pick — pass too late OR shoot too wide.
U18 / Midget.why: Saucers through a square defender's stick are low-percentage. A blue-line slap shot wastes the rush. Driving wide for a rebound or sliding it early before the defender reads it both work.

U18 / Midget.sit: It's 4-on-4 OT. Your team just gained possession in your defensive zone after a turnover.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are GOOD 4-on-4 OT reads?
opts: Skate the puck wide to drag a defender out of position
Force a low-percentage stretch pass into traffic
Hold the puck behind your net to regroup if no good option is there
Use the open ice — controlled rushes beat dump-and-chase in OT
U18 / Midget.tip: 4-on-4 OT rewards possession and patience — open ice, not dump-and-chase.
U18 / Midget.why: Forcing stretch passes into traffic gives back possession in sudden death. Drag, regroup, and controlled rushes use the extra space.

U18 / Midget.sit: You're a forward defending against a 6-on-5 (other team has pulled their goalie).
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are correct reads when defending the empty net push?
opts: Block shots aggressively — getting in lanes is more important than fancy positioning
Stay in the high slot so a clear-attempt can get past the extra attacker
Lock the half-wall — don't let the puck come up the boards on your side
Cheat for the empty-net pass — take risks since the game's almost over
U18 / Midget.tip: Defending 6-on-5: shot-blocks + clear lane awareness + wall lockdown. Don't gamble.
U18 / Midget.why: Cheating for the empty-net is how teams give up the tying goal. Block shots, stay high for the breakout, and lock the wall.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team has been on a 5-on-3 power play for 30 seconds without a shot. The puck is at the point.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are GOOD adjustments?
opts: Move the puck side-to-side to shift the kill before shooting
Force a one-timer through three sticks — at least it's a shot
Get a player to the front of the net for screens and tips
Use a shot-pass — fake the shot, slide it across to a one-timer
U18 / Midget.tip: 5-on-3 fails when shots are forced through traffic. Move the kill, screen, shot-pass.
U18 / Midget.why: Forcing a shot through three sticks is the easy block + counterattack. The other three move the kill out of position.

U18 / Midget.sit: Your team is up by one with 90 seconds left in regulation. You're a defenseman in your own zone.
U18 / Midget.q: Which of these are GOOD reads with a 1-goal lead late?
opts: Keep all clears on the right side of the line — no icings, no offensive zone risks
Pinch on the wall to extend your offensive shift
Get pucks deep — don't try to skate it through the neutral zone
Take the body when you can — finish checks, eat clock
U18 / Midget.tip: Up 1 late: clear it, dump it, finish checks. Pinching = extra-attacker chance against.
U18 / Midget.why: Pinching on the wall with a 1-goal lead is how leads disappear. Smart clears, dumps, and physical play ice the game.
